Panels (a,b) use unaltered CIFAR-10 images with ŌÉ= 0.5 noise. Panels (c,d) and (e,f) use CIFAR-10 images at 16 √ó 16 scale with ŌÉ= 0.12 and ŌÉ= 0.25 respectively. Panels (g,h) use CIFAR-10 images at 8 √ó 8 scale with ŌÉ= 0.12.

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper establishes fundamental high-dimensional limitations (‚Äúcurse of dimensionality‚ÄĚ) of randomized smoothing for certifiable robustness against ‚Ąďp-bounded adversaries when p > 2. Specifically: (1) For a broad class of i.i.d. symmetric, zero-mean, continuous-support noise distributions with variance ŌÉ2, the largest certifiable ‚Ąďp radius r*p decays as O(1/d^{1/2‚ąí1/p}); Theorem 1 gives r*p upper bounds depending on p1(x), p2(x). (2) For generalized Gaussian smoothing (includes Gaussian, Laplace, uniform), a tighter bound r*p ‚Č§ 2ŌÉ d^{‚ąí(1/2‚ąí1/p)}(‚ąölog(1/(1‚ąíp1)) + ‚ąölog(1/p2)) is proved (Theorem 2), showing Gaussian smoothing is optimal up to a constant factor for p ‚Č• 2. (3) For uniform ‚Ąď‚ąě smoothing, r*p < 2b d^{‚ąí(1‚ąí1/p)} = 2‚ąö3 ŌÉ d^{‚ąí(1‚ąí1/p)} (Theorem 4). (4) For uniform ‚Ąď1 smoothing, r*p < 2b/d, independent of p (Theorem 5). (5) They show Cohen et al.‚Äôs Gaussian certificate for ‚Ąď2 extends to ‚Ąďp (p ‚Č• 2) via norm equivalence with the same d-scaling as their upper bounds, essentially matching up to constants. Empirical results on CIFAR-10 validate that changing the noise shape (for fixed ŌÉ) yields at best constant-factor gains and p1(x) is largely determined by ŌÉ, not the noise family.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: - Theoretical framework: Define smoothed classifier h¬Į(x) via additive noise őĒ ‚ąľ P and study the largest certifiable ‚Ąďp radius r*p that guarantees label invariance. Construct adversarial classifiers and point pairs (x, x‚Ä≤) to upper bound r*p by ‚ą•x‚ąíx‚Ä≤‚ą•p. - i.i.d. smoothing (Theorem 1): Let S = ‚ąĎ Zi, apply Chebyshev bounds to tail probabilities P(S ‚Č• s) to bound thresholds s1, s2 linked to p1, p2, then propagate through the slab-separation argument to upper bound r*p, revealing O(1/d^{1/2‚ąí1/p}) decay. - Generalized Gaussian smoothing (Theorem 2): Sharpen analysis using Chernoff bounds and bounding E[e^{tZ}] via series expansion and Gamma function inequalities to obtain logarithmic dependence on 1/(1‚ąíp1) and 1/p2 with the same d-scaling. - Uniform ‚Ąď‚ąě smoothing (Theorem 4): Geometric overlap of shifted hypercubes; compare volumes V1‚ąíV2 and V2‚ąíV1 to bound the change in class probabilities after a shift ŌĶ and derive r*p < 2b d^{‚ąí(1‚ąí1/p)}. - Uniform ‚Ąď1 smoothing (Theorem 5): Use ‚Ąď1-ball intersection geometry (Lemma 6) and volume formulas 2^d R^d/d! to bound overlap under a shift, yielding r*p < 2b/d. - Compare to Gaussian certificate (Cohen et al., 2019) extended to ‚Ąďp via norm equivalence: rp = ŌÉ d^{‚ąí(1/2‚ąí1/p)}(ő¶^{-1}(p1) ‚ąí ő¶^{-1}(p2))/2, matching d-dependence. - Empirical evaluation: Implement randomized smoothing with generalized Gaussian noise; estimate p1, p2 via Monte Carlo; compare achieved Gaussian certificates to the new upper bounds.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: - Datasets: CIFAR-10 at resolutions 32√ó32, 16√ó16, and 8√ó8; ImageNet statistics (median certificates) referenced from prior work for comparison. - Models: ResNet-110 trained for 90 epochs per configuration; identical architecture across resolutions (downscale image, add noise, then upsample to 32√ó32 before classification). - Noise distributions: Generalized Gaussian with parameter q (including Gaussian q=2, Laplace q=1, and approximating uniform as q‚Üí‚ąě); experiments often set q=p to match ‚Ąďp; multiple ŌÉ tested (0.12, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00) depending on resolution. - Certification protocol: Following Cohen et al. (2019) with 99.9% confidence; candidate class via small sample; then 100,000 samples to estimate p1(x); set p2(x)=1‚ąíp1(x). Compare actual Gaussian certificates to theoretical upper bounds derived here. - Observations: For fixed ŌÉ, p1(x) is largely invariant to the choice of i.i.d. noise distribution shape; at low ŌÉ and higher resolution, generalized Gaussian bounds can be tighter than i.i.d. bounds; at higher ŌÉ the i.i.d. bound is tighter. Scaling experiments show that in realistic high-dimensional tasks (e.g., ImageNet vs CIFAR-10), increased dimensionality does not sufficiently raise p1(x) to offset the d-scaling penalty.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: - Theoretical results focus primarily on i.i.d., symmetric, zero-mean, continuous-support smoothing distributions (with generalized Gaussian and specific uniform cases); findings may not extend to non-i.i.d. or input-dependent noise. - Bounds are most meaningful for p ‚Č• 2; some become weak for p < 2 (and for p < 1 in ‚Ąď‚ąě uniform case). - Assumptions like e^{‚ąíd/4} < p2 ‚Č§ p1 < 1‚ąíe^{‚ąíd/4} are required in Theorem 2. - Upper bounds are derived via Chebyshev/Chernoff and may have conservative constants; tightness is shown only up to constant factors. - Empirical conclusions rely on specific architectures (ResNet-110) and datasets; no exploration of non-additive or data-dependent smoothing. - No new certifiers are proposed; analysis focuses on limits rather than constructive improvements.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: - Develop non-i.i.d., input-dependent, or data-adaptive smoothing schemes to escape the O(1/d^{1/2‚ąí1/p}) scaling for p > 2. - Explore correlated or structured noise aligned with data manifolds or model gradients to improve certifiable radii. - Design certification methods that exploit more information than just (p1, p2), potentially leveraging full class probability vectors or local decision geometry. - Investigate anisotropic or learned covariance kernels (e.g., non-isotropic Gaussian, low-rank perturbations) and theoretical limits thereof. - Extend analysis to other metrics (e.g., Wasserstein, perceptual distances) or non-additive perturbation models (patches, sparse attacks) in high dimensions. - Tighter inequalities and matching lower/upper bounds with optimal constants across broader distribution classes. - Combine adversarial training with advanced smoothing for p > 2 and study sample efficiency and estimator variance effects on certificates.

research_study_list.title: Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes
research_study_list.abstract:
Randomized smoothing is the current state-of-the-art defense with provable
robustness against $\ell_2$ adversarial attacks. Many works have devised new
randomized smoothing schemes for other metrics, such as $\ell_1$ or
$\ell_\infty$; however, substantial effort was needed to derive such new
guarantees. This begs the question: can we find a general theory for randomized
smoothing?
  We propose a novel framework for devising and analyzing randomized smoothing
schemes, and validate its effectiveness in practice. Our theoretical
contributions are: (1) we show that for an appropriate notion of "optimal", the
optimal smoothing distributions for any "nice" norms have level sets given by
the norm's *Wulff Crystal*; (2) we propose two novel and complementary methods
for deriving provably robust radii for any smoothing distribution; and, (3) we
show fundamental limits to current randomized smoothing techniques via the
theory of *Banach space cotypes*. By combining (1) and (2), we significantly
improve the state-of-the-art certified accuracy in $\ell_1$ on standard
datasets. Meanwhile, we show using (3) that with only label statistics under
random input perturbations, randomized smoothing cannot achieve nontrivial
certified accuracy against perturbations of $\ell_p$-norm $\Omega(\min(1,
d^{\frac{1}{p} - \frac{1}{2}}))$, when the input dimension $d$ is large. We
provide code in github.com/tonyduan/rs4a.
research_study_list.full_text: Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Greg Yang* 1 Tony Duan* 1 2 J. Edward Hu 1 2 Hadi Salman 1 Ilya Razenshteyn 1 Jerry Li 1 Abstract Randomized smoothing is the current state-of-the- art defense with provable robustness against ‚Ąď2 adversarial attacks. Many works have devised new randomized smoothing schemes for other metrics, such as ‚Ąď1 or ‚Ąď‚ąě; however, substantial effort was needed to derive such new guarantees. This begs the question: can we Ô¨Ānd a general theory for randomized smoothing? We propose a novel framework for devising and analyzing randomized smoothing schemes, and validate its effectiveness in practice. Our theoreti- cal contributions are: (1) we show that for an ap- propriate notion of ‚Äúoptimal‚ÄĚ, the optimal smooth- ing distributions for any ‚Äúnice‚ÄĚ norms have level sets given by the norm‚ÄôsWulff Crystal; (2) we pro- pose two novel and complementary methods for deriving provably robust radii for any smoothing distribution; and, (3) we show fundamental lim- its to current randomized smoothing techniques via the theory of Banach space cotypes. By com- bining (1) and (2), we signiÔ¨Ācantly improve the state-of-the-art certiÔ¨Āed accuracy in ‚Ąď1 on stan- dard datasets. Meanwhile, we show using (3) that with only label statistics under random in- put perturbations, randomized smoothing cannot achieve nontrivial certiÔ¨Āed accuracy against per- turbations of ‚Ąďp-norm ‚Ą¶(min(1,d 1 p‚ąí1 2 )), when the input dimension dis large. We provide code in github.com/tonyduan/rs4a. 1. Introduction Deep learning models are vulnerable to adversarial exam- ples ‚Äď small imperceptible perturbations to their inputs that lead to misclassiÔ¨Ācation (Goodfellow et al., 2015; Szegedy et al., 2014). To solve this problem, recent works proposed heuristic defenses that are robust to speciÔ¨Āc classes of per- *Equal contribution 1Microsoft Research AI 2Work done as part of the Microsoft AI Residency Program. Corre- spondence to: Greg Yang <gregyang@microsoft.com>, Tony Duan <tony.duan@microsoft.com>, Jerry Li <jerrl@microsoft.com>. Proceedings of the 37 th International Conference on Machine Learning, Vienna, Austria, PMLR 119, 2020. Copyright 2020 by the author(s). turbations, but many would later be broken by stronger at- tacking algorithms (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Athalye et al., 2018; Uesato et al., 2018). This led the community to both strengthen empirical defenses (Kurakin et al., 2016; Madry et al., 2017) as well as build certiÔ¨Āed defenses that provide robustness guarantees, i.e., models whose predictions are constant within a neighborhood of their inputs (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018a). In particular, ran- domized smoothing is a recent method that has achieved state-of-the-art provable robustness (Lecuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Cohen et al., 2019). In short, given an input, it outputs the class most likely to be returned by a base classiÔ¨Āer, typically a neural network, under random noise perturbation of the input. This mechanism confers stabil- ity of the output against ‚Ąďp perturbations, even if the base classiÔ¨Āer itself is highly non-Lipschitz. Canonically, this noise has been Gaussian, and the adversarial perturbation it protects against has been ‚Ąď2 (Cohen et al., 2019; Salman et al., 2019a; Zhai et al., 2020), but some have explored other kinds of noises and adversaries as well (Lecuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Dvijotham et al., 2019). In this paper, we seek to comprehensively understand the interaction between the choice of smoothing distribution and the perturbation norm.1 1. We propose two new methods to compute robust cer- tiÔ¨Ācates for additive randomized smoothing against different norms. 2. We show that, for ‚Ąď1,‚Ąď2,‚Ąď‚ąěadversaries, the optimal smoothing distributions have level sets that are their re- spective Wulff Crystals‚ÄĒ a kind of equilibrated crystal structure studied in physics since 1901 (Wulff). 3. Using the above advances, we obtain state-of-the-art‚Ąď1 certiÔ¨Āed accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. With stability training (Li et al., 2019), semi-supervised learning (Carmon et al., 2019), and pre-training in the fashion of Hendrycks et al. (2019), we further improve CIFAR-10 certiÔ¨Āed accuracies, with >30% advantage over prior SOTA for‚Ąď1 radius ‚Č•1.5. See Table 1. 4. Finally, we leverage the classical theory of Banach space cotypes (Wojtaszczyk, 1996) to show that current techniques for randomized smoothing cannot certify nontrivial accuracy at more than ‚Ą¶(min(1,d 1 p‚ąí1 2 )) ‚Ąďp- radius, if all one uses are the probabilities of labels when classifying randomly perturbed input. 1V2 update: we added results using stability training, semi- supervised learning, and ImageNet pre-training. See Table 1. arXiv:2002.08118v5  [cs.LG]  23 Jul 2020Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes ImageNet ‚Ąď1 Radius 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 Laplace, Teng et al. (2019) (%) 48 40 31 26 22 19 17 14 Uniform, Ours (%) 55 49 46 42 37 33 28 25 + Stability Training 60 55 51 48 45 43 41 39 CIFAR-10 ‚Ąď1 Radius 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 Laplace, Teng et al. (2019) (%) 61 39 24 16 11 7 4 3 Uniform, Ours (%) 70 59 51 43 33 27 22 18 + Stability Training 70 60 53 47 43 39 35 28 + Stability Training, Semi-supervision 74 63 54 48 43 38 34 31 + Stability Training, Pre-training 74 62 55 48 43 40 37 33 Table 1.CertiÔ¨Āed top-1 accuracies of our ‚Ąď1-robust classiÔ¨Āers, vs previous state-of-the-art, at various radii, for ImageNet and CIFAR-10.3 2. Related Works Defences against adversarial examples are mainly divided into empirical defenses and certiÔ¨Āed defenses. Empirical defenses are heuristics designed to make learned models empirically robust. An example of these are adver- sarial training based defenses (Kurakin et al., 2016; Madry et al., 2017) which optimize the parameters of a model by minimizing the worst-case loss over a neighborhood around the input to these models (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Laidlaw & Feizi, 2019; Wong et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020). Such defenses may seem powerful, but have no guarantees that they are not ‚Äúbreakable‚ÄĚ. In fact, the majority of the empiri- cal defenses proposed in the literature were later ‚Äúbroken‚ÄĚ by stronger attacks (Carlini & Wagner, 2017; Athalye et al., 2018; Uesato et al., 2018; Athalye & Carlini, 2018). CertiÔ¨Āed defenses guarantee that for any input x, the clas- siÔ¨Āer‚Äôs output is constant within a small neighborhood ofx. Such defenses are typically based on certiÔ¨Ācation methods that are either exact or conservative. Exact methods include those based on SatisÔ¨Āability Modulo Theories solvers (Katz et al., 2017; Ehlers, 2017) or mixed integer linear program- ming (Tjeng et al., 2019; Lomuscio & Maganti, 2017; Fis- chetti & Jo, 2017), which, although guaranteed to Ô¨Ānd adver- sarial examples if they exist, are unfortunately computation- ally inefÔ¨Ācient. On the other hand, conservative methods are more computationally efÔ¨Ācient, but might mistakenly Ô¨āag a ‚Äúsafe‚ÄĚ data point as vulnerable to adversarial examples (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Wang et al., 2018a;b; Raghunathan et al., 2018a;b; Wong et al., 2018; Dvijotham et al., 2018b;a; Croce et al., 2018; Salman et al., 2019b; Gehr et al., 2018; Mirman et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018; Weng et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). However, none of these defenses scale to practical networks. Recently, a new method called randomized smoothing has been proposed as a probabilistically certiÔ¨Āed defense, whose architecture- independence makes it scalable. 3Unless stated otherwise, these models were trained with noise augmentation. In our replication of Teng et al. (2019), our noise augmentation results matched their adversarial training results. Randomized smoothing Randomized smoothing was Ô¨Ārst proposed as a heuristic defense without any guaran- tees (Liu et al., 2018; Cao & Gong, 2017). Later on, Lecuyer et al. (2018) proved a robustness guarantee for smoothed classiÔ¨Āers from a differential privacy perspective. Subse- quently, Li et al. (2019) gave a stronger robustness guarantee utilizing tools from information theory. Recently, Cohen et al. (2019) provided a tight ‚Ąď2 robustness guarantee for randomized smoothing, applied by Salman et al. (2020) to provably defend pre-trained models for the Ô¨Ārst time. Furthermore, a series of papers came out recently that devel- oped robustness guarantees against other adversaries such as ‚Ąď1-bounded (Teng et al., 2019), ‚Ąď‚ąě-bounded (Zhang* et al., 2020), ‚Ąď0-bounded (Levine & Feizi, 2019a; Lee et al., 2019), and Wasserstein attacks (Levine & Feizi, 2019b). In Section 4.3, we give a more in-depth comparison on how our techniques compare to their results. Wulff Crystal We are the Ô¨Ārst to relate to adversarial ro- bustness the theory of Wulff Crystals. Just as the round soap bubble minimizes surface tension for a given volume, the Wulff Crystal minimizes certain similar surface energy that arises when the crystal interfaces with another material. The Russian physicist George Wulff Ô¨Ārst proposed this shape via physical arguments in 1901 (Wulff, 1901), but its energy minimization property was not proven in full generality un- til relatively recently, building on a century worth of work (Gibbs, 1875; Wulff, 1901; Hilton, 1903; Liebmann, 1914; von Laue; Dinghas, 1944; Burton et al., 1951; Herring; Con- stable, 1968; Taylor, 1975; 1978; Fonseca & M¬®uller, 1991; Brothers & Morgan, 1994; Cerf, 2006). No-go theorems for randomized smoothing Prior to the initial submission of this manuscript, the only other no- go theorem for randomized smoothing in the context of adversarial robustness is Zheng et al. (2020). However, they are only concerned with a non-standard notion of certiÔ¨Āed robustness that does not imply anything for the original problem. Moreover, they show that, under this different notion of robustness, if they are robust for ‚Ąď‚ąě, then the ‚Ąď2 norm of the noise must be large on average. While thisRandomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes provides indirect evidence for the hardness of certifying ‚Ąď‚ąě, it does not actually address the question. Our result, on the other hand, directly rules out a large suite of current techniques for deriving robust certiÔ¨Ācates for all ‚Ąďp norms for p> 2, for the standard notion of certiÔ¨Āed robustness. After the initial submission of this manuscript, we became aware of two concurrent works (Blum et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2020) that claim impossibility results for random- ized smoothing. Blum et al. (2020) demonstrate that, under some mild conditions, any smoothing distribution for ‚Ąďp with p >2 must have large component-wise magnitude. This gives indirect evidence for the hardness of the problem, but does not directly show a limit for the utility of random- ized smoothness for the robust classiÔ¨Ācation problem, which we do in this work. Kumar et al. (2020) demonstrate that certain classes of smoothing distributions cannot certify ‚Ąď‚ąě without losing dimension-dependent factors. Our result is more general, as it rules out any class of smoothing distri- butions, and in fact, any smoothing scheme that allows the distribution to vary arbitrarily with the input point. 3. Randomized Smoothing Consider a classiÔ¨Āer f from Rd to classes Yand a distribu- tion qon Rd. Randomized smoothing with qis a method that constructs a new, smoothed classiÔ¨Āer gfrom the base classiÔ¨Āer f. The smoothed classiÔ¨Āer g assigns to a query point xthe class which is most likely to be returned by the base classiÔ¨Āer f when x is perturbed by a random noise sampled from q, i.e., g(x) def = argmax c‚ąąY q(Uc ‚ąíx) (1) where Uc is the decision region {x‚Ä≤ ‚ąąRd : f(x‚Ä≤) = c}, Uc‚ąíxdenotes the translation of Uc by ‚ąíx, and q(U) is the measure of U under q, i.e. q(U) = Pőī‚ąľq(őī‚ąąU). Robustness guarantee for smoothed classiÔ¨Āers For p‚ąą [0,1],v ‚ąąRd,deÔ¨Āne the growth function Gq(p,v) def = sup U‚äÜRd:q(U)=p q(U ‚ąív), (2) One can think of U has the decision region of some base classiÔ¨Āer. Thus Gq(p,v) gives the maximal growth of mea- sure of a set (i.e. decision region) when qis shifted by the vector v, if we only know the initial measure pof the set. Consider an adversary that can perturb an input additively by any vector vinside an allowed set B. In the case when Bis the ‚Ąď2 ball and qis the Gaussian measure, Cohen et al. (2019) gave a simple expression for Gq involving the Gaussian CDF, derived via the Neyman-Pearson lemma, which is later rederived by Salman et al. (2019a) as a nonlinear Lipschitz property. Likewise, the expression for Laplace distributions was derived by Teng et al. (2019). (See Theorem F.10 and Theorem F.11 for their expressions.) Suppose when the base classiÔ¨Āer f classiÔ¨Āes x+ őī, őī‚ąľq, the class c‚ąąY is returned with probabilityŌĀ= Pőī‚ąľq(f(x+ őī) = c) > 1/2. Then the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer g will not change its prediction under the adversary‚Äôs perturbations if4 sup v‚ąąB Gq(1 ‚ąíŌĀ,v) <1/2. (3) 4. Methods for Deriving Robust Radii Let qbe a distribution with a density function, and we shall write q(x),x ‚ąąRd, for the value of the density function on x. Then, given a shift vector v ‚ąąRd and a ratio őļ> 0, deÔ¨Āne the Neyman-Pearson set NPőļ def = {x‚ąąRd : őļq(x‚ąív) ‚Č•q(x)}. (4) Then the Neyman-Pearson lemma tells us that (Neyman & Pearson, 1933; Cohen et al., 2019) Gq(q(NPőļ),v) = q(NPőļ ‚ąív). (NP) While this gives way to a simple expression for the growth function when qis Gaussian (Cohen et al., 2019), it is difÔ¨Ā- cult for more general distributions as the geometry of NPőļ becomes hard to grasp. To overcome this difÔ¨Āculty, we propose the level set method that decomposes this geome- try so as to compute the growth function exactly, and the differential method that upper bounds the growth function derivative, loosely speaking. 4.1. The Level Set Method For each t> 0, let Ut be the superlevel set Ut def = {x‚ąąRd : q(x) ‚Č•t}. Then its boundary ‚ąāUt is the level set with q(x) = tunder regularity assumptions. In general, this yields an upper bound of the robust radius sup { r: sup ‚ą•v‚ą•p‚Č§r Gq(1 ‚ąíŌĀ,v) <1/2 } ‚Č§sup {r: Gq(1 ‚ąíŌĀ,ru) <1/2} for any particular uwith ‚ą•u‚ą•p = 1. The Differential Method To derive certiÔ¨Ācation (robust radius lower bounds ) for more general distributions, we propose adifferential method, which can be thought of as a vast generalization of the proof in Salman et al. (2019a) of the Gaussian robust radius. The idea is to compute the largest possible inÔ¨Ānitesimal increase in q-measure due to an inÔ¨Ānitesimal adversarial perturba- tion. More precisely, given a norm ‚ą•¬∑‚ą•, and a smoothing measure q, we deÔ¨Āne ő¶(p) def = sup ‚ą•v‚ą•=1 sup U‚äÜRd:q(U)=p lim r‚Üė0 q(U ‚ąírv) ‚ąíp r . (5) Intuitively, one can then think of 1/ő¶(p) as the smallest possible perturbation in ‚ą•¬∑‚ą• needed to effect a unit of inÔ¨Ānitesimal increase in p. Therefore, Theorem 4.1 (Theorem F.6). The robust radius in ‚ą•¬∑‚ą• is at least R def = ‚ąę 1/2 1‚ąíŌĀ 1 ő¶(p) dp, where ŌĀis the probability that the base classiÔ¨Āer predicts the right label under random perturbation by q. Comparison of the Two Methods and Prior Works We summarize the distributions our methods cover in Fig. 1 and the bounds we derive in Table A.1. We highlight a few broadly applicable robustness guarantees: Example 4.3 (Theorem I.1). Let ŌÜ: R ‚ÜíR be convex and even, and let CDF‚ąí1 ŌÜ be the inverse CDF of the 1D random variable with density‚ąĚexp(‚ąíŌÜ(x)). If q(x) ‚ąĚ‚ąŹ ie‚ąíŌÜ(xi), and ŌĀis the probability of the correct class, then the robust radius in ‚Ąď1 is R= CDF‚ąí1 ŌÜ (ŌĀ) and this radius is tight. This in particular recovers the Gaus- sian bound of Cohen et al. (2019), Laplace bound of Teng et al. (2019), and Uniform bound of Lee et al. (2019) in the setting of ‚Ąď1 adversary. Example 4.4 (Appendices I.2.1 and I.3.1) . Facing an ‚Ąď1 adversary, cubical distributions, like that in Example 4.2, typically enjoy, via the differential method,‚Ąď1 robust radii of the form R= c(ŌĀ‚ąí1/2) for some constant cdepending on the distribution. In general, the level set method always gives certiÔ¨Ācate as tight as Neyman-Pearson, while the differential method is tight only for inÔ¨Ānitesimal perturbations, but can be shown to be tight for certain families, like in Example 4.3 above. On the other hand, the latter will often give efÔ¨Āciently evalu- able symbolic expressions and apply to more general distri- butions, while the former in general will only yield a table of robust radii, and only for distributions whose level sets are sufÔ¨Āciently symmetric (such as a sphere or cube). For distributions that are covered by both methods, we com- pare the bounds obtained and note that the differential and level set methods yield almost identical robustness certiÔ¨Ā- cates in high dimensions (e.g. number of pixels in CIFAR- 10 or ImageNet images). See Appendix B.1. Many earlier works used differential privacy orf-divergence methods to compute robust radii of smoothed modelsRandomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes (Lecuyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Dvijotham et al., 2019). In particular, Dvijotham et al. (2019) proposed a general f-divergence framework that subsumed all such works. Our robust radii are computed only from ŌĀ; Dvi- jotham et al. called this the ‚Äúinformation-limited‚ÄĚ setting, and we shall compare with their robustness guarantees of this type. While their algorithm in a certain limit becomes as good as Neyman-Pearson, in practice outside the Gaussian distribution, their robust radii are too loose. This is evi- dent by comparing our baseline Laplace results in Table 1 with theirs, which are trained the same way. Additionally, our differential method often yields symbolic expressions for robust radii, making the certiÔ¨Ācation algorithm easy to implement, verify, and run. Moreover, we derive robust- ness guarantees for many more (distributions, adversary) pairs (Fig. 1 and Table A.1). See Appendix B.2 for a more detailed comparison. 5. No-Go Results for Randomized Smoothing Recall that given a smoothing distributionq, a point x‚ąąRd, and a binary base classiÔ¨Āer U ‚äÜRd (identiÔ¨Āed wth its deci- sion region), the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer outputs sgn(ŌĀ‚ąí1/2) where ŌĀ= q(U ‚ąíx) is the ‚ÄúconÔ¨Ādence‚ÄĚ of this prediction (Eq. (1)). Randomized smoothing (via Neyman-Pearson) tells us that, if ŌĀis large enough, then, no matter what U is, a small perturbation of xcannot decrease ŌĀtoo much to change sgn(ŌĀ‚ąí1/2) (Eq. (3)). If all we care about is robustness, then the optimal strategy would set qto be an arbitrarily wide distribution (say, e.g. a wide Gaussian), and the resulting smoothed classiÔ¨Āer is roughly constant. Of course, such a smoothed classiÔ¨Āer can never achieve good clean accuracy, so it is not useful. Thus there is an inherent tension between 1) having to have large enough noise variance to be robust and 2) having to have small enough noise variance to avoid trivializing the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer. In this section, we seek to formalize this tradeoff. As we‚Äôll show, even if we only assume a very weak condition on the accuracy, we can show strong upper bounds on the best robust radius for each ‚Ąďp norm. In fact, our negative results below will hold for a more gen- eral class of smoothing schemes than those in our positive results in previous sections: In what follows, a smoothing scheme for Rd is any family of probability distributions Q= {qx}x‚ąąRd. In practice, including in our paper, almost all smoothing schemes are translational, that is, there is some base distribution q, and for every x, the smoothing dis- tribution at xis deÔ¨Āned by qx(U) = q(U ‚ąíx), for all base classiÔ¨Āers U ‚äÜRd. The above discussion then motivates the following DeÔ¨Ānition 7.1. Let ‚ą•¬∑‚ą• be a norm over Rd, and let Q= {qx}x‚ąąRd be a smoothing scheme for Rd. We say that Q satisÔ¨Āes (őĶ,s,‚Ąď )-useful smoothing with respect to ‚ą•¬∑‚ą• if: 1. ((őĶ,s)-Robustness) For all x,y with ‚ą•x‚ąíy‚ą•< őĶ, if U ‚äÜRd is any set ( read: base classiÔ¨Āer ) satisfying qx(U) ‚Č•1/2 + s, then qy(U) ‚Č•1/2. 2. (‚Ąď-Accuracy) For all x,y with ‚ą•x‚ąíy‚ą• ‚Č•1, there exists a set ( read: base classiÔ¨Āer ) U ‚äÜRd so that |qx(U) ‚ąíqy(U)|‚Č• ‚Ąď. label prob. ūĚúĆ robust radius ūĚúÄ 0.5+ūĚĎ†0.5 0 ūĚúÄ,ūĚĎ† -Robustness ūĚĎ• ūĚĎ¶ ūĚĎą ūĚĎěūĚĎ• ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ ūĚĎČūĚĎúūĚĎô ‚Č• ‚Ąď ‚Č• 1 ‚Ąď-Accuracy We pause to interpret this deÔ¨Ānition. Condition (1) indicates how large the certiÔ¨Āed radii can be for a classiÔ¨Āer at any given point x, if the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer assigns likelihood at least 1/2 + s to it; i.e. (1/2 + s,őĶ) is a point on the robust radii curve in the style of Fig. A.1. The goal of the smoothing scheme is to achieve the largest possible őĶ, for every Ô¨Āxed s. In particular, observe that for ‚Ąď2, Gaussian smoothing achieves dimension-independent őĶ, for every Ô¨Āxed choice of s(Theorem F.10). Condition (2) says that the resulting smoothing should not ‚Äúcollapse‚ÄĚ points: in particular, if x,y are far in norm, then there should be some smoothed classiÔ¨Āer that distinguishes them. We argue that this is a very mild assumption. For Con- dition (2) to be satisÔ¨Āed, the U which distinguishes these two points can be completely arbitrary. Thus, if it is violated for ‚Ąď= o(1), the two distributions are indistinguishable by any statistical test in high dimension, implying the impossi- bility of classifying between xand yafter smoothing. We seek to show that, for constant s and l, any (őĶ,s,‚Ąď )- useful smoothing scheme must have őĶ= o(1) for a number of norms, including ‚Ąď‚ąě. This would imply that any smooth- ing scheme that satisfying our weak notion of accuracy can only certify a vanishingly small radius, even when the conÔ¨Ā- dence of the classiÔ¨Āer is strictly bounded away from 1/2 by a constant.Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Randomized Smoothing as Metric Embedding A smoothing scheme can be thought of as a mapping from a normed space supported on Rd to the space of distribu- tions, e.g. each point x is mapped to the distribution qx. We will show that DeÔ¨Ānition 7.1 is roughly equivalent to a bi-Lipschitz condition on this mapping, where the target distributions are equipped with the total variation distance. Then the existence of a useful smoothing scheme is equiva- lent to whether (Rd,‚ą•¬∑‚ą•) can be embedded with low distor- tion into the total variation space of distributions. Classical mathematics has a deÔ¨Ānitive answer to this question in the form of a geometric invariant, called the cotype. DeÔ¨Ānition 7.2 (see e.g. Wojtaszczyk (1996)) . A normed space T = ( X,‚ą•¬∑‚ą• ) is said to have cotype p for 2 ‚Č§ p ‚Č§‚ąě if there exists C such that for all Ô¨Ānite sequences x1,...,x n ‚ąąX, we have E Ô£ģ Ô£į ÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻ n‚ąĎ j=1 ŌÉjxj ÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻÓĶĻ Ô£Ļ Ô£Ľ‚Č•C‚ąí1 Ô£ę Ô£≠ n‚ąĎ j=1 ‚ą•xi‚ą•p Ô£∂ Ô£ł 1/p , where the ŌÉj are independent Rademacher random variables. The smallest such Cis denoted Cp(T). When the underlying space of the normed space T is Rd, John‚Äôs theorem (John, 1948) implies that any norm has co- type 2 with C2(T) ‚Č§O(d1/2). Because C2 lower bounds the distortion of a metric embedding of T, by the aforemen- tioned connection with randomized smothing,C2 also limits the usefulness of any smoothing scheme of T: Theorem 7.3. Let T be any normed space over Rd. There exist universal constants c,K > 0 so that any (őĶ,s,‚Ąď )- useful smoothing scheme for T with s/‚Ąď<c must have őĶ‚Č§K 4 ‚ąö s/‚Ąď¬∑C2(T)‚ąí1. In particular, it is well-known that C2((Rd,‚ą•¬∑‚ą• p)) = ‚Ą¶(max(1,d1/2‚ąí1/p)), for all p ‚ąą[1,‚ąě]. Thus, as an im- mediately corollary, we get: Corollary 7.4. For the value of c in Theorem 7.3 and for p ‚ąą[1,‚ąě], any (őĶ,s,‚Ąď )-useful smoothing scheme for (Rd,‚ą•¬∑‚ą•p) with s/‚Ąď<c must have őĶ‚Č§O(min(1,d‚ąí1/2+1/p)). It is easy to see that, up to constants, the Gaussian smoothing scheme achieves equality, and thus is optimal (in terms of dimension dependence), for all p‚ąą[1,‚ąě]. Discussion After Cohen et al. (2019) showed the surpris- ing scalability of Gaussian randomized smoothing to high- dimensional ‚Ąď2-robust classiÔ¨Ācation problems, many antic- ipated that this can be extended to ‚Ąď‚ąěas well. One might also hope that, even though it seems like we cannot certify ‚Ąď2 radius that grows with input dimension, we could do so for ‚Ąď1. But Theorem 7.3 and Corollary 7.4 present a strong barrier to such hopes. In words: Without using more than the information of the probability ŌĀof correctly classifying an input under random noise, no smoothing techniques can certify nontrivial robust accuracy at ‚Ąď‚ąě radius ‚Ą¶(d‚ąí1/2), or at ‚Ąď2 or ‚Ąď1 radius ‚Ą¶(1). Indeed, the ‚Ąď1-radii we can obtain nontrivial certiÔ¨Āed ac- curacy at are on the same order between CIFAR10 and Imagenet (Fig. 2). However, there are some ways to bypass this barrier. For one, more information about the base classiÔ¨Āer can be col- lected to produce better robustness certiÔ¨Ācates. In fact, Dvi- jotham et al. (2019) proposed a ‚Äúfull-information‚ÄĚ algorithm that computes many moments of the base classiÔ¨Āer in a convex optimization procedure to improve certiÔ¨Āed radius, but it is 100 times slower than the ‚Äúinformation-limited‚ÄĚ algorithms we discuss here that use only ŌĀ. It would be interesting to see whether this technique can be scaled up, and whether other methods can leverage more information7. Another route is to directly look for better randomized smoothing schemes for multi-class classiÔ¨Ācation. We formu- lated our no-go result in the setting of binary classiÔ¨Ācation, and it is not clear whether a similarly strong barrier applies for multi-class classiÔ¨Ācation. However, current techniques for certiÔ¨Ācation only look at the two most likely classes, and separately reason about how much each one can change by perturbing the input. Our no-go result then straightforwardly applies to this case as well. 8. Conclusion In this work, we have showed how far we can push ran- domized smoothing with different smoothing distributions against different ‚Ąďp adversaries, by presenting two new tech- niques for deriving robustness guarantees, by elucidating the geometry connecting the noise and the norm, and by empirically achieving state-of-the-art in ‚Ąď1 provable defense. At the same time, we have showed the limit current tech- niques face against ‚Ąďp adversaries when p >2, especially ‚Ąď‚ąě. Our results point out ways to bypass this barrier, by either leveraging more information about the base classiÔ¨Āer or by taking advantage of the multi-class problem structure better. We wish to investigate both directions in the future. More broadly, randomized smoothing is a method for in- ducing stability in a mechanism while maintaining utility ‚ÄĒ precisely the bread and butter of differential privacy. We suspect our methods for deriving robustness guarantees here and for optimizing the noise distribution can be useful in that setting as well, where Laplace and Gaussian noise dom- inate the discussion. Whereas previous work Lecuyer et al. (2018) has applied differential privacy tools to randomized smoothing, we hope to go the other way around in the future. 7 Lee et al. (2019) also used the decision tree structure of their base classiÔ¨Āer to improve ‚Ąď0 certiÔ¨Ācation, but the ‚Ąď0-adversary does not fall within our framework.Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Acknowledgements We thank Huan Zhang for brainstorming of ideas and per- forming a few experiments that unfortunately did not work out. Analysis of Robust Radii Here we make a few observations about the robust radii of the distributions studied in this paper. Distributions that concentrate around the same level set have similar robust radii. This is evident, for example, in the top middle subplot of Fig. A.1, where the distribution ‚ąĚ‚ą•x‚ą•‚ąíj ‚ąěe‚ąí‚ą•x/őĽ‚ą•‚ąě with ‚Äúsmall‚ÄĚ j = 2972 has robust radii almost the same as those of ‚ąĚe‚ąí‚ą•x/őĽ‚ą•‚ąě (here őĽfor each distribution is the one that setsŌÉ= 1), and both distributions concentrate around the sphere of radius ‚ąö d. We can also see this in the top right (‚Ąď‚ąě-based power law), middle left (‚Ąď1- based exponential law), bottom left (‚Ąď2-based exponential law), and bottom middle (‚Ąď2-basd power law) subplots of Fig. A.1. This is also reÔ¨āected in the center subplot of Fig. A.1, which shows that Pareto distribution with large power gets the same robust radii as Laplace. The reason is that such a high-power Pareto distribution concentrates around an ‚Ąď1-ball in high dimension. We can understand this phenomenon intuitively via the level set method: Two distributions concentrating around the same level set will have Eq. ( ‚ą®) and Eq. ( ‚ąß) evaluate to similar quantities. Among distributions concentrated around some level set, the shape of the level set is the biggest determinant of performance. This is evident in the top left, middle right, and bottom right subplots of Fig. A.1. Distributions that don‚Äôt concentrate on a level set do worse than those that do. This is evident, for example, in the top middle subplot of Fig. A.1, where the distribution ‚ąĚ‚ą•x‚ą•‚ąíj ‚ąěe‚ąí‚ą•x/őĽ‚ą•‚ąě with ‚Äúlarge‚ÄĚj = 3070 has robust radii much smaller than those of ‚ąĚe‚ąí‚ą•x/őĽ‚ą•‚ąě. Same thing can be observed in the top right, center, middle right, bottom left, bottom middle subplots of Fig. A.1. Introducing a singularity at the origin only reduces ro- bust radii. The top middle and bottom left subplots of Fig. A.1 illustrate this point. Thus, we see no evidence for the ‚Äúsoap-bubble hypothesis‚ÄĚ put forth by Zhang* et al. (2020); see also Fig. C.8. Introducing a fatter tail yields larger robust radii for large ňÜŌĀlower, as long as the level set concentration is not affected. The middle left and bottom left subplots of Fig. A.1 demonstrate this behavior. The robust radii for- mulas for exp(‚ąí‚ą•x‚ą•‚ąě) (Theorem I.6) and for the uniform distribution (Theorem I.8) also reÔ¨āect this, as the former has robust radius ‚Üí‚ąě as ŌĀ‚Üí1, but the latter has a Ô¨Ānite maximal robust radius. B.2. In-Depth Comparison with Dvijotham et al. (2019) The information-limited certiÔ¨Ācation algorithm in Dvi- jotham et al. (2019) relaxes the optimization problem sup v‚ąąB Gq(p,v) = sup q‚Ä≤‚ąąqB sup U:q(U)=p q‚Ä≤(U) ‚Č§ sup q‚Ä≤‚ąąDF(q) sup U:q(U)=p q‚Ä≤(U) (7) enlarging the set of shifted distributions qB def = {q(¬∑‚ąív) : v ‚ąąB} to the set of distributions close to q in several f- divergences DF def = {q‚Ä≤ : Df(q‚Ä≤‚ą•q) ‚Č§ŌĶf,‚ąÄf ‚ąąF}, for a set of functions F. Dvijotham et al. (2019) showed that when F consists of all Hockey-Stick divergences, Eq. (7) becomes tight,, but in practice this is not feasible. In fact, Dvijotham et al. (2019) admits themselves that It turns out that the Renyi and KL divergences are computationally attractive for a broad class of smoothing measures, while the Hockey-Stick di- vergences are theoretically attractive as they leadRandomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes to optimal certiÔ¨Ācates in the information-limited setting. However, Hockey-Stick divergences are harder to estimate in general, so we only use them for Gaussian smoothing measures. Concretely, the looseness of their relaxation can be observed when comparing our baseline Laplace results (Table 1) with theirs. Operationally, their algorithm proceeds as follows 1. For each distribution qand function f, manually Ô¨Ānd the f-divergence ‚Äúball‚ÄĚ that contains {q(¬∑‚ąív) : v ‚ąą B}, i.e. compute {ŌĶf}f‚ąąF such that {q(¬∑‚ąív) : v‚ąąB}‚äÜ{ q‚Ä≤: Df(q‚Ä≤‚ą•q) ‚Č§ŌĶf,‚ąÄf ‚ąąF}. 2. Then they relax the original certiÔ¨Ācation problem to the certiÔ¨Ācation of all q‚Ä≤close to qin f-divergence, i.e. they solve Eq. (7) for the ŌĶf found in the previous step. The 2nd step is a straightforward low-dimensional convex optimization problem, but the trickiness of the 1st step limits the distributions they can apply their technique to. For example, they only know how to do step 1 for exp(‚ąí‚ą•x‚ą•p) against ‚Ąďpadversary, but not against‚Ąďr for rŐł= p; in contrast, our differential method computes robust radii for Laplace against ‚Ąď‚ąěperturbation, for example. Does Training and Testing on Different Noises Help? One may hope that certifying with a different noise than what a model was trained on may improve performance of the classiÔ¨Āer. For example, Exp2 noise with large j has more mass concentrated around zero compared to Gaussian noise, and may therefore be easier to ‚Äúde-noise‚ÄĚ. In this section we Ô¨Ānd that training and testing with different noises does not improve clean accuracy, when we compare noises at a Ô¨Āxed level of ŌÉ = 0.5 (Figure Fig. C.9). For all the noises we considered, testing a model with the same noise it was trained upon results in the best clean accuracy. This suggests the classiÔ¨Āer‚Äôs de-noising process is quite reliant on the properties of the noise to which it is exposed in the training process.Randomized Smoothing of All Shapes and Sizes Figure C.4.Effect of Architecture. Experiment Hyperparameters For all experiments we trained with a cosine-annealed learning rate of 0.1, opti- mized by stochastic gradient descent with momentum of 0.9 and weight decay of 0.0001. For ImageNet experiments we used a ResNet-50 model and trained with a batch size of 64 for 30 epochs. For CIFAR-10 experiments we used a Wide ResNet 40-2 model and trained with a batch size of 128 for 120 epochs. Ablation studies with a fully connected neural network em- ployed two hidden layers of 2048 and 512 nodes followed by ReLU activations, trained with a learning rate of 0.01. To compute the top categories for certiÔ¨Ācation (which used N = 100,000 samples), we used 64 samples. Our code is publicly available at: github.com/ tonyduan/rs4a More Data for Improved Robustness We explore using more data to improve the robustness of our SOTA smoothed classiÔ¨Āers for CIFAR-10 in two ways: using pre-training as in Hendrycks et al. (2019), and semi-supervised learning as in Carmon et al. (2019). Results are listed in Table 1 and Figure D.1. Pre-training is inspired by Hendrycks et al. We then re-initialize the Ô¨Ānal logit layers for the CIFAR-10 dataset and Ô¨Āne-tune with a learning rate of 0.001. Semi-supervised learning is inspired by Carmon et al. (2019), who showed that self-training on the unlabeled 80 Million Tiny Images dataset can improve robustness of CIFAR-10 classiÔ¨Āers. We use their publicly released dataset of 500k images equipped with pseudo-labels generated by a network trained by CIFAR-10, and train on mini-batches from this dataset and CIFAR-10. E. Mathematical Preliminaries In this section, we rigorously deÔ¨Āne several mathematical notions and their properties that will recurrent throughout what follows. We will be brief here, but readers can skip this on Ô¨Ārst reading and refer back when necessary. Note about Notation We will use Vol to denote measure, typically Hausdorff measure with the dimension implicit from context. When integrating over a measurable set, the underlying measure is also typically the Hausdorff measure as well. By ‚ąāU of a set U, we typically mean reduced boundary (when U has Ô¨Ānite perimeter), especially in a measure-theoretic context. Readers needing more back- ground can consult Evans & Gariepy (2015). Sobolev Functions and Regular Functions While many distributions like Gaussian have continuously differentiable densities, many others, like Laplace, only have ‚Äúweak‚ÄĚ derivatives. Thus, to cover all such distributions, we need to pin down a notion of ‚Äúweakly differentiable.‚ÄĚ DeÔ¨Ānition E.1. Let ‚Ą¶ ‚äÜRd, and f : ‚Ą¶ ‚ÜíR, g: ‚Ą¶ ‚ÜíRd. We say g is a weak derivative of f if for every smooth function ŌÜ: ‚Ą¶ ‚ÜíRd with compact support, ‚ąę fdiv ŌÜ= ‚ąí ‚ąę g¬∑ŌÜ. We write g= ‚ąáf in this case. For any open set ‚Ą¶ ‚äÜRd, the Sobolev space W1,p(‚Ą¶) is deÔ¨Āned as the functions f ‚ąąLp(‚Ą¶) whose weak derivative exists and is in Lp(‚Ą¶; Rd), i.e. W1,p(‚Ą¶) def = {f ‚ąąLp(‚Ą¶) : ‚ąáf ‚ąąLp(‚Ą¶; Rd)}. DeÔ¨Ānition E.2. Let ‚Ą¶ ‚äÜRdbe an open set. For the purpose of this paper, we say a function f : ‚Ą¶ ‚ÜíR is regular if f ‚ąąW1,1(‚Ą¶). This means that f has a weak derivative ‚ąáf such that both f and ‚ąáf are integrable. For example, ReLU is not a continuously differentiable function, but it is regular since it has the Heavyside step function as its weak derivative. Coarea Formula and the Weak Sard‚Äôs Theorem Theorem E.3 (Coarea Formula (Federer, 2014; Evans & Gariepy, 2015)). Let ‚Ą¶ ‚äÜRd be an open set, g ‚ąąL1(‚Ą¶), and f : Rd ‚ÜíR regular in the sense of DeÔ¨Ānition E.2. DeÔ¨Āne Ut def = {x : f(x) ‚Č•t}to be f‚Äôs superlevel sets. Sobolev Functions and Absolute Continuity Recall the standard deÔ¨Ānition of absolute continuity, which can be thought of as a more general notion of differentiability. DeÔ¨Ānition E.5. Wulff Crystal The following is an intuitive statement of the main isoperi- metric property of Wulff Crystals. Theorem G.1 (Isoperimetric property of Wulff Crystals (Brothers & Morgan, 1994), informal statement). Let ‚ą•¬∑‚ą• be any norm on Rn. Let Zbe the Wulff Crystal of ‚ą•¬∑‚ą•, i.e. the unit ball of the norm ‚ą•¬∑‚ą•‚ąódual to ‚ą•¬∑‚ą•. Z = {x: ‚ą•x‚ą•‚ąó‚Č§1}. Let ‚Ą¶ be any measurable subset of Rn with Ô¨Ānite perimeter and of the same volume as Z. Then with n(‚Ą¶,x) denoting the normal vector at xwith respect to ‚Ą¶, normalized to have ‚Ąď2 norm 1, ‚ąę ‚ąā‚Ą¶ ‚ą•n(‚Ą¶,x)‚ą•dx‚Č• ‚ąę ‚ąāZ ‚ą•n(Z,x)‚ą•dx with equality holding if and only if‚Ą¶ differs from a translate of Zby a set of volume zero. This statement carries across the core essence of the isoperimetry, and is a rigorous statement if ‚Ą¶ is restricted to have smooth boundary, but care needs to be taken to ex- plain the concept of ‚ÄúÔ¨Ānite perimeter,‚ÄĚ ‚Äúnormal vector,‚ÄĚ the ‚Äúboundary ‚ąā‚Ą¶,‚ÄĚ and the boundary measure on‚ąā‚Ą¶, in the con- text of general, measurable ‚Ą¶. These quantities are deÔ¨Āned in Appendix H, but we also refer the interested reader to (Brothers & Morgan, 1994) for more mathematical details. G.1. Wulff Crystals are Zonotopes In this paper, the norm ‚ą•¬∑‚ą• referred to in Theorem G.1 will take the form of ‚ą•x‚ą•= Ev|‚ü®x,v‚ü©|where vis sampled from some distribution, as in DeÔ¨Ānition 5.1. Given a Ô¨Ānite collection of vec- tors S, the zonotope Zon(S) is deÔ¨Āned as the Minkowski sum of the vectors of S, i.e. Zon(S) def = {‚ąĎ v‚ąąS avv: av ‚ąą[0,1],‚ąÄv‚ąąS } . Generalization of Differential Method and Wulff Crystal Optimality Results to Bounded Variation Densities While the regularity condition DeÔ¨Ānition E.2 covers most distributions we care about, we still have to reason sepa- rately about, e.g. uniform distributions on sets, or mixture of such distributions and regular distribution. However, regu- larity can be weakened to the notion ofbounded variation to cover all such cases. Bounded variation (BV) is ‚Äúessentially the weakest measure theoretic sense in which a function can be differentiable‚ÄĚ (Evans & Gariepy, 2015). BV functions include the usual continuously differentiable functions as well as indicator functions of ‚ÄúÔ¨Ānite perimeter‚ÄĚ sets. More generally, the notion of BV allows a ‚Äúcontrolled‚ÄĚ amount of jump-type discontinuities. Our differential method and our Wulff Crystal optimality results can be generalized to distributions with BV densities. Readers exposed to the notion of bounded variation for the Ô¨Ārst time might Ô¨Ānd it helpful to mentally substitute ‚ÄúBV‚ÄĚ in our results below with ‚Äúdifferentiable‚ÄĚ or with ‚Äúindicator function‚ÄĚ on the Ô¨Ārst read through. All probability distribu- tion densities we work with concretely in this paper have bounded variation. DeÔ¨Ānition H.1 (Bounded Variation). Let ‚Ą¶ ‚äÜRd be an open set. A function f ‚ąąL1(‚Ą¶) is said be of bounded variation (or BV), writtenf ‚ąąBV(‚Ą¶), if there exists a Ô¨Ānite Radon measure |Df|on Rd along with a vector function n : Rd ‚ÜíRd with ‚ą•n(x)‚ą•2 = 1 almost everywhere w.r.t. |Df|, such that, for every compactly supported, continuous differentiable ŌÜ: ‚Ą¶ ‚ÜíRd, we have ‚ąę ‚Ą¶ f(x) divŌÜ(x) dx= ‚ąí ‚ąę ‚Ą¶ ‚ü®ŌÜ,n(x)‚ü©|Df|(x). llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Presents a general theory and practical framework for randomized smoothing across norms. (1) Introduces two general certification techniques‚ÄĒthe level set method (exact via coarea/Neyman‚ÄďPearson geometry) and the differential method (infinitesimal growth bound integrating 1/ő¶)‚ÄĒthat yield provable radii for broad families of smoothing distributions. (2) Proves that, for symmetric norm balls, optimal smoothing distributions (in an appropriate sense) have level sets equal to the norm‚Äôs Wulff Crystal (e.g., cube for ‚Ąď1, sphere for ‚Ąď2, a zonotope for ‚Ąď‚ąě). (3) Derives certified radii for many distribution‚Äďnorm pairs (Gaussian, Laplace, uniform, exponential/power-law families), producing closed forms or efficient tables. (4) Achieves state-of-the-art ‚Ąď1 certified accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet using uniform (cube) noise, further boosted by stability training, semi-supervision, and pre-training. (5) Establishes a no-go theorem: any information-limited randomized smoothing scheme (using only label probabilities under noise) cannot certify beyond O(min(1, d^{-1/2+1/p})) radius for ‚Ąďp (notably O(d^{-1/2}) for ‚Ąď‚ąě), via Banach space cotype/metric-embedding arguments.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Growth function and Neyman‚ÄďPearson sets: Formalizes certification via G_q(p, v), the worst-case measure growth under shifts, and uses NP sets to link to exact bounds for certain distributions.
- Level set method: Decomposes NP measures via the coarea formula into integrals over level/superlevel sets; enables exact or numerically precise computation when level sets are symmetric (spheres, cubes). Provides precomputation/lookup algorithms for radii (Algorithms 1‚Äď2).
- Differential method: Defines ő¶(p) as the worst-case directional derivative of set measure under infinitesimal shift; integrates R = ‚ąę_{1-ŌĀ}^{1/2} 1/ő¶(p) dp to lower-bound robust radius. Produces symbolic expressions for broad distribution families (iid log-concave/convex*, ‚Ąď‚ąě/‚Ąď2-based exponentials, power law, Pareto), and recovers known Gaussian/Laplace bounds.
- Wulff Crystals: Uses geometric measure theory (isoperimetry for sets of finite perimeter, coarea, BV functions) to prove that among level-equivalent distributions, those with Wulff Crystal superlevel sets minimize ő¶(1/2); and among uniform measures, Wulff Crystal-shaped supports minimize instantaneous measure growth. Provides zonotope characterization and examples (cube for ‚Ąď1, sphere for ‚Ąď2, zonotope of {¬Ī1}^d for ‚Ąď‚ąě).
- No-go theorem: Models smoothing schemes as metric embeddings into total variation space, applies threshold-map arguments and cotype lower bounds (via embeddings into ‚Ąď_{0.99}), yielding dimension-dependent limits for information-limited randomized smoothing.
- Derived closed-form/efficient bounds: Table of radii for numerous distributions and norms; includes generalizations to BV densities and multiple families (e.g., Gamma/Beta/Beta-prime based formulas).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Additional data: 80 Million Tiny Images (pseudo-labeled) for semi-supervised training; downsampled ImageNet for pre-training.
- Models: Wide ResNet 40-2 (CIFAR-10), ResNet-50 (ImageNet). Also ablations with MLP and AlexNet.
- Training: Primarily noise augmentation with various smoothing distributions; additional setups include stability training (ő≥=6), semi-supervised self-training with 500k pseudo-labeled images, and pre-training followed by fine-tuning.
- Distributions evaluated: Gaussian, Laplace, uniform over ‚Ąď‚ąě-cube, ‚Ąď‚ąě-based exponential/power-law families, Pareto (iid), ‚Ąď2-based exponential/power-law, uniform over ‚Ąď2-ball. Swept noise scale ŌÉ over a broad range; for cube-level-set distributions used larger ŌÉ range to cover large radii.
- Certification: Monte Carlo with N=100,000 samples and failure probability őĪ=0.001. Report certified top-1 accuracy vs radius; for each radius, select the best model across ŌÉ (upper envelope). Considered ‚Ąď1, ‚Ąď2, and ‚Ąď‚ąě adversaries using the new bounds/tables.
- Key findings: Uniform (cube) noise substantially improves ‚Ąď1 certified accuracy on both datasets, setting new SOTA; spherical-level-set noises are best for ‚Ąď‚ąě and comparable for ‚Ąď2. Code: github.com/tonyduan/rs4a.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- The strongest results and improvements are for ‚Ąď1; ‚Ąď2 results are comparable to Gaussian baselines, and ‚Ąď‚ąě remains limited by dimension-dependent barriers.
- The certification framework and no-go theorem focus on information-limited smoothing (using only label probabilities under noise); stronger certificates may require richer classifier statistics or structure.
- Level set method is most tractable for highly symmetric level sets (spheres/cubes); general shapes may require numerical tables or be hard to analyze.
- Optimal Wulff distributions for ‚Ąď‚ąě are complex (zonotopes of {¬Ī1}^d) and difficult to sample; spherical approximations are used instead.
- Theoretical optimality results for Wulff Crystal distributions are clearest near hard inputs (ŌĀ‚Čą1/2); behavior far from this regime is not fully characterized.
- Certification requires large Monte Carlo budgets (N=100k) and can be computationally intensive; noise can degrade clean accuracy at higher ŌÉ.
- Assumptions include even/symmetric densities, BV regularity, and symmetry of the norm ball; results may not directly extend beyond these conditions.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Move beyond the information-limited setting: develop scalable full-information certificates that leverage more statistics (moments, logits, decision regions) or classifier structure to bypass cotype barriers.
- Multi-class specific certification using joint reasoning about more than the top-2 classes; design smoothing and analysis tailored to multi-class geometry.
- Practical constructions/samplers for ‚Ąď‚ąě Wulff Crystal (zonotope) distributions or tight spherical approximations with improved constants.
- Extend level set/differential methods to less symmetric level sets with efficient computation; automate symbolic derivations for broader distribution families.
- Integrate the new bounds into certified training objectives; combine with adversarial/stability training, semi-supervised learning, and pre-training systematically.
- Explore other threat models and metrics (e.g., Wasserstein, functional norms) under the same framework.
- Theoretically characterize optimality away from ŌĀ‚Čą1/2 and understand trade-offs between clean accuracy and robustness for different noise families.
- Bridge to differential privacy: transfer optimization and analysis of noise distributions between robustness and privacy objectives.

research_study_list.title: Higher-Order Certification For Randomized Smoothing
research_study_list.abstract:
Randomized smoothing is a recently proposed defense against adversarial
attacks that has achieved SOTA provable robustness against $\ell_2$
perturbations. A number of publications have extended the guarantees to other
metrics, such as $\ell_1$ or $\ell_\infty$, by using different smoothing
measures. Although the current framework has been shown to yield near-optimal
$\ell_p$ radii, the total safety region certified by the current framework can
be arbitrarily small compared to the optimal. In this work, we propose a
framework to improve the certified safety region for these smoothed classifiers
without changing the underlying smoothing scheme. The theoretical contributions
are as follows: 1) We generalize the certification for randomized smoothing by
reformulating certified radius calculation as a nested optimization problem
over a class of functions. 2) We provide a method to calculate the certified
safety region using $0^{th}$-order and $1^{st}$-order information for
Gaussian-smoothed classifiers. We also provide a framework that generalizes the
calculation for certification using higher-order information. 3) We design
efficient, high-confidence estimators for the relevant statistics of the
first-order information. Combining the theoretical contribution 2) and 3)
allows us to certify safety region that are significantly larger than the ones
provided by the current methods. On CIFAR10 and Imagenet datasets, the new
regions certified by our approach achieve significant improvements on general
$\ell_1$ certified radii and on the $\ell_2$ certified radii for color-space
attacks ($\ell_2$ restricted to 1 channel) while also achieving smaller
improvements on the general $\ell_2$ certified radii. Our framework can also
provide a way to circumvent the current impossibility results on achieving
higher magnitude of certified radii without requiring the use of data-dependent
smoothing techniques.
research_study_list.full_text: Higher-Order CertiÔ¨Ācation for Randomized Smoothing Jeet Mohapatra1 Ching-Yun Ko1 Tsui-Wei Weng1,2 Pin-Yu Chen2 Sijia Liu2 Luca Daniel1 1 MIT 2 MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab, IBM Research Abstract Randomized smoothing is a recently proposed defense against adversarial attacks that has achieved state-of-the-art provable robustness against ‚Ąď2 perturbations. A number of publications have extended the guarantees to other metrics, such as ‚Ąď1 or ‚Ąď‚ąě, by using different smoothing measures. Although the current framework has been shown to yield near-optimal ‚Ąďp radii, the total safety region certiÔ¨Āed by the current framework can be arbitrarily small compared to the optimal. In this work, we propose a framework to improve the certiÔ¨Āed safety region for these smoothed classiÔ¨Āers without changing the underlying smoothing scheme. The theoretical contributions are as follows: 1) We generalize the certiÔ¨Ācation for randomized smoothing by reformulating certiÔ¨Āed radius calculation as a nested optimization problem over a class of functions. 2) We provide a method to calcu- late the certiÔ¨Āed safety region using zeroth-order and Ô¨Ārst-order information for Gaussian-smoothed classiÔ¨Āers. We also provide a framework that generalizes the calculation for certiÔ¨Ācation using higher-order information. 3) We design efÔ¨Ācient, high-conÔ¨Ādence estimators for the relevant statistics of the Ô¨Ārst-order information. Combining the theoretical contribution 2) and 3) allows us to certify safety region that are signiÔ¨Ācantly larger than the ones provided by the current methods. On CIFAR10 and Imagenet datasets, the new regions certiÔ¨Āed by our approach achieve signiÔ¨Ācant improvements on general ‚Ąď1 certiÔ¨Āed radii and on the ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radii for color-space attacks (‚Ąď2 perturbation restricted to only one color/channel) while also achieving smaller improvements on the general ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radii. As discussed in the future works section, our framework can also provide a way to circumvent the current impossibility results on achieving higher magnitudes of certiÔ¨Āed radii without requiring the use of data-dependent smoothing techniques. 1 Introduction Deep neural networks (DNNs) can be highly sensitive, i.e., small imperceptible input perturbations can lead to mis-classiÔ¨Ācation [1, 2]. This poses a big problem for the deployment of DNNs in safety critical applications including aircraft control systems, video surveillance and self-driving cars, which require near-zero tolerance to lack of robustness. Thus, it is important to provide guarantees for the robustness of deep neural network models against multiple worst-case perturbations. Popular threat models are the ‚Ąďp-norm-based attacks, where possible perturbations are constrained in an ‚Ąďp-ball with respect to a given input x. To that end recent research efforts have focused on attack-agnostic robustness certiÔ¨Ācation which, given an input x, provides a safety region within which the model is guaranteed not to change its prediction. Randomized smoothing is a recently-proposed defense [ 3, 4, 5] that has achieved state-of-the-art robustness guarantees. Given any classiÔ¨Āer f, denoted as a base classiÔ¨Āer, randomized smoothing Preprint. Under review. arXiv:2010.06651v1  [cs.LG]  13 Oct 2020predicts the class that is ‚Äúmost likely‚ÄĚ to be returned when noise is added to the input x. Thus, randomized smoothing acts as an operator that given a base classiÔ¨Āer and a noise model, denoted as smoothing measure, produces a new classiÔ¨Āer, denoted as the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer. The smoothed classiÔ¨Āers thus produced are easily-certiÔ¨Āable with strong (even near-optimal for ‚Ąď2) robustness guarantees under various ‚Ąďp norm threat models [3, 6, 7]). However, for any given threat model, the state-of-the-art robustness guarantees are achieved only for the smoothed classiÔ¨Āers obtained using very speciÔ¨Āc smoothing measures. Thus, the classiÔ¨Āers that attain state-of-the-art guarantees under one threat model might perform poorly under another (Gaussian-smoothed classiÔ¨Āers are optimal under ‚Ąď2 but perform poorly under ‚Ąď1; uniform noise- smoothed classiÔ¨Āers are state-of-the-art under ‚Ąď1 but have poor performance under ‚Ąď2). Moreover, some of the recent works [8, 9, 10] show that the existing framework, which uses only the zeroth order information (the function value of the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer g(x)), is incapable of producing large certiÔ¨Āed radii for ‚Ąďp norms with high values of p. Motivated by the two limitations above, we focus our attention on improving the certiÔ¨Āed safety region which is agnostic of threat models. To that end, we propose a general framework to provide a larger certiÔ¨Āed safety region by better utilizing the information derived from a hard-label classiÔ¨Āer. In particular, we summarize our contributions as follows: 1. We propose a general framework that calculates a certiÔ¨Āed safety region of a smoothed classiÔ¨Āer g, around an input point x, by exploiting the estimated local properties (e.g. gradient, Hessian, etc.) of the classiÔ¨Āer gat x. 2. We give a threat-model-agnostic asymptotic-optimality result for smoothed classiÔ¨Āers ob- tained by using standard Gaussian as the smoothing measure, i.e. gaussian-smoothed classiÔ¨Āers. Using Theorem 2 in Section 3.1, we show that theoretically it is possible to produce arbitrarily tight certiÔ¨Ācates for any classiÔ¨Āer1. As a consequence, we see that the impossibility results for the existing framework cannot be extended to certiÔ¨Ācates obtained using higher-order information. 3. We motivate and prove properties, like convexity (Proposition 1) and non-decreasing depen- dence on angle (Proposition 2), regarding the certiÔ¨Āed safety regions of gaussian-smoothed classiÔ¨Āers produced using the zeroth and Ô¨Ārst-order local information. Using these properties, we give formulas for calculating certiÔ¨Āed radii for gaussian-smoothed classiÔ¨Āers under ‚Ąďp threat models and their subspace variants with p= 1,2,‚ąě. 4. We design new efÔ¨Ācient estimators (see Table 1) to provide high-conÔ¨Ādence interval esti- mates of relevant Ô¨Ārst-order information about the classiÔ¨Āer since the naive Monte-Carlo estimators have prohibitively high sample complexity. Finally, we use the 3rd and the 4th contributions above to empirically verify the effectiveness of the new certiÔ¨Ācation framework in providing state-of-the-art certiÔ¨Āed accuracy for multiple threat models simultaneously. In particular, our proposed framework substantially boosts the certiÔ¨Āed accuracy for ‚Ąď1 norm and subspace ‚Ąď2 norm while maintaining (at times marginally improving) the state-of-the-art near-optimal results for ‚Ąď2 norm. On the CIFAR10 dataset, our results for the ‚Ąď‚ąěnorm also show improvement over the state-of-the-art bounds given by Gaussian smoothing. 2 Background and Related Works 2.1 Related Works Randomized Smoothing. Randomized smoothing was initially introduced as a heuristic defense by [11] and [12]. Later, [3] formulated it as a certiÔ¨Ācation method using ideas from differential privacy, which was then improved by [ 6] using Renyi divergence. For gaussian-smoothed classiÔ¨Āers, [ 5] made the certiÔ¨Āed bounds worst-case-optimal in the context of certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď2 norm radii by using the Neyman-Pearson Lemma, while authors of [13] combined the certiÔ¨Ācation method with adversarial training to further improve the empirical results. Along another line of works, some extended existing certiÔ¨Ācation methods to get better ‚Ąďp norm certiÔ¨Āed radii using different smoothing distributions, e.g. 1For this result, the optimality assumes we abstain at points where the top-1 class has probability lower than 0.5. 2a discrete distribution for ‚Ąď0 certiÔ¨Ācates [14], the Laplace distribution for ‚Ąď1 certiÔ¨Ācates [15], and the generalized Gaussian distribution for ‚Ąď‚ąěcertiÔ¨Ācates [16]. Recently, [8] proposed a general method for Ô¨Ānding the optimal smoothing distribution given any threat model, as well as a framework for calculating the certiÔ¨Āed robustness for the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer. Impossibility Results. Recently, a number of works have shown that for ‚Ąďp norm threat models with large p, it is impossible to give a big certiÔ¨Āed radius ( O(d 1 p‚ąí1 2 ) where dis the input dimension) while retaining a high standard accuracy. In particular, the results on ‚Ąď‚ąěthreat model given in [ 9, 10] and the results on ‚Ąďp (for sufÔ¨Āciently large p) threat models given in [ 8] establish a certiÔ¨Ācation/accuracy trade-off, which also exaggerates the need for an extended and generalized framework that breaks the conÔ¨Āned trade-off and impossibility results. 2.2 Preliminaries and Notations Generally, a classiÔ¨Āer in machine learning is represented as a function f that maps from the feature space Rd to a probability vector over all the classes Y. We use Fto denote the set of all classiÔ¨Āers f : Rd ‚Ü¶‚Üí‚ąÜY, where ‚ąÜYis a probability simplex collecting all possible probability vectors with dimension Y. In this paper, we work under the restrictive black-box model, i.e., we assume we only have access to the class labels outputted by classiÔ¨Āer f. For ease of use, we consider the output of f to be the hard-thresholding of the probability vector, i.e. the output is a one-hot encoding vector. Given a black-box classiÔ¨Āer f ‚ąąF, randomized smoothing is a technique that creates a smoothed classiÔ¨Āer gby convolving f (hereafter referred to as the base classiÔ¨Āer) with a smoothing measure ¬Ķ, i.e., g = f ‚čÜ¬Ķ. We use G¬Ķ F to denote the class of these smoothed functions and ‚čÜdenotes the convolution operator. Note that the smoothing measure ¬Ķis a probability measure and each smoothed function g‚ąąG¬Ķ FdeÔ¨Ānes a mapping from Rd to ‚ąÜY. Thus, we have G¬Ķ F‚äāF. 3 A General Framework for Randomized Smoothing Given a smoothing measure ¬Ķ, the certiÔ¨Āed safety region around a point xis given as the subset of the space over which the classiÔ¨Āer output is guaranteed not to change. In practice, this involves estimating some local information about garound the point xand giving the certiÔ¨Āed safety region as the common subset (intersection) of the safety region of all classiÔ¨Āer h‚ąąG¬Ķ Fwhose local properties match the estimated local information. In the current literature, the only local information used is the function value g(x) [5, 13]; however, more local information of g(x), such as the gradient or higher-order derivatives, can in fact be exploited for deriving better certiÔ¨Āed radii. Under the proposed general framework for calculating certiÔ¨Āed radii, it is easy to see that adding more local constraints ( Hx i ) in Equation (2) gives a bigger value of px(z) for any x,z which makes the super-level set of px, equivalently the certiÔ¨Āed safety region, bigger. Then, we provide an example usage of this framework that exploits additional local higher-order information to give larger certiÔ¨Āed radii. The proofs of all theoretical results hereafter are supplemented in the appendix. 3.1 Regularity Properties of G¬Ķ F For any black-box classiÔ¨Āer f, the function map between the input pixel-space Rd and the prediction ‚ąÜYis discontinuous. As a result, the higher-order derivatives might not always exist for the smoothed function gfor smoothing with general probability measures ¬Ķ. Therefore, it is crucial to establish conditions on the probability measure ¬Ķthat guarantee the existence of higher-order derivatives for g at all points in the pixel-space Rd. Therefore in this section we complete the framework by giving ways to get high conÔ¨Ādence estimates of vector y(1). Recalling that the hard-label classiÔ¨Āer f is not continuous and hence not differentiable, we need to use the zeroth order information about f to approximate y(1) , i.e., use the result from Corollary 1.1 : y(1) = Ey‚ąľ¬Ķ[ y ŌÉ2 f(x+ y)]. As the estimator y ŌÉ2 f(x+ y) is noisy [13], approximating the expectation using Monte-Carlo sampling is hard, i.e., the number of samples required to achieve a non-trivial high-conÔ¨Ādence estimate of y(1) scales linearly with the input dimension. In practice, this translates to a sample complexity of 10 billion for CIFAR and near a trillion for Imagenet. Although estimating the vector y(1) is hard, we observe from Corollary 3.2, 3.1, 3.3, 3.4 that we don‚Äôt need the whole vectory(1) but only ÓĶĻÓĶĻy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ 2, ÓĶĻÓĶĻy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ ‚ąěfor ‚Ąď1 certiÔ¨Āed radius; ÓĶĻÓĶĻy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ 2 for ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radius; ÓĶĻÓĶĻy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ 1, ÓĶĻÓĶĻy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ 2 for ‚Ąď‚ąěcertiÔ¨Āed radius; and ÓĶĻÓĶĻPSy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ p, ÓĶĻÓĶĻy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ 2 for the subspace ‚Ąďp certiÔ¨Āed radius (p= 1,2,‚ąě). Most of these statistics can be estimated much more efÔ¨Āciently due to the following observation : Theorem 4. Given a black-box classiÔ¨Āer f and the random vector z = w(f(x+ w)c ‚ąí1 2 ) where w‚ąľN(0,ŌÉ2I), we have thatz‚ąíŌÉ2y(1) is a sub-gaussian random vector with parameterk= ŌÉ2(1 4 + 3‚ąö 8ŌÄe). We reuse the models given by Cohen et al. [5] and calculate the certiÔ¨Āed accuracy at radius Rby counting the samples of the test set that are correctly classiÔ¨Āed by the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer gwith certiÔ¨Āed radii of at least R. For both our proposed certiÔ¨Ācate and the baseline certiÔ¨Ācate [5], we use a failure probability of őĪ= 0.001 and N = 200,000 samples for CIFAR10 and N = 1,250,000 samples for Imagenet. For ‚Ąď‚ąěradius we require a lot more samples to get better results as our current estimator is too noisy. In order to show the possibility presented by our approach, we also provide results for the ‚Ąď‚ąěcertiÔ¨Āed radius for CIFAR10 estimated using 4,000,000 samples. In our plots, we present, for each threat model the upper envelopes of certiÔ¨Āed accuracies attained over the range of considered ŌÉ‚ąą{0.12,0.25,0.50,1.00}. Further experiments (including all Imagenet experiments) are given in the appendix. 85.1 CertiÔ¨Āed Accuracy As expected we see from Figure 2b that the new framework gives only marginal improvement over the ‚Ąď2 radius certiÔ¨Āed by existing methods. This follows from the fact that the existing methods already produce near-optimal certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď2 radii. However, certifying a signiÔ¨Ācantly bigger certiÔ¨Āed safety region allows us to give signiÔ¨Ācant improvements over the certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď1 radius, the ‚Ąď‚ąěradius and the subspace ‚Ąď2 radii (the subspace considered here is the red channel of the image, i.e., we only allow perturbations over red component of the RGB pixels of the image). We note here that considering the performance for individual threat models, the ‚Ąď1 norm certiÔ¨Āed accuracy given here is still smaller than the existing state-of-the-art. However, the current state-of-the-art for ‚Ąď1 certiÔ¨Āed radius uses the uniform smoothing distribution which performs quite poorly for ‚Ąď2 norm radius. If we consider multiple threat models simultaneously the proposed method gives the best joint performance. Similar Ô¨Āndings are also reported for Imagenet experiments (given in the appendix). Moreover, we see that for the certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď‚ąěradius the new method gives an improvement over the certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď‚ąěradius provided by existing methods for Gaussian noise. As Gaussian noise was shown to achieve the best possible certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď‚ąěradius for existing methods, this provides an improvement over the best possible ‚Ąď‚ąěradius certiÔ¨Āable using existing methods. Although the improvements are small in magnitude, we believe they can be improved upon by using tighter estimators for ÓĶĻÓĶĻy(1)ÓĶĻÓĶĻ 1. 6 Conclusion and Future Work In this work, we give a new direction for improving the robustness certiÔ¨Ācates for randomized- smoothing-based classiÔ¨Āers. We have shown that even in the black-box (hard-label classiÔ¨Āer) setting, leveraging more information about the distribution of the labels among the sampled points allows us to certify larger regions and thus guarantee large certiÔ¨Āed radii against multiple threat models simultaneously. We have shown this to hold theoretically and also demonstrated it on CIFAR and Imagenet classiÔ¨Āers. Additionally, for gaussian smoothing, the proposed framework gives a way to circumvent the recently-proposed impossibility results and also promises a threat-model-agnostic asymptotic-optimality result. However, we note that the Ô¨Ārst-order smoothing technique given in this paper is only a proof-of-concept to show it is possible to better leverage local information to certify larger safety regions without changing the smoothing measure. In future, this work could be extended to derive and use, for any given threat model, the best local information to exploit in order to improve the certiÔ¨Ācates for that threat model. 9Broader Impact In recent years, machine learning and intelligent systems have started to be widely adopted into everyday life, including several safety-critical applications. The wide-spread use of these systems requires them to be held to a higher level of scrutiny. One such requirement is ‚Äúrobustness‚ÄĚ, i.e., the systems‚Äô prediction should not be too sensitive to small input perturbations. In general, unde- fended/vanilla deep learning models have been found to be extremely sensitive to small imperceptible perturbations. This in turn has given rise to many defense techniques, able to overcome ‚Äúexisting‚ÄĚ threats to robustness. However most of these models have later proved to be ineffective against newer threats. In turn, this has then given rise to a class of techniques which provide some mathematical guarantees about the robustness of their predictions. In this paper, we extended one such framework that provides some mathematically provable guarantees against any ‚Äúsingle individual‚ÄĚ threat model chosen from a large class of threat models. SpeciÔ¨Ācally, we provided a way to construct models with some mathematically provable guarantees against ‚Äúmultiple simultaneous‚ÄĚ threat models. The beneÔ¨Āts of this contribution include providing a more holistic picture of the robustness guarantees for deployed models. This can hopefully bring us a step closer to trustworthy AI. Since this work helps building models that give some guarantees on their behavior, we hope it would also lead to the wider adoption of deep learning models. Moreover, we hope that the guarantees given by these models would allow people to have some improved sense of security when using deep-learning-based products. Considering intensive applications such as product matching, categorization in assembly lines or video surveillance that require long periods of hard effort, robust models will give more reliable and efÔ¨Ācient means of achieving the task and considerably reducing the burden on humans. As for the potentially negative effects of this contribution, we feel that any progress towards robust- ness certiÔ¨Ācation could easily become a double-edged sword. This is because, if adopted blindly (i.e. speciÔ¨Ācally without extremely careful attention to the types of guarantees provided and most impor- tantly those NOT provided), it may give also the false sense of security that current deep-learning systems are already ready for deployment. These robustness concerns are one of the major bottlenecks for the adoption of deep-learning models in safety-critical applications such as self-driving car and air trafÔ¨Āc control systems. hence the ability to provide ‚Äúsome‚ÄĚ robustness guarantees might result in wide premature adoption of deep learning models in such applications. We would like to candidly admit to developer and user readers of this paper that the presence of some robustness guarantees for a model does not mean we understand the model or that it is completely safe to deploy it. To the contrary the authors of this paper believe that we are still quite far from such safe adoption scenario. Further issues lie also beyond robustness. A negative effect of this publication and publication like this on robustness, is might give the highly incorrect impression that with robustness one can feel safe in deploying AI systems in society. On the contrary we would also like to candidly admit and remind the readers that deep-learning-based models suffer from problems such as lack of accuracy guarantees for out-of-distribution data , lack of fairness, lack of explain-ability and many others that MUST to be solved before AI systems are viable for real-world applications. More speciÔ¨Ācally about the lack of accuracy guarantees for out-of-distribution data: the robustness of a model does not necessarily mean its prediction is always accurate. Robustness and accuracy are indeed two disjoint concepts. While it is well-known that an accurate prediction might not be robust, it is also essential to keep in mind that a robust prediction need not always be accurate. In other words, some models may generate a prediction that is indeed highly robust (i.e. does not change upon perturbation) but consistently incorrect! As a result, in applications such as air trafÔ¨Āc control systems, the models might display extremely bad behaviour because of situations not present in its training, while the presence of some robustness guarantees might give a false sense of security to an inexperience user (e.g. not familiar with the admittedly subtle mathematical intricacies of different threat models), that these systems are completely fault-tolerant. In conclusion, we would like to remark that although this paper helps to take a step towards building more trustworthy AI, that goal is indeed still quite far-off. 10References [1] C. Szegedy, W.
