Using Theorem 4 and the properties of subgaussian random vectors, we see that Xn1 ‚ąľ subG( k n1 ),Yn2 ‚ąľsubG( k n2 ). Then, using Theorem 5 we get the required values of t,ŌĶu,ŌĶl. 26E Additional Experiments Here, we give additional experiments on the Imagenet dataset. We reuse the models given by Cohen et al. [5] and calculate the certiÔ¨Āed accuracy at radius Rby counting the samples of the test set that are correctly classiÔ¨Āed by the smoothed classiÔ¨Āer gwith certiÔ¨Āed radii of at least R. For both our proposed certiÔ¨Ācate and the baseline certiÔ¨Ācate [ 5], we use a failure probability of őĪ= 0.001 and N = 200,000 samples for CIFAR and N = 1,250,000 samples for Imagenet. In the following plots we give a more detailed account of the improvement seen by using both the Ô¨Ārst and zeroth order information. For every trained model (depending on variance ŌÉused during training), we give the certiÔ¨Āed accuracy under ‚Ąď2 norm threat model, ‚Ąď1 norm threat model and the subspace ‚Ąď2 norm threat model. Figure 4: CertiÔ¨Āed Accuracy for Imagenet seen under various threat models and ŌÉvalues. The scale of x-axis is different for the 3 different models (denoted by training noise variance) as the certiÔ¨Āed radii we get for these three models have different ranges. The Ô¨Āndings here are similar to the ones reported for CIFAR. As expected we see from Figure 4 that the smallest improvements are for ‚Ąď2 norm threat model where the new framework gives only marginal improvement over the ‚Ąď2 radius certiÔ¨Āed by existing methods. This follows from the fact that the existing methods already produce near-optimal certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď2 radii. However, certifying a signiÔ¨Ācantly bigger certiÔ¨Āed safety region allows us to give signiÔ¨Ācant improvements over the certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď1 radius and the subspace ‚Ąď2 radii (the subspace considered here is the red channel of the image, i.e., we only allow perturbations over red component of the RGB pixels of the image). From these Ô¨Āgures we are also able to see that, for any given model, most of the improvement in certiÔ¨Āed accuracy occurs at smaller values of radius R. We think one of the causes for this is the interval size of our estimates of y(0),y(1). So, we give the following results using larger number of samples to estimate both y(0),y(1). In Figure 5 we show the results by using the observed values from experiments us- ing Nobs = 1250000 and then constructing the estimates assuming we used N = 200000,800000,1250000,1600000,3200000,6400000 samples respectively. Using these estimates we get a certiÔ¨Āed ‚Ąď1 radius for both the existing method and for the proposed method. We see that 27Figure 5: Effect of number of samples used on certiÔ¨Āed accuracy. using larger number of samples allows us to get improvements at even larger values ofR. However, we note that it is still not possible to get improvements at very high values of R. We think this would require very precise bounds for y(0),y(1) and thus a very high number of samples. 28

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions:
- Introduces a general, higher-order certification framework for randomized smoothing that enlarges the certified safety region without changing the smoothing distribution.
- Reformulates certification as a nested optimization problem over smoothed classifiers using local constraints (function value, gradient, higher-order derivatives) and solves it via a generalized Neyman‚ÄďPearson approach.
- Proves regularity and asymptotic optimality for Gaussian-smoothed classifiers: Gaussian smoothing yields real-analytic g with infinite-radius Taylor series; with full higher-order info the framework recovers the exact binary safety region, bypassing prior impossibility results tied to zeroth-order methods.
- Derives first-order certification with key properties (convexity of safety region; directional robustness monotone in angle to gradient) and closed-form computations for ‚Ąď2, ‚Ąď1, ‚Ąď‚ąě and subspace ‚Ąďp radii.
- Designs efficient, high-confidence estimators for gradient-norm statistics needed for certification (||‚ąág||1, ||‚ąág||2, ||‚ąág||‚ąě, and subspace variants), dramatically reducing sample complexity vs naive Monte Carlo.
- Empirically demonstrates significant gains on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet in ‚Ąď1 and subspace ‚Ąď2 radii, small gains in general ‚Ąď2, and modest improvements in ‚Ąď‚ąě with larger sample sizes.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- General framework: Given smoothing measure ¬Ķ and a base classifier f, define smoothed g=f‚čÜ¬Ķ. For an input x, define the certified set via px(z)=min_{h‚ąąG_¬Ķ^F} h_c(z) subject to local constraints H_i^x(h)=H_i^x(g) (e.g., h(x), ‚ąáh(x), higher derivatives). Safety region is {z | px(z)>0.5} under top-1 vs rest reduction.
- Solution via Generalized Neyman‚ÄďPearson Lemma: For Gaussian ¬Ķ=N(0,ŌÉ^2I), and using first-order constraints y(0)=g(x), y(1)=‚ąág(x), derive a parametric form for the worst-case h leading to integral equations involving ő¶(c(x)) that lower bound px(x+ŌÉrv) (Theorem 3). Basis rotation/scaling isolates components parallel/orthogonal to gradient.
- Theoretical regularity: Show G_¬Ķ^F‚äāC^‚ąě and, for Gaussian ¬Ķ, g is real-analytic with infinite convergence radius; the Taylor series uniquely identifies g (Theorems 1‚Äď2).
- Geometry of first-order certified set: Prove convexity of the certified region and that directional robustness decreases with angle from ‚ąág(x) (Propositions 1‚Äď2). Use these to derive:
  - ‚Ąď2 radius via solving ő¶(w1‚ąír)‚ąíő¶(w2‚ąír)=0.5 with constraints from y(0), ||‚ąág||2 (Corollary 3.1).
  - ‚Ąď1 radius via worst standard basis direction (projection ‚ąí||‚ąág||‚ąě) and corresponding constraints (Corollary 3.2).
  - ‚Ąď‚ąě radius via worst ¬Ī1/‚ąöd direction (projection ‚ąí||‚ąág||1/‚ąöd) (Corollary 3.3).
  - Subspace ‚Ąďp radii (p‚ąą{1,2,‚ąě}) via projections PS‚ąág and dual norms (Corollary 3.4).
- Estimation of first-order stats: Show z=w(f(x+w)_c‚ąí1/2) with w‚ąľN(0,ŌÉ^2I) is sub-Gaussian around ŌÉ^2‚ąág(x) (Theorem 4). Derive concentration and product-of-sub-Gaussian bounds (Theorem 5) to construct high-confidence estimators for ||‚ąág||2, ||‚ąág||‚ąě, and approximate ||‚ąág||1 (Table 1), including subspace versions by projecting samples.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
- Models: Pretrained smoothed classifiers from Cohen et al. (2019); multiple Gaussian noise levels ŌÉ‚ąą{0.12, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00}.
- Evaluation metric: Certified accuracy at radius R (fraction of test samples both correctly classified by g and certified at ‚Č•R) under different threat models.
- Threat models: ‚Ąď2 (general), ‚Ąď1 (general), ‚Ąď‚ąě (general), and subspace ‚Ąď2 (restricted to single color channel‚ÄĒred).
- Monte Carlo settings: Failure probability őĪ=0.001; N=200,000 samples per input for CIFAR-10 and N=1,250,000 for ImageNet. For ‚Ąď‚ąě on CIFAR-10, also report results with 4,000,000 samples due to higher estimator variance. Report upper envelopes across ŌÉ.
- Additional analysis: Study the effect of increasing sample size on ‚Ąď1 certification (up to 6.4M effective samples) and provide detailed ImageNet plots across ŌÉ values.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Empirical method uses only first-order information; higher-order certification is not implemented, so asymptotic optimality is theoretical.
- Estimation of ||‚ąág||1 remains sample-inefficient/noisy; ‚Ąď‚ąě improvements are small and often require millions of samples, limiting practicality.
- Gains for general ‚Ąď2 radii are marginal because zeroth-order methods are already near-optimal for ‚Ąď2.
- Assumes binary reduction (top-1 vs rest) and abstains when top-1 probability ‚Č§0.5; optimality statements hold under this relaxation.
- Computational cost is high (200k‚Äď1.25M+ samples per point; more for ‚Ąď‚ąě), which may hinder scalability.
- ‚Ąď1 certified accuracy does not surpass specialized non-Gaussian smoothing methods tailored for ‚Ąď1, though joint performance across norms improves.
- Framework depends on accurate interval estimates of local statistics; uncertainty widens constraints and weakens certificates.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Implement and evaluate higher-order (second-order and beyond) certification to realize the asymptotic benefits and larger multi-norm radii.
- Develop tighter, lower-variance, and more sample-efficient estimators, especially for ||‚ąág||1, to improve ‚Ąď‚ąě certification; explore variance-reduction (antithetic sampling, control variates, importance sampling).
- Jointly train base classifiers to maximize first-/higher-order certifiable regions (e.g., training objectives that shape ‚ąág statistics) while maintaining accuracy.
- Extend the framework to other smoothing measures and hybrid/data-dependent smoothing while preserving agnosticism to threat models.
- Broaden subspace certifications (e.g., semantic/structured subspaces) and multi-threat joint certification objectives.
- Move beyond binary reduction to multi-class-aware certification if feasible.
- Optimize computational pipelines and parallelization to reduce sample costs; theoretical bounds linking sample size to certificate tightness.

research_study_list.title: Accelerating Certified Robustness Training via Knowledge Transfer
research_study_list.abstract:
Training deep neural network classifiers that are certifiably robust against
adversarial attacks is critical to ensuring the security and reliability of
AI-controlled systems. Although numerous state-of-the-art certified training
methods have been developed, they are computationally expensive and scale
poorly with respect to both dataset and network complexity. Widespread usage of
certified training is further hindered by the fact that periodic retraining is
necessary to incorporate new data and network improvements. In this paper, we
propose Certified Robustness Transfer (CRT), a general-purpose framework for
reducing the computational overhead of any certifiably robust training method
through knowledge transfer. Given a robust teacher, our framework uses a novel
training loss to transfer the teacher's robustness to the student. We provide
theoretical and empirical validation of CRT. Our experiments on CIFAR-10 show
that CRT speeds up certified robustness training by $8 \times$ on average
across three different architecture generations while achieving comparable
robustness to state-of-the-art methods. We also show that CRT can scale to
large-scale datasets like ImageNet.
research_study_list.full_text: Accelerating CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Training via Knowledge Transfer Pratik Vaishnavi Stony Brook University pvaishnavi@cs.stonybrook.edu Kevin Eykholt IBM Research kheykholt@ibm.com Amir Rahmati Stony Brook University amir@cs.stonybrook.edu Abstract Training deep neural network classiÔ¨Āers that are certiÔ¨Āably robust against adver- sarial attacks is critical to ensuring the security and reliability of AI-controlled systems. Although numerous state-of-the-art certiÔ¨Āed training methods have been developed, they are computationally expensive and scale poorly with respect to both dataset and network complexity. Widespread usage of certiÔ¨Āed training is fur- ther hindered by the fact that periodic retraining is necessary to incorporate new data and network improvements. In this paper, we propose CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a general-purpose framework for reducing the computational over- head of any certiÔ¨Āably robust training method through knowledge transfer. Given a robust teacher, our framework uses a novel training loss to transfer the teacher‚Äôs robustness to the student. We provide theoretical and empirical validation of CRT. Our experiments on CIFAR-10 show that CRT speeds up certiÔ¨Āed robustness train- ing by 8√óon average across three different architecture generations while achiev- ing comparable robustness to state-of-the-art methods. We also show that CRT can scale to large-scale datasets like ImageNet. 1 Introduction Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are susceptible to adversarial evasion attacks [31, 9], that add a small amount of carefully crafted imperceptible noise to an input to reliably trigger misclassiÔ¨Ācation. As a defense, numerous training methods have been proposed [25, 40, 35] to grant a classiÔ¨Āer empirical robustness. But in the absence of any provable guarantees for this robustness, these defenses were frequently broken [1, 32]. These failures have motivated the development of training methods that grant certiÔ¨Āable/provable robustness to a classiÔ¨Āer, hence safeguarding them against all attacks (known or unknown) within a pre-determined threat model. Such methods are broadly categorized as either deterministic or probabilistic [23]. Deterministic robustness training methods [12, 26, 33, 34, 28, 10, 41, 30] rely on computing provable bounds on the output neurons of a classiÔ¨Āer for a given perturbation budget in the input space. However, the deterministic robustness guarantees provided by these methods come at a high computational cost. Probabilistic robustness training methods address this limitation by providing highly probable (e.g., with 0.99 probability) robustness guarantees at a greatly reduced computational cost. Within this category, randomized smoothing- based methods [19, 3, 29, 22, 20, 7, 37, 39, 16, 15] are considered the state-of-the-art for certiÔ¨Āable robustness in the ‚Ąď2-space. Even so, these training methods remain an order of magnitude slower than standard training. In commercial applications where constant model re-deployment occurs to provide improvements (see Figure 1), re-training using computationally expensive methods is burdensome. 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). arXiv:2210.14283v1  [cs.LG]  25 Oct 2022ResNet101 (2016) ResNeXt101 (2017) DLA-X (2018) RegNetX-16GF (2020) ViT-B/16 (2021) 76 80 84 88Top-1 Accuracy (%)  45 60 75 90 Number of Params (M) Figure 1: Evolution of DNN architectures on the ImageNet dataset. We plot the performance (top-1 accuracy) and the number of parameters of a few popular architectures (year of release is noted in brackets). Newer generations attempt to improve performance and/or reduce network parameters. In this work, we reduce the training overhead of randomized smoothing-based robustness training methods with minimal impact on the robustness achieved. We propose CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a knowledge transfer framework that signiÔ¨Ācantly speeds up the process of training ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers. Given a pre-trained classiÔ¨Āer that is certiÔ¨Āably robust ( i.e., teacher), CRT trains a new classiÔ¨Āer ( i.e., student) that has comparable levels of robustness in a fraction of the time required by state-of-the-art methods. CRT brings down the cost of training certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers to be comparable to standard training while preserving state- of-the-art robustness. On CIFAR-10, CRT speeds up training by an average of 8√óacross three different architecture generations compared to a state-of-the-art robustness training method [ 15]. Furthermore, we show that state-of-the-art robustness training is only necessary to train the initial classiÔ¨Āer. Afterward, CRT can be continuously reused to transfer robustness in order to expedite future model re-deployments and greatly reduce costs associated with computational resources. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: ‚ÄĘ We present CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), the Ô¨Ārst framework, to our knowledge, that can transfer the robustness of a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher classiÔ¨Āer to a new student classiÔ¨Āer. CRT greatly reduces the time required to train certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers relative to existing state-of-the-art methods while achieving comparable or better robustness. ‚ÄĘ We provide a theoretical understanding of CRT, showing how our approach of matching outputs enables robustness transfer between the student and teacher irrespective of the certiÔ¨Āed robustness training method used to train the teacher. ‚ÄĘ On CIFAR-10, we show that CRT trains certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers on average8√ófaster than a state-of-the-art method while having comparable or better Average CertiÔ¨Āed Radius (by 8% in the best case). Furthermore, CRT reduces the cumulative computational cost of training three classiÔ¨Āers by 87.84%. ‚ÄĘ We also show that CRT can be reused in a recursive manner, thus supporting a continuous re- deployment scenario (e.g., in commercial applications). Finally, we show that CRT remains effective on a large-scale dataset, ImageNet. 2 Background In this section, we brieÔ¨āy introduce certiÔ¨Āed robustness and discuss notable existing methods for training certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers using randomized smoothing. 2.1 Preliminaries Problem Setup. Consider a neural network classiÔ¨Āer f parameterized by őł (denoted főł) trained to map a given input x ‚ąą Rd to a set of discrete labels Y using a set of i.i.d. samples S= {(x1,y1),(x1,y1),¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,(xn,yn)}drawn from a data distribution D. The output of the classiÔ¨Āer can be written as főł(x) = arg maxc‚ąąY zc őł(x). Here zőł(x) is the softmax output of the classiÔ¨Āer and zc őł(x) denotes the probability that image xbelongs to class c. 2CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness via Randomized Smoothing. The robustness of the classiÔ¨Āer főł for a given input pair (x,y) is deÔ¨Āned using the radius of the largest ‚Ąď2 ball centered at xwithin which főł has a constant output y. This radius is referred to asrobust radiusand it can mathematically be expressed as: R(főł; x,y) = { inf főł(x‚Ä≤)Őł=főł(x) ‚ą•x‚Ä≤‚ąíx‚ą•2 , when főł(x) = y 0 , when főł(x) Őł= y (1) Within this ‚Ąď2-neighborhood of x, főł is considered to be certiÔ¨Āably robust. Therefore, to improve the robustness of a classiÔ¨Āer, one needs to maximize this robust radius corresponding to any point sampled from the given data distribution. Directly maximizing the robust radius of a DNN classiÔ¨Āer is an NP-hard problem [ 17]. Therefore, several prior works attempt to derive a lower bound for the robust radius [21, 19, 3]. This lower bound, often termed as the certiÔ¨Āed radius, satisÔ¨Āes the following condition: 0 ‚Č§CR(főł; x,y) ‚Č§R(főł; x,y), for any főł, (x, y). In this paper, we utilize the certiÔ¨Āed robustness framework derived by Cohen et al. [3] using randomized smoothing. Given a classiÔ¨Āer főł, they Ô¨Ārst deÔ¨Āne the smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł as: DeÔ¨Ānition 2.1. For a given (base) classiÔ¨Āer főł and ŌÉ >0, the smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł corresponding to főł is deÔ¨Āned as follows: gőł(x) = arg max c‚ąąY Pő∑‚ąľN(0,ŌÉ2I)(főł(x+ ő∑) = c) (2) Simply put, gőł returns the class c, which has the highest probability mass under the Gaussian distribution N(x,ŌÉ2I). Using Theorem 2.2, they proved that if the smooth classiÔ¨Āer correctly classiÔ¨Āes a given input x, it is certiÔ¨Āably robust at x. They also provided an analytical form of the ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radius at x. Theorem 2.2. Let főł : Rd ‚Ü¶‚ÜíY be a classiÔ¨Āer and gőł be its smoothed version (as deÔ¨Āned in DeÔ¨Ānition 2.1). For a given input x ‚ąąRd and corresponding ground truth y ‚ąąY, if gőł correctly classiÔ¨Āes xas y, i.e., Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y) ‚Č•max y‚Ä≤Őł=y Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y‚Ä≤) (3) then gőł is provably robust at xwithin the certiÔ¨Āed radius Rgiven by: CR(gőł; x,y) = ŌÉ 2 [ő¶‚ąí1(Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y)) ‚ąíő¶‚ąí1(max y‚Ä≤Őł=y Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y‚Ä≤))] (4) where ő¶ is the c.d.f. of the standard Gaussian distribution. This certiÔ¨Āed radius is a tight lower bound of the robust radius deÔ¨Āned in Equation 1, i.e., it is impossible to certify gőł at xfor a radius larger than CR. 2.2 Training Methods for Maximizing CertiÔ¨Āed Radius In addition to the theoretical framework discussed above, Cohen et al. [3] also propose a simple yet effective method for training the base classiÔ¨Āer in a way that maximizes the ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radius of the smooth classiÔ¨Āer, as expressed in Equation 4. We include an evaluation of their method in Appendix B.6. Following their work, several other works build upon the randomized smoothing framework and propose training methods that better maximize the ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radius of the smooth classiÔ¨Āer. Salman et al. [29] proposed combining adversarial training [ 25] with randomized smooth- ing (called SmoothAdv). They adapted the vanilla PGD attack to target the smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł instead of the base classiÔ¨Āer főł. Zhai et al. [39] proposed a new robustness loss, a hinge loss that en- forces maximization of the soft approximation of the certiÔ¨Āed radius. Their method (called MACER) is faster than SmoothAdv as it does not use adversarial training. More recently, Jeong et al. [15] proposed training with a convex combination of samples along the direction of adversarial pertur- bation for each input to regularize over-conÔ¨Ādent predictions. Their method (called SmoothMix) is the current state-of-the-art in the domain of ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed robust image classiÔ¨Āers. Finally, we note the Consistency regularization method proposed by Jeong et al. [16], which adds a regularization loss to existing methods that helps better maximize the certiÔ¨Āed radius. 3Table 1: Training on CIFAR-10 using a ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer on a single Nvidia V100 GPU. State-of- the-art robustness training methods signiÔ¨Ācantly slow down training compared to standard training. METHOD TRAINING SLOWDOWN FACTOR SMOOTH ADV 46.20√ó MACER 20.86√ó SMOOTH MIX 4.97√ó 3 Maximizing CertiÔ¨Āed Radius via Knowledge Transfer Although prior works have proposed methods for increasing the certiÔ¨Āed radius of the smooth classiÔ¨Āer, their training overhead is signiÔ¨Ācant, making them much slower than standard training. As we show in Table 1, training a certiÔ¨Āably robust ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer to convergence using SmoothAdv, MACER, and SmoothMix is46.20√ó, 20.86√ó, and 4.97√óslower, respectively, compared to training a non-robust classiÔ¨Āer with standard training. Given constant innovations in architecture design (Figure 1) and the inÔ¨āux of new data, which may result in various tweaks to deployed networks that elicit retraining, the large overhead of state-of-the- art robustness training methods makes preserving certiÔ¨Āed robustness across model re-deployment difÔ¨Ācult. Therefore, we propose CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a training method that improves the usability of certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods by dramatically reducing their training overhead while preserving the certiÔ¨Āed robustness. Given the base classiÔ¨Āer of a pre-trained certiÔ¨Āably robust smooth classiÔ¨Āer, we leverage the knowledge transfer framework to guide the training of a new base classiÔ¨Āer (and its associated robust smooth classiÔ¨Āer).1 In this section, we describe our method and provide theoretical justiÔ¨Ācation for its effectiveness. 3.1 Transferring CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness From Equation 4, it follows that training the base classiÔ¨Āer to maximize Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y) for any given input xwill result in the maximization of the certiÔ¨Āed radius associated with the smooth classiÔ¨Āer, provided Equation 3 is satisÔ¨Āed. Thus, for the base classiÔ¨Āer főł(x), our goal is to maximize the following quantity over the training set: n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑1[főł(xi + ő∑) = yi] ‚Čą n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑[zyi őł (xi + ő∑)] (5) In the above equation, like prior works [3, 29, 39], we leverage the fact that the softmax output of a classiÔ¨Āer can be treated as a continuous and differentiable approximation of its arg max output. Methods like SmoothAdv [29], MACER [39] and SmoothMix [15] that target ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āable robustness propose training objectives that maximize this term. Now, suppose we have a pre-trained base classiÔ¨Āer fŌÜ. It follows that Eő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑)] ‚Č•0. Through straightforward algebraic manipulations (see Appendix A), we derive the following lower bound: n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑[zyi őł (xi + ő∑)] ‚Č•‚ąí n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑[zyi ŌÜ (xi + ő∑) ‚ąízyi őł (xi + ő∑)] (6) That is to say that, for a given input xi, if we minimize the difference between the softmax outputs of the teacher and the student (fŌÜ and főł) corresponding to the correct label yi, we will maximize Equation 5 for the student. However, to ensure that the student has a non-trivial certiÔ¨Āed radius, we must also ensure that Equation 3 is satisÔ¨Āed. If we assume that Equation 3 holds true for the teacher (i.e., the base classiÔ¨Āer of a certiÔ¨Āably robust smooth classiÔ¨Āer), this condition can also be achieved for the student by matching the overall softmax output of the student to that of the teacher. 3.2 CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT) Based on the previous discussion, we now describe our method for training a certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āer through knowledge transfer. First, we obtain a pre-trained base classiÔ¨Āer fŌÜ, which has been trained using a randomized smoothing based robustness training method as this maximizes 1If no pre-trained classiÔ¨Āer is available, we Ô¨Ārst train an architecture of lower complexity (i.e., fast to train) compared to the target architecture (Section 5.1). 4Algorithm 1 CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT) 1: Input: Training data distribution D, certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher base classiÔ¨Āer fŌÜ, noise level ŌÉ, total training iterations T, learning rate őĪ 2: Output: CertiÔ¨Āably robust student base classiÔ¨Āer főł 3: őł‚Üźrandom initialization 4: i‚Üź0 5: while i< T do 6: From D, sample a batch of inputs {x1,x2,¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,xn}. 7: From N(0,ŌÉ2I), generate a batch of Gaussian noise samples {ő∑1,ő∑2,¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,ő∑n}. 8: li ‚Üź1 n ‚ąĎn j=1 ‚ą•zŌÜ(xj + ő∑j) ‚ąízőł(xj + ő∑j)‚ą•2 9: őł‚Üźőł‚ąíőĪ¬∑‚ąáőłli 10: i‚Üźi+ 1 11: end while Eő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑)]. Next, we use fŌÜ as a teacher to train a new student base classiÔ¨Āer főł. The student is trained to match the output of the teacher. In doing so, we can maximize the certiÔ¨Āed radius of the associated smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł (Equation 6), as well as ensure that Equation 3 is satisÔ¨Āed. We describe our implementation in Algorithm 1. Given a batch of inputs, we Ô¨Ārst perturb them with additive Gaussian noise. Next, we compute the ‚Ąď2 distance between the student and the teacher‚Äôs outputs for these Gaussian perturbed inputs. This distance serves as our loss function, and we update the parameters of the student to minimize this loss. At test time, the classiÔ¨Āer főł is converted to its smooth version gőł following DeÔ¨Ānition 2.1. 3.3 Prior Works on Robustness Transfer Several prior works have examined transferring adversarial robustness between classiÔ¨Āers, but these works have been limited to transferring empirical rather than certiÔ¨Āed robustness [2, 8, 14, 42, 43]. Of note is the work by Goldblum et al. [8] in which they combine adversarial training [ 25] with knowledge distillation [13]. They show that distilling knowledge from a large network to a small network improves its empirical robustness as compared to training the small network on its own, but their method makes no effort to improve the computational cost of adversarial training. 4 Evaluation Our goal is to improve the usability of randomized smoothing based robustness training methods. In this section, we demonstrate how CRT enables the reuse of an existing certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āer to train new certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers at signiÔ¨Ācantly reduced training cost compared to prior methods. In our Ô¨Ārst experiment, we train a ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer with a state-of-the-art method, i.e., SmoothMix [15]), and use CRT to transfer its robustness to train several newer generation classiÔ¨Āers. In a second experiment, we recursively use CRT to train a newer generation classiÔ¨Āer using the previous generation classiÔ¨Āer that was also trained using CRT. In each experiment, we compare the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of classiÔ¨Āer trained using CRT against a classiÔ¨Āer trained using SmoothMix (Section 4.1). We Ô¨Ānd that classiÔ¨Āers trained using CRT are similarly robust as when trained using SmoothMix but only require a fraction of training time (Section 4.2). Our main results are generated using the CIFAR-10 dataset [18], but we also demonstrate the effectiveness of CRT on ImageNet [5] (Section 5.3). Both these datasets are open-source and free for non-commercial use. Architectures. We use several popular DNN architectures that were proposed to either improve upon the visual recognition performance of the previous generation architectures or preserve performance while requiring signiÔ¨Ācantly fewer parameters (or both). For the CIFAR-10 experiments, we use ResNet110 [11], ResNeXt29-2x64d [36], DLA [38], and RegNetX_200MF [27].2 Training details. All SmoothMix classiÔ¨Āers were trained using the code made available by the authors3 and the hyperparameters reported by them [ 15]. All CRT classiÔ¨Āers were trained using Stochastic Gradient Descent till convergence (200 epochs), with a batch size of 128. Further hyperparameter details are available in Appendix C.1. Unless speciÔ¨Āed, we report results for noise 2We use the CIFAR-10 version of these architectures, code [MIT License]: https://github.com/ kuangliu/pytorch-cifar. 3SmoothMix code [MIT License]: https://github.com/jh-jeong/smoothmix 5level ŌÉ = 0.25 in the main paper. Additional results for higher noise levels ŌÉ = 0.5 and 1.0 are reported in Appendix B.1. Evaluation Metrics. We report our results using two metrics. First, as done in prior work, we measure the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of a classiÔ¨Āer based on (1) the certiÔ¨Āed test accuracy at ‚Ąď2 radius r4, which is deÔ¨Āned as the fraction of test set inputs that the smooth classiÔ¨Āer classiÔ¨Āes correctly within an ‚Ąď2 ball of radius rcentered at each input, and (2) average certiÔ¨Āed radius (ACR), which is the average of the certiÔ¨Āed radius across all inputs in the test set: ACR(gőł) = 1 ntest ntest‚ąĎ i=1 CR(gőł; xi,yi) On CIFAR-10, we compute these metrics using the entire test set. Second, we measure training time of a classiÔ¨Āer based on the per-epoch time and total training time. The total training time is computed once the model‚Äôs loss has converged. All classiÔ¨Āers were trained on the same machine with a single Nvidia Titan V GPU. 4.1 CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Comparison Standard CRT Training. Given a ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer trained using SmoothMix, we transfer its robustness to several newer generation classiÔ¨Āers. We compare the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of these classiÔ¨Āers with their SmoothMix trained versions. The results are summarized in Table 2. We observe that using CRT does not reduce the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of the trained classiÔ¨Āer compared to training with SmoothMix. In fact, interestingly, CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers exhibit higher certiÔ¨Āed robustness compared to their SmoothMix baseline. Not only do CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers have higher ACR (improvement of 8.1% in the best case), they also exhibit higher certiÔ¨Āed accuracy at different‚Ąď2 radii. Furthermore, CRT remains effective even as the generation gap between the student and the teacher increases. This implies that the same teacher can potentially be reused indeÔ¨Ānitely, amortizing the teacher‚Äôs training cost to a constant. These results empirically validate our theoretical justiÔ¨Ācation of CRT. Finally, we note that in Table 2, the accuracy on clean inputs and ACR of CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers follow the same trend as in Figure 1, thus motivating the need for periodic model re-deployment to incorporate architectural improvements. We observe that the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of the resulting classiÔ¨Āers remains high even with recursive use of CRT. The empirical results are to be expected given our theoretical understanding of CRT: In order to train a robust student, we only require that the teacher is already robust ( i.e., satisÔ¨Āes the condition of Theorem 2.2) irrespective of the training method used to achieve robustness. Thus, we expect CRT to remain effective even at longer chain lengths. In Section 5.2, we will highlight the relationship between the teacher‚Äôs training method and the robustness of a CRT trained student. 4.2 Training Time Comparison Having established that CRT effectively transfers certiÔ¨Āed robustness between classiÔ¨Āers, we now evaluate its training overhead. For comparison, we also evaluate the training overhead of SmoothMix. In Table 4, we report the per-epoch time and total time of training different architectures with each method. For brevity, we only compare the training time for standard CRT.5 We observe that the per- epoch time of CRT is signiÔ¨Ācantly lower than SmoothMix. Similarly, when trained until convergence, the total training time of CRT is signiÔ¨Ācantly lower. Across the three architectures that we run our experiments on, CRT achieves an average epoch time speedup of 10.75√ó. Comparing overall training times, CRT speeds up training by, on average, 8.06√ó. If we consider the real-world scenario where the model has to be periodically redeployed to incorporate architectural improvements, the cumulative training time using SmoothMix is 96.21 hours as each new architecture is trained from scratch. With CRT, the cumulative time is reduced to 11.70 hours representing a 87.84% savings in costs associated with computational resources. Teacher‚Äôs availability.So far, we assumed the availability of a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher (ResNet110). We argue that this is a reasonable assumption as the amortized cost associated with the one-time training of a robust teacher is negligible across many generations of the model. Regardless, in Section 5.1, we examine a scenario where the teacher is unavailable. Under this scenario, we demonstrate how CRT can be used to speedup the training of ResNet110 for use as teacher. The section layout is as follows: in Section 5.1, we discuss the scenario in which a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher is not readily available and demonstrate how CRT can still speed up robustness training; in Section 5.2, we examine how the method used to train the teacher affects the robustness of the student; in Section 5.3, we study the scalability of CRT using the ImageNet dataset; in Section 5.4, we compare CRT with a closely related prior work on fast certiÔ¨Āed robustness training, i.e., Consistency regularization [16]; in Section 5.5, we discuss the limitations of CRT; in Section 5.6, we address the broader impact of CRT. 5.1 Teacher Not Available We‚Äôve designed CRT under the assumption that a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher is already available. However, even if a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher is not available, CRT can still speed up training. Given a certiÔ¨Āable robust training method and a large network architecture, we can reduce the training overhead by robustly training a comparatively smaller network Ô¨Ārst. Then, we can use CRT to transfer the robustness of the small network to a larger network. In Table 5, we present results for such a process. First, we trained a ResNet20 network using SmoothMix, then we used CRT to train a ResNet110 network. We compare the robustness of a ResNet110 trained using this process with one trained using SmoothMix. As we can see, the CRT ResNet110 network has comparable robustness with the SmoothMix ResNet110 network. However, even when adding the teacher and student training times, CRT still speeds up training by approximately 2√órelative to SmoothMix. For MACER and SmoothAdv training, we use the code made available by the authors67 and the hyperpa- rameters reported by them. Using CRT, we transfer the robustness of each teacher to a ResNet110 clas- siÔ¨Āer. The results are reported in Table 6. For reference, we also report robustness of a ResNet110 net- work trained independently using the chosen robustness training methods. Overall, we observe a slight variation in the robustness of the CRT trained networks depending on the teachers training method. Based on Equation 6, this is expected as maximizing the teacher‚Äôs performance will in turn maximize the performance of the student. Our empirical results align with this reasoning: the MACER teacher was the least robust of the three methods, and its student is similarly the least robust of the students. However, in all cases, the CRT trained network obtained certiÔ¨Āed robustness comparable to its teacher. 5.3 Scalability Here, we study the effectiveness of CRT on a large-scale dataset, i.e., ImageNet. For this purpose, we train ResNet18 classiÔ¨Āers using three certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods (MACER, SmoothAdv, and SmoothMix). Next, we transfer their robustness to a ResNet50 classiÔ¨Āer. The results were generated on a 500 sample test set (following prior works [29, 39, 15]) and are summarized in Table 7. For reference, we also report robustness of a ResNet50 network trained independently using the chosen robustness training methods. In all cases, we observe that students achieve certiÔ¨Āed robustness comparable to their respective teachers. However, another closely related work was recently published by Jeong & Shin [ 16], which shows state-of-the-art ACR and potential training time improvements. They proposed a consistency regularization loss that improves the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of smooth classiÔ¨Āers by enforcing the base classiÔ¨Āer‚Äôs soft outputs to be consistent across multiple noisy copies of a given input. Therefore, their additional computational overhead scales linearly with the number of noisy samples used to compute the consistency loss. With respect to computational overhead, CRT adds only one forward pass,i.e., the pass through the teacher. When paired with Gaussian data augmentation training, their regularization loss signiÔ¨Ācantly improves the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of a smooth classiÔ¨Āer. By applying their regularization loss over only two noisy copies of the input, they can achieve better certiÔ¨Āed robustness than prior state-of-the- art robustness training methods like MACER [39] and SmoothAdv [29]. The key difference between CRT and consistency regularization is in the training overhead when combined with other state-of-the-art certiÔ¨Āed training methods. Consistency regularization augments classiÔ¨Āer training with an additional loss term. Therefore, their training overhead is dominated by the training method selected. In their experiments, they focused on Gaussian data augmentation, which adds little to no training overhead relative to standard training. However, if a more computationally intensive method was selected (e.g., MACER), they remark their training overhead would dramatically increase. With respect to CRT, if a teacher is available ( i.e., a previous generation model), the overhead of CRT is agnostic to the training method. If it is not available, we demonstrated in Section 5.1, that CRT can still greatly reduce training overhead. For interested readers, we include results for transferring robustness from a teacher trained using Consistency regularization in Appendix B.4. 95.5 Limitations In this paper, we use probabilistic certiÔ¨Āed robustness methods as they rely on Theorem 2.2 and, thus, are designed to maximize the certiÔ¨Āed radius (Equation 4). We found that deterministic methods (e.g., CROWN-IBP [41]) impose a stricter training requirement on the base teacher classiÔ¨Āer. For a given input, deterministic training methods require the base classiÔ¨Āer to be correct for all inputs within the ‚Ąď2-norm ball, rather than simply be likely to correctly classify inputs within the ‚Ąď2-norm ball. This restriction lowers the potential ACR of the smooth teacher classiÔ¨Āer, which also lowers the ACR of the student trained using CRT. For example, when using CROWN-IBP [41] to train a ResNeXt base classiÔ¨Āer, the ACR for the corresponding smooth classiÔ¨Āer is only 0.064. When transferring the robustness of this ResNeXt classiÔ¨Āer to a WideResNet34-10 student, we get an ACR of 0.065. Additionally, we note that the classiÔ¨Āer architectures we present in the paper are restricted to CNNs. Recently, a new class of transformer-based image classiÔ¨Āers [6, 24, 4] have been proposed that show improved performance over CNN classiÔ¨Āers. We brieÔ¨āy studied the effectiveness of CRT when transferring robustness between CNN and transformer architectures using ViT [6] and present the results in Appendix B.2, but further exploration is needed. Finally, CRT has only been studied using the ‚Ąď2 norm and image data due to the limitations of current certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods. 5.6 Broader Impacts As we have shown, our work improves the efÔ¨Āciency of training certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers, in an effort to improve the security of AI-powered systems. Beyond the broad negative societal impacts of machine learning, we are not aware of any impacts speciÔ¨Āc to our work. 6 Conclusion In this paper, we proposed the Ô¨Ārst general-purpose framework to speed up the training of certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers using knowledge transfer and randomized smoothing. Our proposed method, CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT) enables transferring the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of a classiÔ¨Āer to another classiÔ¨Āer at a cost comparable to standard training. We provided a theoretical understanding of CRT and provided empirical evidence of its effectiveness. On CIFAR-10, we showed that across several generations of classiÔ¨Āer architectures, CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers 8√ófaster than when using a state-of-the-art training method, while achieving comparable or better certiÔ¨Āed robustness. Furthermore, CRT can reduce the training overhead of certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods even when an initial robust classiÔ¨Āer is not present. The use of machine learning in security and safety critical environments motivates a need for models with certiÔ¨Āably robust performance, but the training overhead of existing certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods inhibits their usability. Our work addresses this issue, especially for commercial applications where periodical model re-deployment is inevitable. [N/A] 13A JustiÔ¨Ācation for CRT Training Objective Following from the discussion in Section 3.1, we want to maximize Eő∑[zy őł(x+ ő∑)]. Given a non- negative random variable t= Eő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑)], we have Eő∑[zy őł(x+ ő∑)] ‚Č•Eő∑[zy őł(x+ ő∑)] ‚ąít Here zŌÜ is the soft output associated with classiÔ¨Āer fŌÜ that has been trained independently of főł. Therefore, we have Eő∑[zy őł(x+ ő∑)] ‚Č•Eő∑[zy őł(x+ ő∑)] ‚ąíEő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑)] ‚Č•Eő∑[zy őł(x+ ő∑) ‚ąízy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑)] ‚Č•‚ąíEő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑) ‚ąízy őł(x+ ő∑)] This implies that maximizing Eő∑[zy őł(x+ ő∑)] is equivalent to minimizing Eő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑) ‚ąízy őł(x+ ő∑)]. B Additional Results B.1 Higher Noise Level In the main paper, we conduct experiments on CIFAR-10 using noise levelŌÉ= 0.25 only. Here, we report our main set of results on CIFAR-10 (Table 3) using higher ŌÉvalues. In Table 8, we report results using ŌÉ= 0.5 and in Table 9, we report results using ŌÉ= 1.0. B.2 Using ViT [6] In the main paper, we used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based architectures. However, there is another recently developed class of architectures that use Transformers for the task of image classiÔ¨Ācation [ 6, 24, 4]. In Table 10, we present results measuring the effectiveness of CRT in transferring robustness from ResNet110 (a CNN-based classiÔ¨Āer) to ViT [6] (a Transformer-based classiÔ¨Āer). For comparison, we also report results obtained on training ViT with SmoothMix. CRT trained ViT classiÔ¨Āers perform comparable or better than their SmoothMix counterparts. B.3 Training SmoothMix ClassiÔ¨Āers with CRT Hyperparameters For generating results using prior methods, we strictly adhere to the hyperparameters reported by them. However, the hyperparameters that we use for CRT training is different than the ones used by prior methods (see Table 16). In Table 11, we report results obtained on using CRT training hyperparameters with SmoothMix. We note that there is not a signiÔ¨Ācant difference in the robustness achieved using the two sets of hyperparameters. B.4 Training Teacher with Consistency Regularization [16] For our main set of results on CIFAR-10, we focused on SmoothMix. In Section 5.4, we discussed Consistency Regularization recently proposed by Jeong & Shin [ 16] as another method to attain certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers with at a cost comparable to standard training depending on the setting. Here, we show results when Consistency Regularization is used to train the teacher classiÔ¨Āer in Tables 12 and 13. For comparison, in Table 12, we also report the results for training the classiÔ¨Āers using Consistency Regularization. As with other training methods, CRT is effective in transferring the robustness of the teacher classiÔ¨Āer irrespective of the training method. B.5 ImageNet Results In Table 14, we present an additional ImageNet result using a different student-teacher pair. We note that, as in Table 7, CRT remains effective. B.6 Gaussian Data Augmentation Baseline Along with the theoretical framework for creating certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers using random- ized smoothing, Cohen et al. [3] also proposed a simple yet effective method for training classiÔ¨Āers with high certiÔ¨Āed robustness within this framework. This method involves training the base classi- Ô¨Āer with Gaussian data augmentation. To date, this method remains the fastest way to train classi- Ô¨Āers with non-trivial certiÔ¨Āed robustness using the randomized smoothing framework. However, this method is not as sophisticated as the more recently proposed methods, and so yields much poorer cer- tiÔ¨Āed robustness than them. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Certified Robustness Transfer (CRT), a knowledge-transfer framework that accelerates training of l2-certifiably robust classifiers (via randomized smoothing) by distilling robustness from a robust teacher to a student. Proposes a simple loss that matches the teacher‚Äôs and student‚Äôs soft outputs on Gaussian-perturbed inputs, with theoretical justification showing this maximizes the student‚Äôs certified radius independent of the teacher‚Äôs training method. Empirically, on CIFAR-10 CRT achieves ~8√ó average training speedup over SmoothMix while maintaining or improving certified robustness (up to ~8% ACR gain), supports recursive reuse across architecture generations, reduces cumulative training time by ~87.84% over three redeployments, and scales to ImageNet with comparable ACR to teachers.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Foundations: Uses randomized smoothing (Cohen et al.) to define a smoothed classifier gőł and its l2 certified radius CR via Gaussian noise N(0,ŌÉ^2I).
- Key idea: To maximize Eő∑[z_y^őł(x+ő∑)], derive a lower bound showing that minimizing the gap Eő∑[z_y^ŌÜ(x+ő∑)‚ąíz_y^őł(x+ő∑)] increases the student‚Äôs probability mass on the true class, thus increasing its certified radius (and aligning with the sufficient condition for certification).
- CRT training objective: For each batch, sample Gaussian noise ő∑~N(0,ŌÉ^2I) and minimize L2 distance between teacher and student softmax outputs on noisy inputs: L = (1/n)‚ąĎ||z_ŌÜ(x+ő∑)‚ąíz_őł(x+ő∑)||_2. This encourages the student to match the teacher‚Äôs robust predictions.
- Algorithm: Initialize student; for T iterations sample data and noise; compute loss as above; update őł via SGD; at test time, apply randomized smoothing (Monte Carlo) to certify gőł.
- Generality: Independent of how the teacher was trained (e.g., SmoothAdv, MACER, SmoothMix, Consistency regularization), and can be applied recursively across generations. When no teacher exists, first train a smaller model robustly, then transfer to a larger target.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: CIFAR-10 (primary), ImageNet (scalability; 500-sample test set, following prior works).
- Architectures: CIFAR-10: ResNet110 (teacher), ResNeXt29-2x64d, DLA, RegNetX_200MF; also explored ResNet20‚ÜíResNet110 (when teacher absent) and limited ViT-B/16 transfer. ImageNet: ResNet18‚ÜíResNet50, and ResNet50‚ÜíRegNetX-4.0G.
- Baselines/teacher training: Randomized smoothing training methods: SmoothMix (state-of-the-art), MACER, SmoothAdv; also tested Consistency Regularization and Gaussian data augmentation baseline.
- Training details: CIFAR-10 CRT uses SGD, batch size 128, 200 epochs; ŌÉ=0.25 (main), with additional results for ŌÉ=0.5 and ŌÉ=1.0. SmoothMix/MACER/SmoothAdv use authors‚Äô code/hyperparameters. Timing measured on a single Nvidia Titan V GPU; per-epoch and total time to convergence reported.
- Metrics: Certified test accuracy at l2 radius r and Average Certified Radius (ACR). On CIFAR-10, certification over full test set; on ImageNet, 500 samples.
- Key results: On CIFAR-10, CRT students match or exceed SmoothMix robustness (e.g., ACR improvements up to ~8.1%) while reducing per-epoch time by ~10.75√ó and total training time by ~8.06√ó on average across architectures; cumulative training time reduced by ~87.84% across three redeployments. Recursive CRT maintains robustness across generations. When teacher absent, training small robust teacher (ResNet20) then transferring to ResNet110 yields comparable ACR with ~2√ó total time speedup. On ImageNet, CRT students achieve ACR comparable to their respective teachers across MACER, SmoothAdv, and SmoothMix.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Scope limited to probabilistic certification via randomized smoothing in l2; other norms (e.g., l‚ąě) and modalities beyond images were not studied.
- Deterministic certified training (e.g., CROWN-IBP) produced low ACR teachers, leading to low-ACR students under CRT, indicating reduced effectiveness under such teachers.
- Architectural coverage mainly CNNs; transformer-based models (e.g., ViT) only briefly explored.
- Student robustness can depend on teacher robustness and training method; weaker teachers yield weaker students.
- Full compute accounting not reported for all experiments; ImageNet certification uses a 500-sample subset rather than the full validation set.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend CRT to deterministic certification frameworks and other threat models/norms (e.g., l‚ąě, lp families), and to non-image domains (NLP, audio, tabular).
- Broaden architectural coverage (transformers, hybrid CNN-Transformer, larger foundation models) and study cross-paradigm transfer more deeply.
- Develop theoretical bounds on robustness transfer fidelity (e.g., relation between teacher ACR and student ACR under CRT) and optimality of loss choices (KL vs L2, temperature scaling).
- Explore multi-teacher/ensemble CRT, semi-supervised CRT, and domain adaptation/shift-aware CRT.
- Automate ŌÉ selection and adaptive noise strategies during transfer; analyze robustness-accuracy trade-offs.
- Improve certification efficiency at scale (full ImageNet certification) and integrate CRT with other fast regularizers (e.g., consistency regularization) without compounding overhead.
- Investigate robustness transfer under different data regimes (few-shot, continual learning) and for privacy-preserving or federated settings.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# rs/core.py
'''
Randomized Smoothing: Hard-RS and Soft-RS
Soft-RS uses empirical Bernstein bound

MACER: Attack-free and Scalable Robust Training via Maximizing Certified Radius
ICLR 2020 Submission

References:
[1] J. Cohen, E. Rosenfeld and Z. Kolter. 
Certified Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing. In ICML, 2019.

Acknowledgements:
[1] https://github.com/locuslab/smoothing/blob/master/code/core.py
'''

from math import ceil

import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


class Smooth(object):
  '''
  Smoothed classifier
  mode can be hard, soft or both
  beta is the inverse of softmax temperature
  '''

  # to abstain, Smooth returns this int
  ABSTAIN = -1

  def __init__(self, base_classifier: torch.nn.Module, num_classes: int,
               sigma: float, device, mode='hard', beta=1.0):
    self.base_classifier = base_classifier
    self.num_classes = num_classes
    self.sigma = sigma
    self.device = device
    self.mode = mode
    self.square_sum = None
    self.ss = 0
    self.beta = beta

  def certify(self, x: torch.tensor, n0: int, n: int, alpha: float, batch_size: int) -> (int, float):
    if self.mode == 'both':
      c_hard, c_soft = self.predict(x, n0, batch_size)
      o_hard, o_soft = self._sample_noise(x, n, batch_size)
      na_hard, na_soft = o_hard[c_hard].item(), o_soft[c_soft].item()
      self.ss = self.square_sum[c_soft]
      pa_hard = self._lower_confidence_bound(na_hard, n, alpha, 'hard')
      pa_soft = self._lower_confidence_bound(na_soft, n, alpha, 'soft')
      r_hard = 0.0
      r_soft = 0.0
      if pa_hard < 0.5:
        c_hard = Smooth.ABSTAIN
      else:
        r_hard = self.sigma * norm.ppf(pa_hard)
      if pa_soft < 0.5:
        c_soft = Smooth.ABSTAIN
      else:
        r_soft = self.sigma * norm.ppf(pa_soft)
      return c_hard, r_hard, c_soft, r_soft
    else:
      # make an initial prediction of the label
      cAHat = self.predict(x, n0, batch_size)
      # draw more samples of f(x + epsilon)
      observation = self._sample_noise(x, n, batch_size)
      # use these samples to estimate a lower bound on pA
      nA = observation[cAHat].item()
      if self.mode == 'soft':
        self.ss = self.square_sum[cAHat]
      pABar = self._lower_confidence_bound(nA, n, alpha, self.mode)
      if pABar < 0.5:
        return Smooth.ABSTAIN, 0.0
      else:
        radius = self.sigma * norm.ppf(pABar)
        return cAHat, radius

  def predict(self, x: torch.tensor, n: int, batch_size: int) -> int:
    self.base_classifier.eval()
    if self.mode == 'both':
      result_hard, result_soft = self._sample_noise(x, n, batch_size)
      return result_hard.argsort()[::-1][0], result_soft.argsort()[::-1][0]
    else:
      result = self._sample_noise(x, n, batch_size)
      return result.argsort()[::-1][0]

  def _sample_noise(self, x: torch.tensor, num: int, batch_size) -> np.ndarray:
    with torch.no_grad():
      result_hard = np.zeros(self.num_classes, dtype=int)
      result_soft = np.zeros(self.num_classes, dtype=float)
      self.square_sum = np.zeros(self.num_classes, dtype=float)
      for _ in range(ceil(num / batch_size)):
        this_batch_size = min(batch_size, num)
        num -= this_batch_size

        batch = x.repeat((this_batch_size, 1, 1, 1))
        noise = torch.randn_like(batch, device=self.device) * self.sigma
        predictions = self.base_classifier(batch + noise)
        predictions *= self.beta
        if self.mode == 'hard' or self.mode == 'both':
          p_hard = predictions.argmax(1)
          result_hard += self._count_arr(p_hard.cpu().numpy(),
                                         self.num_classes)
        if self.mode == 'soft' or self.mode == 'both':
          p_soft = F.softmax(predictions, 1)
          p_soft_square = p_soft ** 2
          p_soft = p_soft.sum(0)
          p_soft_square = p_soft_square.sum(0)
          result_soft += p_soft.cpu().numpy()
          self.square_sum += p_soft_square.cpu().numpy()
      if self.mode == 'hard':
        return result_hard
      if self.mode == 'soft':
        return result_soft
      else:
        return result_hard, result_soft

  def _count_arr(self, arr: np.ndarray, length: int) -> np.ndarray:
    counts = np.zeros(length, dtype=int)
    for idx in arr:
      counts[idx] += 1
    return counts

  def _lower_confidence_bound(self, NA, N, alpha: float, mode) -> float:
    if mode == 'hard':
      return proportion_confint(NA, N, alpha=2 * alpha, method="beta")[0]
    else:
      sample_variance = (self.ss - NA * NA / N) / (N - 1)
      if sample_variance < 0:
        sample_variance = 0
      t = np.log(2 / alpha)
      return NA / N - np.sqrt(2 * sample_variance * t / N) - 7 * t / 3 / (N - 1)


# rs/certify.py
'''
Randomized Smoothing Certification: Hard-RS and Soft-RS
Soft-RS uses empirical Bernstein bound

MACER: Attack-free and Scalable Robust Training via Maximizing Certified Radius
ICLR 2020 Submission

References:
[1] J. Cohen, E. Rosenfeld and Z. Kolter. 
Certified Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing. In ICML, 2019.

Acknowledgements:
[1] https://github.com/locuslab/smoothing/blob/master/code/certify.py
'''

import numpy as np
from PIL import Image
import scipy.io as sio

from rs.core import Smooth


def certify(model, device, dataset, transform, num_classes, matfile=None,
            mode='hard', start_img=0, num_img=500, skip=1, sigma=0.25, N0=100, N=100000,
            alpha=0.001, batch=1000, verbose=False,
            grid=(0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.25), beta=1.0):
  print('===certify(N={}, sigma={}, mode={})==='.format(N, sigma, mode))

  model.eval()
  smoothed_net = Smooth(model, num_classes,
                        sigma, device, mode, beta)

  radius_hard = np.zeros((num_img,), dtype=np.float)
  radius_soft = np.zeros((num_img,), dtype=np.float)
  num_grid = len(grid)
  cnt_grid_hard = np.zeros((num_grid + 1,), dtype=np.int)
  cnt_grid_soft = np.zeros((num_grid + 1,), dtype=np.int)
  s_hard = 0.0
  s_soft = 0.0

  for i in range(num_img):
    img, target = dataset[start_img + i * skip]
    img = img.to(device)

    if mode == 'both':
      p_hard, r_hard, p_soft, r_soft = smoothed_net.certify(
          img, N0, N, alpha, batch)
      correct_hard = int(p_hard == target)
      correct_soft = int(p_soft == target)
      if verbose:
        if correct_hard == 1:
          print('Hard Correct: 1. Radius: {}.'.format(r_hard))
        else:
          print('Hard Correct: 0.')
        if correct_soft == 1:
          print('Soft Correct: 1. Radius: {}.'.format(r_soft))
        else:
          print('Soft Correct: 0.')
      radius_hard[i] = r_hard if correct_hard == 1 else -1
      radius_soft[i] = r_soft if correct_soft == 1 else -1
      if correct_hard == 1:
        cnt_grid_hard[0] += 1
        s_hard += r_hard
        for j in range(num_grid):
          if r_hard >= grid[j]:
            cnt_grid_hard[j + 1] += 1
      if correct_soft == 1:
        cnt_grid_soft[0] += 1
        s_soft += r_soft
        for j in range(num_grid):
          if r_soft >= grid[j]:
            cnt_grid_soft[j + 1] += 1
    else:
      prediction, radius = smoothed_net.certify(img, N0, N, alpha, batch)
      correct = int(prediction == target)
      if verbose:
        if correct == 1:
          print('Correct: 1. Radius: {}.'.format(radius))
        else:
          print('Correct: 0.')
      radius_hard[i] = radius if correct == 1 else -1
      if correct == 1:
        cnt_grid_hard[0] += 1
        s_hard += radius
        for j in range(num_grid):
          if radius >= grid[j]:
            cnt_grid_hard[j + 1] += 1

  print('===Certify Summary===')
  print('Total Image Number: {}'.format(num_img))
  if mode == 'both':
    print('===Hard certify===')
    print('Radius: 0.0  Number: {}  Acc: {}'.format(
        cnt_grid_hard[0], cnt_grid_hard[0] / num_img * 100))
    for j in range(num_grid):
      print('Radius: {}  Number: {}  Acc: {}'.format(
          grid[j], cnt_grid_hard[j + 1], cnt_grid_hard[j + 1] / num_img * 100))
    print('ACR: {}'.format(s_hard / num_img))
    print('===Soft certify===')
    print('Radius: 0.0  Number: {}  Acc: {}'.format(
        cnt_grid_soft[0], cnt_grid_soft[0] / num_img * 100))
    for j in range(num_grid):
      print('Radius: {}  Number: {}  Acc: {}'.format(
          grid[j], cnt_grid_soft[j + 1], cnt_grid_soft[j + 1] / num_img * 100))
    print('ACR: {}'.format(s_soft / num_img))
    if matfile is not None:
      sio.savemat(matfile, {'hard': radius_hard, 'soft': radius_soft})
  else:
    print('Radius: 0.0  Number: {}  Acc: {}'.format(
        cnt_grid_hard[0], cnt_grid_hard[0] / num_img * 100))
    for j in range(num_grid):
      print('Radius: {}  Number: {}  Acc: {}'.format(
          grid[j], cnt_grid_hard[j + 1], cnt_grid_hard[j + 1] / num_img * 100))
    print('ACR: {}'.format(s_hard / num_img))
    if matfile is not None:
      sio.savemat(matfile, {mode: radius_hard})


# macer.py (noise sampling and RS-relevant forward path during training)
'''
MACER Algorithm

MACER: Attack-free and Scalable Robust Training via Maximizing Certified Radius
ICLR 2020 Submission
'''

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.distributions.normal import Normal

from rs.certify import certify


def macer_train(sigma, lbd, gauss_num, beta, gamma, num_classes,
                    model, trainloader, optimizer, device):
  m = Normal(torch.tensor([0.0]).to(device),
             torch.tensor([1.0]).to(device))

  cl_total = 0.0
  rl_total = 0.0
  input_total = 0

  for _, (inputs, targets) in enumerate(trainloader):
    inputs, targets = inputs.to(device), targets.to(device)
    input_size = len(inputs)
    input_total += input_size

    new_shape = [input_size * gauss_num]
    new_shape.extend(inputs[0].shape)
    inputs = inputs.repeat((1, gauss_num, 1, 1)).view(new_shape)
    noise = torch.randn_like(inputs, device=device) * sigma
    noisy_inputs = inputs + noise

    outputs = model(noisy_inputs)
    outputs = outputs.reshape((input_size, gauss_num, num_classes))

    # Classification loss
    outputs_softmax = F.softmax(outputs, dim=2).mean(1)
    outputs_logsoftmax = torch.log(outputs_softmax + 1e-10)  # avoid nan
    classification_loss = F.nll_loss(
        outputs_logsoftmax, targets, reduction='sum')
    cl_total += classification_loss.item()

    # Robustness loss
    beta_outputs = outputs * beta  # only apply beta to the robustness loss
    beta_outputs_softmax = F.softmax(beta_outputs, dim=2).mean(1)
    top2 = torch.topk(beta_outputs_softmax, 2)
    top2_score = top2[0]
    top2_idx = top2[1]
    indices_correct = (top2_idx[:, 0] == targets)  # G_theta

    out0, out1 = top2_score[indices_correct,
                            0], top2_score[indices_correct, 1]
    robustness_loss = m.icdf(out1) - m.icdf(out0)
    indices = ~torch.isnan(robustness_loss) & ~torch.isinf(
        robustness_loss) & (torch.abs(robustness_loss) <= gamma)  # hinge
    out0, out1 = out0[indices], out1[indices]
    robustness_loss = m.icdf(out1) - m.icdf(out0) + gamma
    robustness_loss = robustness_loss.sum() * sigma / 2
    rl_total += robustness_loss.item()

    # Final objective function
    loss = classification_loss + lbd * robustness_loss
    loss /= input_size
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

  cl_total /= input_total
  rl_total /= input_total
  print('Classification Loss: {}  Robustness Loss: {}'.format(cl_total, rl_total))
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
Core method components present in the repository
- Randomized smoothing foundations and certification: Implemented in rs/core.py (Smooth class) and rs/certify.py (certify function). These implement Monte Carlo sampling with Gaussian noise N(0, ŌÉ^2 I), hard and soft counting, and certified radius computation r = ŌÉ ő¶^{-1}(p_lower).
- Noise injection during training: macer_train in macer.py shows how each batch is expanded by gauss_num Gaussian samples per input and forwarded through the model; softmax temperature scaling (beta) is applied before aggregating predictions. Although the loss here is the MACER objective (not teacher-student), the Gaussian noise sampling and aggregation align with the described smoothing framework and can be reused for CRT-style objectives.

Experimental settings (defaults from main.py and rs/certify.py)
- Datasets and models:
  - MNIST: LeNet
  - CIFAR-10: ResNet-110 (ResNet_Cifar with BasicBlock [18,18,18])
  - SVHN: ResNet-110
- Data preprocessing:
  - MNIST: transforms.ToTensor() for train/test
  - CIFAR-10 & SVHN (train): RandomCrop(32, padding=4), RandomHorizontalFlip(), ToTensor()
  - CIFAR-10 & SVHN (test): ToTensor()
- Training hyperparameters:
  - epochs: 440
  - batch_size: 64
  - gauss_num (number of Gaussian samples per input during training): 16
  - optimizer: SGD(lr=0.01, momentum=0.9, weight_decay=5e-4)
  - lr scheduler: MultiStepLR with milestones [200, 400], gamma=0.1
  - sigma (Gaussian noise std, also used at test): 0.25
  - beta (inverse softmax temperature, also used at test): 16.0
  - lbd (weight of robustness loss in MACER): 12.0
  - gamma (hinge factor for MACER robustness loss): 8.0
- Certification/evaluation hyperparameters (rs/certify.py):
  - mode: 'hard' by default (also supports 'soft' and 'both')
  - sigma: 0.25
  - beta: 1.0 by default in certify; can be set (main.py passes args.beta)
  - N0 (initial samples for predicting class): 100
  - N (samples for certification): 100000
  - alpha (failure probability): 0.001
  - batch (number of noise samples per pass): 1000
  - start_img: 500, num_img: 500, skip: 1 (evaluation subset control)
  - grid of radii for reporting: (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.25)
- Hardware settings:
  - device: 'cuda' if available; cudnn.benchmark=True when cuda
  - model wrapped by torch.nn.DataParallel when using cuda

Notes for applying the CRT objective with this repository
- Use macer_train‚Äôs Gaussian sampling pattern to form noisy_inputs and per-input stacks of model outputs.
- Replace the MACER loss block with the CRT objective: for a given teacher network ŌÜ and student őł, compute L = (1/n) ő£ ||softmax(z_ŌÜ(x+ő∑)) ‚ąí softmax(z_őł(x+ő∑))||_2 over sampled ő∑ ~ N(0, ŌÉ^2 I), where n is the number of noise samples per input (gauss_num). Update őł via SGD.
- At test time, reuse rs/certify.py and rs/core.py to certify the trained student model using the same ŌÉ and desired mode (hard/soft/both).

research_study_list.title: Accelerating Certified Robustness Training via Knowledge Transfer
research_study_list.abstract:
Training deep neural network classifiers that are certifiably robust against
adversarial attacks is critical to ensuring the security and reliability of
AI-controlled systems. Although numerous state-of-the-art certified training
methods have been developed, they are computationally expensive and scale
poorly with respect to both dataset and network complexity. Widespread usage of
certified training is further hindered by the fact that periodic retraining is
necessary to incorporate new data and network improvements. In this paper, we
propose Certified Robustness Transfer (CRT), a general-purpose framework for
reducing the computational overhead of any certifiably robust training method
through knowledge transfer. Given a robust teacher, our framework uses a novel
training loss to transfer the teacher's robustness to the student. We provide
theoretical and empirical validation of CRT. Our experiments on CIFAR-10 show
that CRT speeds up certified robustness training by $8 \times$ on average
across three different architecture generations while achieving comparable
robustness to state-of-the-art methods. We also show that CRT can scale to
large-scale datasets like ImageNet.
research_study_list.full_text: Accelerating CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Training via Knowledge Transfer Pratik Vaishnavi Stony Brook University pvaishnavi@cs.stonybrook.edu Kevin Eykholt IBM Research kheykholt@ibm.com Amir Rahmati Stony Brook University amir@cs.stonybrook.edu Abstract Training deep neural network classiÔ¨Āers that are certiÔ¨Āably robust against adver- sarial attacks is critical to ensuring the security and reliability of AI-controlled systems. Although numerous state-of-the-art certiÔ¨Āed training methods have been developed, they are computationally expensive and scale poorly with respect to both dataset and network complexity. Widespread usage of certiÔ¨Āed training is fur- ther hindered by the fact that periodic retraining is necessary to incorporate new data and network improvements. In this paper, we propose CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a general-purpose framework for reducing the computational over- head of any certiÔ¨Āably robust training method through knowledge transfer. Given a robust teacher, our framework uses a novel training loss to transfer the teacher‚Äôs robustness to the student. We provide theoretical and empirical validation of CRT. Our experiments on CIFAR-10 show that CRT speeds up certiÔ¨Āed robustness train- ing by 8√óon average across three different architecture generations while achiev- ing comparable robustness to state-of-the-art methods. We also show that CRT can scale to large-scale datasets like ImageNet. 1 Introduction Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are susceptible to adversarial evasion attacks [31, 9], that add a small amount of carefully crafted imperceptible noise to an input to reliably trigger misclassiÔ¨Ācation. As a defense, numerous training methods have been proposed [25, 40, 35] to grant a classiÔ¨Āer empirical robustness. But in the absence of any provable guarantees for this robustness, these defenses were frequently broken [1, 32]. These failures have motivated the development of training methods that grant certiÔ¨Āable/provable robustness to a classiÔ¨Āer, hence safeguarding them against all attacks (known or unknown) within a pre-determined threat model. Such methods are broadly categorized as either deterministic or probabilistic [23]. Deterministic robustness training methods [12, 26, 33, 34, 28, 10, 41, 30] rely on computing provable bounds on the output neurons of a classiÔ¨Āer for a given perturbation budget in the input space. However, the deterministic robustness guarantees provided by these methods come at a high computational cost. Probabilistic robustness training methods address this limitation by providing highly probable (e.g., with 0.99 probability) robustness guarantees at a greatly reduced computational cost. Within this category, randomized smoothing- based methods [19, 3, 29, 22, 20, 7, 37, 39, 16, 15] are considered the state-of-the-art for certiÔ¨Āable robustness in the ‚Ąď2-space. Even so, these training methods remain an order of magnitude slower than standard training. In commercial applications where constant model re-deployment occurs to provide improvements (see Figure 1), re-training using computationally expensive methods is burdensome. 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). arXiv:2210.14283v1  [cs.LG]  25 Oct 2022ResNet101 (2016) ResNeXt101 (2017) DLA-X (2018) RegNetX-16GF (2020) ViT-B/16 (2021) 76 80 84 88Top-1 Accuracy (%)  45 60 75 90 Number of Params (M) Figure 1: Evolution of DNN architectures on the ImageNet dataset. We plot the performance (top-1 accuracy) and the number of parameters of a few popular architectures (year of release is noted in brackets). Newer generations attempt to improve performance and/or reduce network parameters. In this work, we reduce the training overhead of randomized smoothing-based robustness training methods with minimal impact on the robustness achieved. We propose CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a knowledge transfer framework that signiÔ¨Ācantly speeds up the process of training ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers. Given a pre-trained classiÔ¨Āer that is certiÔ¨Āably robust ( i.e., teacher), CRT trains a new classiÔ¨Āer ( i.e., student) that has comparable levels of robustness in a fraction of the time required by state-of-the-art methods. CRT brings down the cost of training certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers to be comparable to standard training while preserving state- of-the-art robustness. On CIFAR-10, CRT speeds up training by an average of 8√óacross three different architecture generations compared to a state-of-the-art robustness training method [ 15]. Furthermore, we show that state-of-the-art robustness training is only necessary to train the initial classiÔ¨Āer. Afterward, CRT can be continuously reused to transfer robustness in order to expedite future model re-deployments and greatly reduce costs associated with computational resources. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: ‚ÄĘ We present CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), the Ô¨Ārst framework, to our knowledge, that can transfer the robustness of a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher classiÔ¨Āer to a new student classiÔ¨Āer. CRT greatly reduces the time required to train certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers relative to existing state-of-the-art methods while achieving comparable or better robustness. ‚ÄĘ We provide a theoretical understanding of CRT, showing how our approach of matching outputs enables robustness transfer between the student and teacher irrespective of the certiÔ¨Āed robustness training method used to train the teacher. ‚ÄĘ On CIFAR-10, we show that CRT trains certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers on average8√ófaster than a state-of-the-art method while having comparable or better Average CertiÔ¨Āed Radius (by 8% in the best case). Furthermore, CRT reduces the cumulative computational cost of training three classiÔ¨Āers by 87.84%. ‚ÄĘ We also show that CRT can be reused in a recursive manner, thus supporting a continuous re- deployment scenario (e.g., in commercial applications). Finally, we show that CRT remains effective on a large-scale dataset, ImageNet. 2 Background In this section, we brieÔ¨āy introduce certiÔ¨Āed robustness and discuss notable existing methods for training certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers using randomized smoothing. 2.1 Preliminaries Problem Setup. Consider a neural network classiÔ¨Āer f parameterized by őł (denoted főł) trained to map a given input x ‚ąą Rd to a set of discrete labels Y using a set of i.i.d. samples S= {(x1,y1),(x1,y1),¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,(xn,yn)}drawn from a data distribution D. The output of the classiÔ¨Āer can be written as főł(x) = arg maxc‚ąąY zc őł(x). Here zőł(x) is the softmax output of the classiÔ¨Āer and zc őł(x) denotes the probability that image xbelongs to class c. 2CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness via Randomized Smoothing. The robustness of the classiÔ¨Āer főł for a given input pair (x,y) is deÔ¨Āned using the radius of the largest ‚Ąď2 ball centered at xwithin which főł has a constant output y. This radius is referred to asrobust radiusand it can mathematically be expressed as: R(főł; x,y) = { inf főł(x‚Ä≤)Őł=főł(x) ‚ą•x‚Ä≤‚ąíx‚ą•2 , when főł(x) = y 0 , when főł(x) Őł= y (1) Within this ‚Ąď2-neighborhood of x, főł is considered to be certiÔ¨Āably robust. Therefore, to improve the robustness of a classiÔ¨Āer, one needs to maximize this robust radius corresponding to any point sampled from the given data distribution. Directly maximizing the robust radius of a DNN classiÔ¨Āer is an NP-hard problem [ 17]. Therefore, several prior works attempt to derive a lower bound for the robust radius [21, 19, 3]. This lower bound, often termed as the certiÔ¨Āed radius, satisÔ¨Āes the following condition: 0 ‚Č§CR(főł; x,y) ‚Č§R(főł; x,y), for any főł, (x, y). In this paper, we utilize the certiÔ¨Āed robustness framework derived by Cohen et al. [3] using randomized smoothing. Given a classiÔ¨Āer főł, they Ô¨Ārst deÔ¨Āne the smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł as: DeÔ¨Ānition 2.1. For a given (base) classiÔ¨Āer főł and ŌÉ >0, the smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł corresponding to főł is deÔ¨Āned as follows: gőł(x) = arg max c‚ąąY Pő∑‚ąľN(0,ŌÉ2I)(főł(x+ ő∑) = c) (2) Simply put, gőł returns the class c, which has the highest probability mass under the Gaussian distribution N(x,ŌÉ2I). Using Theorem 2.2, they proved that if the smooth classiÔ¨Āer correctly classiÔ¨Āes a given input x, it is certiÔ¨Āably robust at x. They also provided an analytical form of the ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radius at x. Theorem 2.2. Let főł : Rd ‚Ü¶‚ÜíY be a classiÔ¨Āer and gőł be its smoothed version (as deÔ¨Āned in DeÔ¨Ānition 2.1). For a given input x ‚ąąRd and corresponding ground truth y ‚ąąY, if gőł correctly classiÔ¨Āes xas y, i.e., Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y) ‚Č•max y‚Ä≤Őł=y Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y‚Ä≤) (3) then gőł is provably robust at xwithin the certiÔ¨Āed radius Rgiven by: CR(gőł; x,y) = ŌÉ 2 [ő¶‚ąí1(Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y)) ‚ąíő¶‚ąí1(max y‚Ä≤Őł=y Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y‚Ä≤))] (4) where ő¶ is the c.d.f. of the standard Gaussian distribution. This certiÔ¨Āed radius is a tight lower bound of the robust radius deÔ¨Āned in Equation 1, i.e., it is impossible to certify gőł at xfor a radius larger than CR. 2.2 Training Methods for Maximizing CertiÔ¨Āed Radius In addition to the theoretical framework discussed above, Cohen et al. [3] also propose a simple yet effective method for training the base classiÔ¨Āer in a way that maximizes the ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radius of the smooth classiÔ¨Āer, as expressed in Equation 4. We include an evaluation of their method in Appendix B.6. Following their work, several other works build upon the randomized smoothing framework and propose training methods that better maximize the ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed radius of the smooth classiÔ¨Āer. Salman et al. [29] proposed combining adversarial training [ 25] with randomized smooth- ing (called SmoothAdv). They adapted the vanilla PGD attack to target the smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł instead of the base classiÔ¨Āer főł. Zhai et al. [39] proposed a new robustness loss, a hinge loss that en- forces maximization of the soft approximation of the certiÔ¨Āed radius. Their method (called MACER) is faster than SmoothAdv as it does not use adversarial training. More recently, Jeong et al. [15] proposed training with a convex combination of samples along the direction of adversarial pertur- bation for each input to regularize over-conÔ¨Ādent predictions. Their method (called SmoothMix) is the current state-of-the-art in the domain of ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āed robust image classiÔ¨Āers. Finally, we note the Consistency regularization method proposed by Jeong et al. [16], which adds a regularization loss to existing methods that helps better maximize the certiÔ¨Āed radius. 3Table 1: Training on CIFAR-10 using a ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer on a single Nvidia V100 GPU. State-of- the-art robustness training methods signiÔ¨Ācantly slow down training compared to standard training.
