As we show in Table 1, training a certiÔ¨Āably robust ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer to convergence using SmoothAdv, MACER, and SmoothMix is46.20√ó, 20.86√ó, and 4.97√óslower, respectively, compared to training a non-robust classiÔ¨Āer with standard training. Given constant innovations in architecture design (Figure 1) and the inÔ¨āux of new data, which may result in various tweaks to deployed networks that elicit retraining, the large overhead of state-of-the- art robustness training methods makes preserving certiÔ¨Āed robustness across model re-deployment difÔ¨Ācult. Therefore, we propose CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT), a training method that improves the usability of certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods by dramatically reducing their training overhead while preserving the certiÔ¨Āed robustness. Given the base classiÔ¨Āer of a pre-trained certiÔ¨Āably robust smooth classiÔ¨Āer, we leverage the knowledge transfer framework to guide the training of a new base classiÔ¨Āer (and its associated robust smooth classiÔ¨Āer).1 In this section, we describe our method and provide theoretical justiÔ¨Ācation for its effectiveness. 3.1 Transferring CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness From Equation 4, it follows that training the base classiÔ¨Āer to maximize Pő∑(főł(x+ ő∑) = y) for any given input xwill result in the maximization of the certiÔ¨Āed radius associated with the smooth classiÔ¨Āer, provided Equation 3 is satisÔ¨Āed. Thus, for the base classiÔ¨Āer főł(x), our goal is to maximize the following quantity over the training set: n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑1[főł(xi + ő∑) = yi] ‚Čą n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑[zyi őł (xi + ő∑)] (5) In the above equation, like prior works [3, 29, 39], we leverage the fact that the softmax output of a classiÔ¨Āer can be treated as a continuous and differentiable approximation of its arg max output. Methods like SmoothAdv [29], MACER [39] and SmoothMix [15] that target ‚Ąď2 certiÔ¨Āable robustness propose training objectives that maximize this term. Now, suppose we have a pre-trained base classiÔ¨Āer fŌÜ. It follows that Eő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑)] ‚Č•0. Through straightforward algebraic manipulations (see Appendix A), we derive the following lower bound: n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑[zyi őł (xi + ő∑)] ‚Č•‚ąí n‚ąĎ i=1 Eő∑[zyi ŌÜ (xi + ő∑) ‚ąízyi őł (xi + ő∑)] (6) That is to say that, for a given input xi, if we minimize the difference between the softmax outputs of the teacher and the student (fŌÜ and főł) corresponding to the correct label yi, we will maximize Equation 5 for the student. However, to ensure that the student has a non-trivial certiÔ¨Āed radius, we must also ensure that Equation 3 is satisÔ¨Āed. If we assume that Equation 3 holds true for the teacher (i.e., the base classiÔ¨Āer of a certiÔ¨Āably robust smooth classiÔ¨Āer), this condition can also be achieved for the student by matching the overall softmax output of the student to that of the teacher. 3.2 CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT) Based on the previous discussion, we now describe our method for training a certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āer through knowledge transfer. First, we obtain a pre-trained base classiÔ¨Āer fŌÜ, which has been trained using a randomized smoothing based robustness training method as this maximizes 1If no pre-trained classiÔ¨Āer is available, we Ô¨Ārst train an architecture of lower complexity (i.e., fast to train) compared to the target architecture (Section 5.1). 4Algorithm 1 CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT) 1: Input: Training data distribution D, certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher base classiÔ¨Āer fŌÜ, noise level ŌÉ, total training iterations T, learning rate őĪ 2: Output: CertiÔ¨Āably robust student base classiÔ¨Āer főł 3: őł‚Üźrandom initialization 4: i‚Üź0 5: while i< T do 6: From D, sample a batch of inputs {x1,x2,¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,xn}. 7: From N(0,ŌÉ2I), generate a batch of Gaussian noise samples {ő∑1,ő∑2,¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,ő∑n}. 8: li ‚Üź1 n ‚ąĎn j=1 ‚ą•zŌÜ(xj + ő∑j) ‚ąízőł(xj + ő∑j)‚ą•2 9: őł‚Üźőł‚ąíőĪ¬∑‚ąáőłli 10: i‚Üźi+ 1 11: end while Eő∑[zy ŌÜ(x+ ő∑)]. Next, we use fŌÜ as a teacher to train a new student base classiÔ¨Āer főł. The student is trained to match the output of the teacher. In doing so, we can maximize the certiÔ¨Āed radius of the associated smooth classiÔ¨Āer gőł (Equation 6), as well as ensure that Equation 3 is satisÔ¨Āed. We describe our implementation in Algorithm 1. Given a batch of inputs, we Ô¨Ārst perturb them with additive Gaussian noise. Next, we compute the ‚Ąď2 distance between the student and the teacher‚Äôs outputs for these Gaussian perturbed inputs. This distance serves as our loss function, and we update the parameters of the student to minimize this loss. At test time, the classiÔ¨Āer főł is converted to its smooth version gőł following DeÔ¨Ānition 2.1. 3.3 Prior Works on Robustness Transfer Several prior works have examined transferring adversarial robustness between classiÔ¨Āers, but these works have been limited to transferring empirical rather than certiÔ¨Āed robustness [2, 8, 14, 42, 43]. Of note is the work by Goldblum et al. [8] in which they combine adversarial training [ 25] with knowledge distillation [13]. They show that distilling knowledge from a large network to a small network improves its empirical robustness as compared to training the small network on its own, but their method makes no effort to improve the computational cost of adversarial training. 4 Evaluation Our goal is to improve the usability of randomized smoothing based robustness training methods. In this section, we demonstrate how CRT enables the reuse of an existing certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āer to train new certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers at signiÔ¨Ācantly reduced training cost compared to prior methods. In our Ô¨Ārst experiment, we train a ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer with a state-of-the-art method, i.e., SmoothMix [15]), and use CRT to transfer its robustness to train several newer generation classiÔ¨Āers. In a second experiment, we recursively use CRT to train a newer generation classiÔ¨Āer using the previous generation classiÔ¨Āer that was also trained using CRT. In each experiment, we compare the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of classiÔ¨Āer trained using CRT against a classiÔ¨Āer trained using SmoothMix (Section 4.1). We Ô¨Ānd that classiÔ¨Āers trained using CRT are similarly robust as when trained using SmoothMix but only require a fraction of training time (Section 4.2). Our main results are generated using the CIFAR-10 dataset [18], but we also demonstrate the effectiveness of CRT on ImageNet [5] (Section 5.3). Both these datasets are open-source and free for non-commercial use. Architectures. We use several popular DNN architectures that were proposed to either improve upon the visual recognition performance of the previous generation architectures or preserve performance while requiring signiÔ¨Ācantly fewer parameters (or both). For the CIFAR-10 experiments, we use ResNet110 [11], ResNeXt29-2x64d [36], DLA [38], and RegNetX_200MF [27].2 Training details. All SmoothMix classiÔ¨Āers were trained using the code made available by the authors3 and the hyperparameters reported by them [ 15]. All CRT classiÔ¨Āers were trained using Stochastic Gradient Descent till convergence (200 epochs), with a batch size of 128. Further hyperparameter details are available in Appendix C.1. Unless speciÔ¨Āed, we report results for noise 2We use the CIFAR-10 version of these architectures, code [MIT License]: https://github.com/ kuangliu/pytorch-cifar. 3SmoothMix code [MIT License]: https://github.com/jh-jeong/smoothmix 5level ŌÉ = 0.25 in the main paper. Additional results for higher noise levels ŌÉ = 0.5 and 1.0 are reported in Appendix B.1. Evaluation Metrics. We report our results using two metrics. First, as done in prior work, we measure the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of a classiÔ¨Āer based on (1) the certiÔ¨Āed test accuracy at ‚Ąď2 radius r4, which is deÔ¨Āned as the fraction of test set inputs that the smooth classiÔ¨Āer classiÔ¨Āes correctly within an ‚Ąď2 ball of radius rcentered at each input, and (2) average certiÔ¨Āed radius (ACR), which is the average of the certiÔ¨Āed radius across all inputs in the test set: ACR(gőł) = 1 ntest ntest‚ąĎ i=1 CR(gőł; xi,yi) On CIFAR-10, we compute these metrics using the entire test set. Second, we measure training time of a classiÔ¨Āer based on the per-epoch time and total training time. The total training time is computed once the model‚Äôs loss has converged. All classiÔ¨Āers were trained on the same machine with a single Nvidia Titan V GPU. 4.1 CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Comparison Standard CRT Training. Given a ResNet110 classiÔ¨Āer trained using SmoothMix, we transfer its robustness to several newer generation classiÔ¨Āers. We compare the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of these classiÔ¨Āers with their SmoothMix trained versions. The results are summarized in Table 2. We observe that using CRT does not reduce the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of the trained classiÔ¨Āer compared to training with SmoothMix. In fact, interestingly, CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers exhibit higher certiÔ¨Āed robustness compared to their SmoothMix baseline. Not only do CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers have higher ACR (improvement of 8.1% in the best case), they also exhibit higher certiÔ¨Āed accuracy at different‚Ąď2 radii. Furthermore, CRT remains effective even as the generation gap between the student and the teacher increases. This implies that the same teacher can potentially be reused indeÔ¨Ānitely, amortizing the teacher‚Äôs training cost to a constant. These results empirically validate our theoretical justiÔ¨Ācation of CRT. Finally, we note that in Table 2, the accuracy on clean inputs and ACR of CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers follow the same trend as in Figure 1, thus motivating the need for periodic model re-deployment to incorporate architectural improvements. We observe that the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of the resulting classiÔ¨Āers remains high even with recursive use of CRT. The empirical results are to be expected given our theoretical understanding of CRT: In order to train a robust student, we only require that the teacher is already robust ( i.e., satisÔ¨Āes the condition of Theorem 2.2) irrespective of the training method used to achieve robustness. Thus, we expect CRT to remain effective even at longer chain lengths. In Section 5.2, we will highlight the relationship between the teacher‚Äôs training method and the robustness of a CRT trained student. 4.2 Training Time Comparison Having established that CRT effectively transfers certiÔ¨Āed robustness between classiÔ¨Āers, we now evaluate its training overhead. For comparison, we also evaluate the training overhead of SmoothMix. In Table 4, we report the per-epoch time and total time of training different architectures with each method. For brevity, we only compare the training time for standard CRT.5 We observe that the per- epoch time of CRT is signiÔ¨Ācantly lower than SmoothMix. Similarly, when trained until convergence, the total training time of CRT is signiÔ¨Ācantly lower. Across the three architectures that we run our experiments on, CRT achieves an average epoch time speedup of 10.75√ó. Comparing overall training times, CRT speeds up training by, on average, 8.06√ó. If we consider the real-world scenario where the model has to be periodically redeployed to incorporate architectural improvements, the cumulative training time using SmoothMix is 96.21 hours as each new architecture is trained from scratch. With CRT, the cumulative time is reduced to 11.70 hours representing a 87.84% savings in costs associated with computational resources. Teacher‚Äôs availability.So far, we assumed the availability of a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher (ResNet110). We argue that this is a reasonable assumption as the amortized cost associated with the one-time training of a robust teacher is negligible across many generations of the model. Regardless, in Section 5.1, we examine a scenario where the teacher is unavailable. Under this scenario, we demonstrate how CRT can be used to speedup the training of ResNet110 for use as teacher. The section layout is as follows: in Section 5.1, we discuss the scenario in which a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher is not readily available and demonstrate how CRT can still speed up robustness training; in Section 5.2, we examine how the method used to train the teacher affects the robustness of the student; in Section 5.3, we study the scalability of CRT using the ImageNet dataset; in Section 5.4, we compare CRT with a closely related prior work on fast certiÔ¨Āed robustness training, i.e., Consistency regularization [16]; in Section 5.5, we discuss the limitations of CRT; in Section 5.6, we address the broader impact of CRT. 5.1 Teacher Not Available We‚Äôve designed CRT under the assumption that a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher is already available. However, even if a certiÔ¨Āably robust teacher is not available, CRT can still speed up training. Given a certiÔ¨Āable robust training method and a large network architecture, we can reduce the training overhead by robustly training a comparatively smaller network Ô¨Ārst. Then, we can use CRT to transfer the robustness of the small network to a larger network. In Table 5, we present results for such a process. First, we trained a ResNet20 network using SmoothMix, then we used CRT to train a ResNet110 network. We compare the robustness of a ResNet110 trained using this process with one trained using SmoothMix. As we can see, the CRT ResNet110 network has comparable robustness with the SmoothMix ResNet110 network. However, even when adding the teacher and student training times, CRT still speeds up training by approximately 2√órelative to SmoothMix. For MACER and SmoothAdv training, we use the code made available by the authors67 and the hyperpa- rameters reported by them. Using CRT, we transfer the robustness of each teacher to a ResNet110 clas- siÔ¨Āer. The results are reported in Table 6. For reference, we also report robustness of a ResNet110 net- work trained independently using the chosen robustness training methods. Overall, we observe a slight variation in the robustness of the CRT trained networks depending on the teachers training method. Based on Equation 6, this is expected as maximizing the teacher‚Äôs performance will in turn maximize the performance of the student. Our empirical results align with this reasoning: the MACER teacher was the least robust of the three methods, and its student is similarly the least robust of the students. However, in all cases, the CRT trained network obtained certiÔ¨Āed robustness comparable to its teacher. 5.3 Scalability Here, we study the effectiveness of CRT on a large-scale dataset, i.e., ImageNet. For this purpose, we train ResNet18 classiÔ¨Āers using three certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods (MACER, SmoothAdv, and SmoothMix). Next, we transfer their robustness to a ResNet50 classiÔ¨Āer. The results were generated on a 500 sample test set (following prior works [29, 39, 15]) and are summarized in Table 7. For reference, we also report robustness of a ResNet50 network trained independently using the chosen robustness training methods. In all cases, we observe that students achieve certiÔ¨Āed robustness comparable to their respective teachers. However, another closely related work was recently published by Jeong & Shin [ 16], which shows state-of-the-art ACR and potential training time improvements. They proposed a consistency regularization loss that improves the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of smooth classiÔ¨Āers by enforcing the base classiÔ¨Āer‚Äôs soft outputs to be consistent across multiple noisy copies of a given input. Therefore, their additional computational overhead scales linearly with the number of noisy samples used to compute the consistency loss. With respect to computational overhead, CRT adds only one forward pass,i.e., the pass through the teacher. When paired with Gaussian data augmentation training, their regularization loss signiÔ¨Ācantly improves the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of a smooth classiÔ¨Āer. By applying their regularization loss over only two noisy copies of the input, they can achieve better certiÔ¨Āed robustness than prior state-of-the- art robustness training methods like MACER [39] and SmoothAdv [29]. The key difference between CRT and consistency regularization is in the training overhead when combined with other state-of-the-art certiÔ¨Āed training methods. Consistency regularization augments classiÔ¨Āer training with an additional loss term. Therefore, their training overhead is dominated by the training method selected. In their experiments, they focused on Gaussian data augmentation, which adds little to no training overhead relative to standard training. However, if a more computationally intensive method was selected (e.g., MACER), they remark their training overhead would dramatically increase. With respect to CRT, if a teacher is available ( i.e., a previous generation model), the overhead of CRT is agnostic to the training method. If it is not available, we demonstrated in Section 5.1, that CRT can still greatly reduce training overhead. For interested readers, we include results for transferring robustness from a teacher trained using Consistency regularization in Appendix B.4. 95.5 Limitations In this paper, we use probabilistic certiÔ¨Āed robustness methods as they rely on Theorem 2.2 and, thus, are designed to maximize the certiÔ¨Āed radius (Equation 4). We found that deterministic methods (e.g., CROWN-IBP [41]) impose a stricter training requirement on the base teacher classiÔ¨Āer. For a given input, deterministic training methods require the base classiÔ¨Āer to be correct for all inputs within the ‚Ąď2-norm ball, rather than simply be likely to correctly classify inputs within the ‚Ąď2-norm ball. This restriction lowers the potential ACR of the smooth teacher classiÔ¨Āer, which also lowers the ACR of the student trained using CRT. For example, when using CROWN-IBP [41] to train a ResNeXt base classiÔ¨Āer, the ACR for the corresponding smooth classiÔ¨Āer is only 0.064. When transferring the robustness of this ResNeXt classiÔ¨Āer to a WideResNet34-10 student, we get an ACR of 0.065. Additionally, we note that the classiÔ¨Āer architectures we present in the paper are restricted to CNNs. Recently, a new class of transformer-based image classiÔ¨Āers [6, 24, 4] have been proposed that show improved performance over CNN classiÔ¨Āers. We brieÔ¨āy studied the effectiveness of CRT when transferring robustness between CNN and transformer architectures using ViT [6] and present the results in Appendix B.2, but further exploration is needed. Finally, CRT has only been studied using the ‚Ąď2 norm and image data due to the limitations of current certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods. 5.6 Broader Impacts As we have shown, our work improves the efÔ¨Āciency of training certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers, in an effort to improve the security of AI-powered systems. Beyond the broad negative societal impacts of machine learning, we are not aware of any impacts speciÔ¨Āc to our work. 6 Conclusion In this paper, we proposed the Ô¨Ārst general-purpose framework to speed up the training of certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers using knowledge transfer and randomized smoothing. Our proposed method, CertiÔ¨Āed Robustness Transfer (CRT) enables transferring the certiÔ¨Āed robustness of a classiÔ¨Āer to another classiÔ¨Āer at a cost comparable to standard training. We provided a theoretical understanding of CRT and provided empirical evidence of its effectiveness. On CIFAR-10, we showed that across several generations of classiÔ¨Āer architectures, CRT trained classiÔ¨Āers 8√ófaster than when using a state-of-the-art training method, while achieving comparable or better certiÔ¨Āed robustness. Furthermore, CRT can reduce the training overhead of certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods even when an initial robust classiÔ¨Āer is not present. The use of machine learning in security and safety critical environments motivates a need for models with certiÔ¨Āably robust performance, but the training overhead of existing certiÔ¨Āed robustness training methods inhibits their usability. Our work addresses this issue, especially for commercial applications where periodical model re-deployment is inevitable. B Additional Results B.1 Higher Noise Level In the main paper, we conduct experiments on CIFAR-10 using noise levelŌÉ= 0.25 only. Here, we report our main set of results on CIFAR-10 (Table 3) using higher ŌÉvalues. In Table 8, we report results using ŌÉ= 0.5 and in Table 9, we report results using ŌÉ= 1.0. B.2 Using ViT [6] In the main paper, we used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based architectures. However, there is another recently developed class of architectures that use Transformers for the task of image classiÔ¨Ācation [ 6, 24, 4]. In Table 10, we present results measuring the effectiveness of CRT in transferring robustness from ResNet110 (a CNN-based classiÔ¨Āer) to ViT [6] (a Transformer-based classiÔ¨Āer). For comparison, we also report results obtained on training ViT with SmoothMix. CRT trained ViT classiÔ¨Āers perform comparable or better than their SmoothMix counterparts. B.3 Training SmoothMix ClassiÔ¨Āers with CRT Hyperparameters For generating results using prior methods, we strictly adhere to the hyperparameters reported by them. However, the hyperparameters that we use for CRT training is different than the ones used by prior methods (see Table 16). In Table 11, we report results obtained on using CRT training hyperparameters with SmoothMix. We note that there is not a signiÔ¨Ācant difference in the robustness achieved using the two sets of hyperparameters. B.4 Training Teacher with Consistency Regularization [16] For our main set of results on CIFAR-10, we focused on SmoothMix. In Section 5.4, we discussed Consistency Regularization recently proposed by Jeong & Shin [ 16] as another method to attain certiÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers with at a cost comparable to standard training depending on the setting. Here, we show results when Consistency Regularization is used to train the teacher classiÔ¨Āer in Tables 12 and 13. For comparison, in Table 12, we also report the results for training the classiÔ¨Āers using Consistency Regularization. As with other training methods, CRT is effective in transferring the robustness of the teacher classiÔ¨Āer irrespective of the training method. B.5 ImageNet Results In Table 14, we present an additional ImageNet result using a different student-teacher pair. We note that, as in Table 7, CRT remains effective. B.6 Gaussian Data Augmentation Baseline Along with the theoretical framework for creating certiÔ¨Āably robust image classiÔ¨Āers using random- ized smoothing, Cohen et al. [3] also proposed a simple yet effective method for training classiÔ¨Āers with high certiÔ¨Āed robustness within this framework. This method involves training the base classi- Ô¨Āer with Gaussian data augmentation. To date, this method remains the fastest way to train classi- Ô¨Āers with non-trivial certiÔ¨Āed robustness using the randomized smoothing framework. However, this method is not as sophisticated as the more recently proposed methods, and so yields much poorer cer- tiÔ¨Āed robustness than them. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Certified Robustness Transfer (CRT), a general-purpose knowledge transfer framework that accelerates training of l2-certified robust classifiers by distilling robustness from a pre-trained robust teacher to a student. Provides theoretical justification that matching teacher and student soft outputs on Gaussian-perturbed inputs increases the student‚Äôs certified radius irrespective of the teacher‚Äôs training method. Empirically demonstrates on CIFAR-10 average 8√ó speedup over SmoothMix with comparable or better robustness (up to ~8% ACR gain), supports recursive reuse across architecture generations (87.84% cumulative training time reduction), and scales to ImageNet with comparable student/teacher ACR.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Assumes a base teacher classifier f_phi with l2-certified robustness obtained via randomized smoothing methods (e.g., SmoothMix, MACER, SmoothAdv). The student f_theta is trained to match the teacher‚Äôs output on Gaussian-perturbed inputs.
- Loss: per-batch L2 distance between teacher and student softmax outputs on x+eta, where eta~N(0, sigma^2 I). Objective aligns student‚Äôs E_eta[z_y^theta(x+eta)] with teacher‚Äôs, maximizing the smoothed classifier‚Äôs certified radius per Cohen et al.
- Theoretical bound: minimizing E_eta[z_y^phi(x+eta) - z_y^theta(x+eta)] lower-bounds maximizing E_eta[z_y^theta(x+eta)], and matching full soft outputs helps satisfy the Cohen condition for certification.
- Training algorithm (Algorithm 1): sample batch, add Gaussian noise, compute L2 loss between z_phi(x+eta) and z_theta(x+eta), SGD update. After training, convert f_theta to smoothed classifier g_theta.
- Method-agnostic to how the teacher was trained; supports recursive application (a CRT-trained student can be a teacher for the next generation).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets: CIFAR-10 (full test set used for certification) and ImageNet (500-sample test subset for certification, following prior work).
- Architectures: Teachers and students include ResNet110, ResNeXt29-2x64d, DLA, RegNetX_200MF; additional experiments with ResNet20 (teacher-not-available case) and ViT; ImageNet with ResNet18‚ÜíResNet50 and ResNet50‚ÜíRegNetX-4.0G.
- Baselines: SmoothMix (primary SOTA), MACER, SmoothAdv; also compare/transfer from Consistency Regularization (appendix). Gaussian augmentation baseline reported.
- Metrics: Certified test accuracy at multiple l2 radii; Average Certified Radius (ACR). Training time: per-epoch and total time to convergence.
- CRT training: SGD, 200 epochs on CIFAR-10, batch size 128, initial LR 0.1 with step decays (100,150), weight decay 1e-4; sigma=0.25 (main), with results for sigma=0.5 and 1.0 in appendix. Timings measured on a single Nvidia Titan V GPU. Certification via randomized smoothing per Cohen et al.
- Key results: On CIFAR-10 (sigma=0.25), CRT students match or exceed SmoothMix robustness (e.g., RegNetX_200MF ACR 0.564 vs 0.538); recursive CRT maintains high robustness over chains up to length 3; average overall training speedup ~8.06√ó, epoch time speedup ~10.75√ó; cumulative training time reduced by 87.84% across three redeployments. Teacher-not-available: ResNet20 (SmoothMix)‚ÜíResNet110 via CRT yields ACR 0.540 vs 0.550 (SmoothMix) with ~2√ó total time speedup (10.07h vs 18.98h). ImageNet: students achieve ACR comparable to teachers across MACER, SmoothAdv, SmoothMix (e.g., ResNet18‚ÜíResNet50, sigma=0.5).
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Primarily applicable to probabilistic certification (randomized smoothing) in l2 norm; deterministic certified training (e.g., CROWN-IBP) yields low ACR for the smoothed teacher, leading to low student ACR.
- Evaluations focus on image classification; limited exploration with transformer-based models (ViT) and no evaluation on non-image modalities.
- Only l2 threat model considered; other norms (l_inf, l1) not studied.
- Assumes access to a robust teacher for maximal gains; when absent, still helps but initial robust teacher must be trained (demonstrated using smaller network first).
- Broader compute details not reported for all experiments; certification on ImageNet uses a 500-sample subset.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend CRT to other certification paradigms (deterministic methods) and threat models (l_inf, l1), and to non-vision modalities (text, audio, tabular).
- Explore alternative/distillation losses (e.g., KL divergence, temperature scaling), multi-noise or noise-schedule training, and ensemble/distillation from multiple teachers.
- Systematically study long recursive chains, larger architecture gaps, and transformer-heavy settings; improve theory with tighter bounds and generalization guarantees.
- Teacher-less regimes: curriculum/self-training for bootstrapping robust teachers; semi-/weakly-supervised CRT and domain adaptation to new distributions.
- Scalability: efficient certification on full ImageNet and larger datasets; dynamic sigma selection; combining CRT with consistency regularization or other training-time accelerations.
- Analyze robustness under distribution shift and robustness‚Äďaccuracy trade-offs in CRT-trained models.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
# File: code/train_crt.py
# Teacher->Student CRT (randomized smoothing distillation)
# - Trains a student f_theta to match a robust teacher f_phi on Gaussian-perturbed inputs
# - Loss: L2 distance between teacher and student softmax outputs on x + eta, eta~N(0, sigma^2 I)
# - After training, certify with code/certify.py (Smooth by Cohen et al.)

import argparse
import os
import time
from typing import Optional

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.optim import SGD
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import StepLR
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from tensorboardX import SummaryWriter

from architectures import get_architecture, ARCHITECTURES
from datasets import get_dataset, DATASETS
from train_utils import accuracy, requires_grad_, log, init_logfile


device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")


def l2_softmax_loss(student_logits: torch.Tensor,
                    teacher_logits: torch.Tensor,
                    temperature: float = 1.0) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Squared L2 distance between student & teacher softmax outputs (optionally temperature-scaled).
    Returns mean over batch.
    """
    s = F.softmax(student_logits / temperature, dim=1)
    t = F.softmax(teacher_logits / temperature, dim=1)
    return ((s - t) ** 2).sum(dim=1).mean()


def kl_softmax_loss(student_logits: torch.Tensor,
                    teacher_logits: torch.Tensor,
                    temperature: float = 1.0) -> torch.Tensor:
    """KL divergence between teacher and student softmax outputs (teacher as target)."""
    t = F.softmax(teacher_logits / temperature, dim=1)
    log_s = F.log_softmax(student_logits / temperature, dim=1)
    # note: reduction='batchmean' equivalent to mean over batch for sum over classes
    return F.kl_div(log_s, t, reduction='batchmean')


def train_epoch(train_loader: DataLoader,
                student: torch.nn.Module,
                teacher: torch.nn.Module,
                optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
                epoch: int,
                sigma: float,
                m_noises: int,
                temperature: float,
                align: str,
                ce_weight: float,
                writer: Optional[SummaryWriter] = None,
                print_freq: int = 10):
    student.train()
    requires_grad_(student, True)
    teacher.eval()
    requires_grad_(teacher, False)

    batch_time = 0.0
    running_loss = 0.0
    running_align = 0.0
    running_ce = 0.0
    running_top1 = 0.0
    count = 0
    start_t = time.time()

    for i, (inputs, targets) in enumerate(train_loader):
        inputs = inputs.to(device)
        targets = targets.to(device)
        b = inputs.size(0)

        # Accumulate alignment loss across m noises to approximate E_eta
        align_loss = 0.0
        for k in range(m_noises):
            noise = torch.randn_like(inputs, device=device) * sigma
            with torch.no_grad():
                t_logits = teacher(inputs + noise)
            s_logits = student(inputs + noise)
            if align == 'l2':
                align_k = l2_softmax_loss(s_logits, t_logits, temperature)
            elif align == 'kl':
                align_k = kl_softmax_loss(s_logits, t_logits, temperature)
            else:
                raise ValueError(f"Unknown align loss: {align}")
            align_loss = align_loss + align_k
        align_loss = align_loss / float(m_noises)

        # Optional supervised CE on clean inputs (helps stabilize training on early epochs)
        ce_loss = torch.tensor(0.0, device=device)
        if ce_weight > 0.0:
            logits_clean = student(inputs)
            ce_loss = F.cross_entropy(logits_clean, targets)

        loss = align_loss + ce_weight * ce_loss

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        # Track stats (Top-1 on clean inputs by student)
        with torch.no_grad():
            logits_eval = student(inputs)
            acc1, = accuracy(logits_eval, targets, topk=(1,))
        running_top1 += acc1.item() * b
        running_loss += loss.item() * b
        running_align += align_loss.item() * b
        running_ce += ce_loss.item() * b
        count += b

        if (i + 1) % print_freq == 0:
            elapsed = time.time() - start_t
            print(f"Epoch [{epoch}] Step [{i+1}/{len(train_loader)}] "
                  f"Loss {running_loss / count:.4f} Align {running_align / count:.4f} "
                  f"CE {running_ce / max(count,1):.4f} Acc@1 {running_top1 / count:.3f} "
                  f"({elapsed:.1f}s)")

    # TensorBoard
    if writer is not None:
        writer.add_scalar('train/loss', running_loss / count, epoch)
        writer.add_scalar('train/align_loss', running_align / count, epoch)
        writer.add_scalar('train/ce_loss', running_ce / count, epoch)
        writer.add_scalar('train/acc1', running_top1 / count, epoch)

    return (running_loss / count, running_top1 / count)


def test_epoch(test_loader: DataLoader,
               student: torch.nn.Module,
               sigma: float,
               epoch: int,
               writer: Optional[SummaryWriter] = None,
               print_freq: int = 10):
    student.eval()
    running_loss = 0.0
    running_top1 = 0.0
    count = 0

    with torch.no_grad():
        for i, (inputs, targets) in enumerate(test_loader):
            inputs = inputs.to(device)
            targets = targets.to(device)
            # Evaluate with Gaussian noise (as in Cohen et al.) to match smoothed behavior
            noisy_inputs = inputs + torch.randn_like(inputs, device=device) * sigma
            logits = student(noisy_inputs)
            ce = F.cross_entropy(logits, targets)
            acc1, = accuracy(logits, targets, topk=(1,))
            b = inputs.size(0)
            running_loss += ce.item() * b
            running_top1 += acc1.item() * b
            count += b
            if (i + 1) % print_freq == 0:
                print(f"Test [{i+1}/{len(test_loader)}] "
                      f"Loss {running_loss / count:.4f} Acc@1 {running_top1 / count:.3f}")

    if writer is not None:
        writer.add_scalar('test/loss', running_loss / count, epoch)
        writer.add_scalar('test/acc1', running_top1 / count, epoch)

    return (running_loss / count, running_top1 / count)


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='CRT: Teacher->Student smoothing distillation')
    parser.add_argument('dataset', type=str, choices=DATASETS)
    parser.add_argument('arch', type=str, choices=ARCHITECTURES, help='student architecture')
    parser.add_argument('--teacher', type=str, required=True,
                        help='path to saved PyTorch model of the TEACHER (checkpoint with keys arch,state_dict)')

    # Optimization
    parser.add_argument('--epochs', default=90, type=int)
    parser.add_argument('--batch', default=256, type=int)
    parser.add_argument('--lr', default=0.1, type=float)
    parser.add_argument('--lr_step_size', default=30, type=int)
    parser.add_argument('--gamma', default=0.1, type=float)
    parser.add_argument('--momentum', default=0.9, type=float)
    parser.add_argument('--weight-decay', default=1e-4, type=float)

    # Smoothing / distillation
    parser.add_argument('--noise_sd', default=0.25, type=float, help='sigma used for Gaussian perturbations')
    parser.add_argument('--num-noise-vec', default=4, type=int, help='m: number of noises per batch to approximate E_eta')
    parser.add_argument('--temperature', default=1.0, type=float, help='softmax temperature for alignment')
    parser.add_argument('--align', default='l2', choices=['l2', 'kl'], help='alignment loss between teacher and student')
    parser.add_argument('--ce-weight', default=0.0, type=float, help='optional CE(y, f_theta(x)) weight')

    # Logging / I/O
    parser.add_argument('--workers', default=4, type=int)
    parser.add_argument('--outdir', default=None, type=str, help='output directory for logs and checkpoints')
    parser.add_argument('--id', default=None, type=str, help='run id to create a unique subdir')
    parser.add_argument('--print-freq', default=10, type=int)

    args = parser.parse_args()

    run_id = args.id if args.id is not None else str(int(time.time()))
    outdir = args.outdir if args.outdir is not None else f"logs/{args.dataset}/crt/noise_{args.noise_sd}/{args.arch}/{run_id}"
    os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True)
    log_file = os.path.join(outdir, 'log.txt')
    init_logfile(log_file, "epoch\ttime\tlr\ttrain_loss\ttrain_acc\ttestloss\ttest_acc")
    writer = SummaryWriter(outdir)

    # Data
    train_ds = get_dataset(args.dataset, 'train')
    test_ds = get_dataset(args.dataset, 'test')
    pin_memory = (args.dataset == 'imagenet')
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, shuffle=True, batch_size=args.batch,
                              num_workers=args.workers, pin_memory=pin_memory)
    test_loader = DataLoader(test_ds, shuffle=False, batch_size=args.batch,
                             num_workers=args.workers, pin_memory=pin_memory)

    # Models
    # Load teacher
    t_ckpt = torch.load(args.teacher, map_location='cpu')
    teacher = get_architecture(t_ckpt['arch'], args.dataset)
    teacher.load_state_dict(t_ckpt['state_dict'])
    teacher = teacher.to(device)
    teacher.eval()
    requires_grad_(teacher, False)

    # Init student
    student = get_architecture(args.arch, args.dataset).to(device)

    # Optimizer & scheduler
    optimizer = SGD(student.parameters(), lr=args.lr, momentum=args.momentum, weight_decay=args.weight_decay)
    scheduler = StepLR(optimizer, step_size=args.lr_step_size, gamma=args.gamma)

    # Train
    model_path = os.path.join(outdir, 'checkpoint.pth.tar')
    for epoch in range(0, args.epochs):
        t0 = time.time()
        train_loss, train_acc = train_epoch(train_loader, student, teacher, optimizer, epoch,
                                            sigma=args.noise_sd, m_noises=args.num_noise_vec,
                                            temperature=args.temperature, align=args.align,
                                            ce_weight=args.ce_weight, writer=writer, print_freq=args.print_freq)
        test_loss, test_acc = test_epoch(test_loader, student, sigma=args.noise_sd, epoch=epoch,
                                         writer=writer, print_freq=args.print_freq)
        t1 = time.time()
        log(log_file, f"{epoch}\t{t1 - t0:.3f}\t{scheduler.get_last_lr()[0]:.3f}\t{train_loss:.3f}\t{train_acc:.3f}\t{test_loss:.3f}\t{test_acc:.3f}")

        # Step LR (PyTorch >=1.1: step after optimizer.step in epoch)
        scheduler.step()

        # Save student checkpoint (compatible with code/certify.py)
        torch.save({
            'epoch': epoch + 1,
            'arch': args.arch,
            'state_dict': student.state_dict(),
            'optimizer': optimizer.state_dict(),
        }, model_path)

    print(f"Training finished. Student checkpoint saved to: {model_path}")
    print("To certify, run: python -m code.certify {dataset} {ckpt} {sigma} {outfile} --N0 100 --N 100000 --alpha 0.001".format(
        dataset=args.dataset, ckpt=model_path, sigma=args.noise_sd, outfile=os.path.join(outdir, 'certify.txt')))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
Overview
- Goal: Distill a robust, smoothed teacher f_phi into a student f_theta by aligning the teacher and student softmax outputs on Gaussian-perturbed inputs x + eta, eta ~ N(0, sigma^2 I). The loss is an L2 distance between soft outputs; minimizing E_eta[||p_theta(x+eta) - p_phi(x+eta)||_2^2] aligns the smoothed decision functions and increases the certified radius per Cohen et al.
- After training, evaluate or certify the smoothed student with code/certify.py (Smooth).

Key files
- code/train_crt.py (above): implementation of CRT teacher->student distillation.
- code/certify.py: certification with Smooth.
- code/predict.py: Monte Carlo prediction with Smooth.
- code/architectures.py, code/datasets.py, code/train_utils.py: models, data, utilities.

Loss and training details
- Alignment loss: squared L2 distance between teacher and student softmax outputs on the same Gaussian perturbation: L_align = E_eta ||softmax(z_theta(x+eta)/T) - softmax(z_phi(x+eta)/T)||_2^2.
- Optionally, a small CE term on clean inputs can be added: L = L_align + lambda_ce * CE(y, f_theta(x)). Default lambda_ce=0 for pure matching as in the method.
- Multiple noises per input (m): approximate the expectation by averaging over m i.i.d. Gaussian draws per mini-batch.
- After training, the student can be used as a base classifier for Smooth and certified with the same sigma.
- Recursive application: use a previously CRT-trained student checkpoint as the next teacher via --teacher.

Recommended hyperparameters (CIFAR-10)
- Dataset: cifar10
- Architectures: student/teacher can be cifar_resnet20, cifar_resnet110, cifar_densenet40
- Sigma (noise_sd): 0.25 or 0.50 are common; must match teacher‚Äôs smoothing sigma at certification time.
- m (num-noise-vec): 4‚Äď16 (start with 4 for speed)
- Epochs: 90 (StepLR at 30 and 60 by gamma=0.1)
- Batch size: 256
- Optimizer: SGD(lr=0.1, momentum=0.9, weight_decay=1e-4)
- Temperature: 1.0
- CE weight: 0.0 (pure alignment). Optionally 0.1 for warmup stability.

Teacher preparation options (method-agnostic)
- Cohen-style noise augmentation (ERM + Gaussian): code/train_cohen.py
  Example: python -m code.train_cohen cifar10 cifar_resnet20 --epochs 90 --batch 256 --lr 0.1 --lr_step_size 30 --gamma 0.1 --noise_sd 0.25
- MACER teacher: code/train_macer.py
  Example: python -m code.train_macer cifar10 cifar_resnet20 --epochs 90 --batch 256 --lr 0.1 --lr_step_size 30 --gamma 0.1 --noise_sd 0.25 --num-noise-vec 16 --beta 16 --lbd 16 --margin 8
- SmoothAdv teacher (adversarial, randomized smoothing): code/train_salman.py
  Example: python -m code.train_salman cifar10 cifar_resnet20 --epochs 90 --batch 256 --lr 0.1 --lr_step_size 30 --gamma 0.1 --noise_sd 0.25 --attack PGD --epsilon 64 --num-steps 10 --warmup 10
  Note: epsilon and init norms are in pixel levels and divided by 256 internally. Ensure noise_sd used during training matches the sigma you‚Äôll certify with.

CRT student training (examples)
1) CIFAR-10, ResNet-20 student distilled from a MACER teacher at sigma=0.25
- Assume teacher checkpoint at logs/cifar10/macer/.../checkpoint.pth.tar
- Command:
  python -m code.train_crt cifar10 cifar_resnet20 \
    --teacher logs/cifar10/macer/.../checkpoint.pth.tar \
    --epochs 90 --batch 256 --lr 0.1 --lr_step_size 30 --gamma 0.1 \
    --noise_sd 0.25 --num-noise-vec 4 --temperature 1.0 --align l2 --ce-weight 0.0

2) CIFAR-10, DenseNet-40 student distilled from a SmoothAdv (PGD) teacher at sigma=0.5
  python -m code.train_crt cifar10 cifar_densenet40 \
    --teacher logs/cifar10/salman/pgd_.../checkpoint.pth.tar \
    --epochs 90 --batch 256 --lr 0.1 --lr_step_size 30 --gamma 0.1 \
    --noise_sd 0.5 --num-noise-vec 8 --temperature 1.0 --align l2

Certification of the student (Smooth, Cohen et al.)
- Use the same sigma as training. Example for CIFAR-10 student at sigma=0.25:
  python -m code.certify cifar10 logs/cifar10/crt/noise_0.25/cifar_resnet20/<run_id>/checkpoint.pth.tar 0.25 \
    logs/cifar10/crt/noise_0.25/cifar_resnet20/<run_id>/certify.txt \
    --N0 100 --N 100000 --alpha 0.001 --batch 1000 --split test
- Quick prediction without radius (same Monte Carlo test):
  python -m code.predict cifar10 <student_ckpt> 0.25 logs/.../predict.txt --N 100000 --alpha 0.001 --batch 1000

Imagenet/MNIST notes
- ImageNet: use resnet50 as base arch; pin_memory True is handled by DataLoader in the script; typical sigma around 0.25‚Äď0.5. Increase batch size and possibly reduce m due to memory.
- MNIST: use lenet; typical sigma ~0.5‚Äď1.0; set batch size higher (e.g., 512), m=4‚Äď8.

Practical tips
- The teacher and student both include the dataset‚Äôs NormalizeLayer as the first module; noise is added in pixel space before normalization by design (to certify in original coordinates).
- num-noise-vec trades off computation for a tighter Monte Carlo estimate of E_eta. Larger m improves alignment but increases runtime.
- Ensure the certify sigma equals the training noise_sd. Certified radii scale with sigma via radius = sigma * Phi^{-1}(pA_bar).
- Recursive CRT: after training, pass the student‚Äôs checkpoint to --teacher for another CRT run to potentially improve performance/certifiability.
- Mixed loss (align='kl') can be tried; default align='l2' matches the method description‚Äôs L2 soft target matching.

research_study_list.title: Adversarial Training and Provable Defenses: Bridging the Gap
Semialgebraic Optimization for Lipschitz Constants of ReLU Networks
research_study_list.abstract:
The Lipschitz constant of a network plays an important role in many
applications of deep learning, such as robustness certification and Wasserstein
Generative Adversarial Network. We introduce a semidefinite programming
hierarchy to estimate the global and local Lipschitz constant of a multiple
layer deep neural network. The novelty is to combine a polynomial lifting for
ReLU functions derivatives with a weak generalization of Putinar's positivity
certificate. This idea could also apply to other, nearly sparse, polynomial
optimization problems in machine learning. We empirically demonstrate that our
method provides a trade-off with respect to state of the art linear programming
approach, and in some cases we obtain better bounds in less time.
research_study_list.full_text: Semialgebraic Optimization for Lipschitz Constants of ReLU Networks Tong Chen LAAS-CNRS Universit√© de Toulouse 31400 Toulouse, France tchen@laas.fr Jean-Bernard Lasserre LAAS-CNRS & IMT Universit√© de Toulouse 31400 Toulouse, France lasserre@laas.fr Victor Magron LAAS-CNRS Universit√© de Toulouse 31400 Toulouse, France vmagron@laas.fr Edouard Pauwels IRIT & IMT Universit√© de Toulouse 31400 Toulouse, France edouard.pauwels@irit.fr Abstract The Lipschitz constant of a network plays an important role in many applications of deep learning, such as robustness certiÔ¨Ācation and Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network. We introduce a semideÔ¨Ānite programming hierarchy to estimate the global and local Lipschitz constant of a multiple layer deep neural network. The novelty is to combine a polynomial lifting for ReLU functions derivatives with a weak generalization of Putinar‚Äôs positivity certiÔ¨Ācate. This idea could also apply to other, nearly sparse, polynomial optimization problems in machine learning. We empirically demonstrate that our method provides a trade-off with respect to state of the art linear programming approach, and in some cases we obtain better bounds in less time. 1 Introduction We focus on the multiple layer networks with ReLU activations. We propose a computationally efÔ¨Ācient method to give a valid upper bound on the Lipschitz constant of such networks. Recall that a function f, deÔ¨Āned on a convex set X‚äÜ Rn, is L-Lipschitz with respect to the norm ||¬∑|| if for all x,y ‚ąąX, we have |f(x) ‚ąíf(y)|‚Č§ L||x ‚ąíy||. The Lipschitz constant of f with respect to norm ||¬∑||, denoted by L||¬∑|| f , is the inÔ¨Āmum of all those valid Ls: L||¬∑|| f := inf{L: ‚ąÄx,y ‚ąąX,|f(x) ‚ąíf(y)|‚Č§ L||x ‚ąíy||}. (1) For deep networks, they play an important role in many applications related to robustness certiÔ¨Ācation which has emerged as an active topic. See recent works [9, 31] based on semideÔ¨Ānite programming (SDP), [8, 40] based on linear programming (LP), [34] based on mixed integer programming (MIP), and [6, 38, 39, 41] based on outer polytope approximation, [7] based on averaged activation operators. We follow a different route and compute upper bounds on the Lipschitz constant of neural networks [35]. Another important application is the Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network (WGAN) [ 3]. Wasserstein distance is estimated by using the space of functions encoded by 1-Lipschitz neural networks. This requires a precise estimation of the Lipschitz constants , see recent contributions [2, 11, 25]. 34th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2020), Vancouver, Canada. arXiv:2002.03657v4  [math.OC]  28 Oct 2020Recently there has been a growing interest in polynomial optimization for such problems. In [31], robustness certiÔ¨Ācation is modeled as a quadratically constrained quadratic problem (QCQP) for ReLU networks. Similarly, in [21], an upper bound on the Lipschitz constant of an ELU network is obtained from a polynomial optimization problem (POP). In contrast to optimization problems with more general functions, powerful (global) positivity certiÔ¨Ācates are available for POP. Such certiÔ¨Ācates are needed to approximate global optima as closely as desired [20]. Such positivity certiÔ¨Ācates have been already applied with success in various areas of science and engineering. The Ô¨Ārst attempt to compute lower bounds of a QCQP by solving an SDP can be traced back to Shor [32], recently applied to certify robustness of neural networks in [31]. Converging LP based hierarchies are based on Krivine-Stengle‚Äôs certiÔ¨Ācates [15, 19, 33]. In [ 21], sparse versions are used to bound the Lipschitz constant of neural networks. On the other hand, Putinar‚Äôs certiÔ¨Ācate [17, 29] is implemented via an SDP-based hierarchy (a.k.a., ‚ÄúLasserre‚Äôs hierarchy‚ÄĚ) and provides converging approximate solutions of a POP. Various applications are described in [16], see also its application to large scale optimal power Ô¨āow problems in [26, 27] and for roundoff error certiÔ¨Ācation in [24]. The LP hierarchy is cheaper than the SDP hierarchy, but less efÔ¨Ācient for combinatorial optimization [22], and cannot converge in Ô¨Ānitely many steps for continuous POPs. Finally, weaker positivity certiÔ¨Ācates can be used, for example DSOS/SDSOS [ 1] based on second-order cone programming, or hybrid BSOS hierarchy [19]. 1.1 Related Works Upper bound on Lipschitz constants of deep networks can be obtained by a product of the layer-wise Lipschitz constants [13]. This is however extremely loose and has many limitations [13]. Note that [36] propose an improvement via a Ô¨Āner product. Departing from this approach, [21] propose a QCQP formulation to estimate the Lipschitz constant of neural networks. Shor‚Äôs relaxation allows to obtain a valid upper bound. Alternatively, using the LP hierarchy, [21] obtain tighter upper bounds. By another SDP-based method, [10] provide an upper bound of the Lipschitz constant. However this method is restricted to the L2-norm whereas most robustness certiÔ¨Ācation problems in deep learning are rather concerned with the L‚ąě-norm. 1.2 Preliminaries and Notations Denote by F the multiple layer neural network, m the number of hidden layers, p0,p1,...,p m the number of nodes in the input layer and each hidden layer. For simplicity,(p0,p1,...,p m) will denote the layer structure of network F. Let x0 be the initial input, and x1,..., xm be the activation vectors in each hidden layer. Each xi, i= 1,...,m , is obtained by a weight Ai, a bias bi, and an activation function ŌÉ, i.e., xi = ŌÉ(Aixi‚ąí1 + bi). We only consider coordinatewise application of the ReLU activation function, deÔ¨Āned as ReLU(x) = max{0,x}for x‚ąąR. The ReLU function is non-smooth, we deÔ¨Āne its generalized derivative as the set-valued function G(x) such that G(x) = 1 for x> 0, G(x) = 0 for x< 0 and G(x) = {0,1}for x= 0. We assume that the last layer in our neural network is a softmax layer with K entries, that is, the network is a classiÔ¨Āer for Klabels. For each label k‚ąą{1,...,K }, the score of label kis obtained by an afÔ¨Āne product with the last activation vector, i.e., cT kxm for some ck ‚ąąRpm. The Ô¨Ānal output is the label with the highest score, i.e., y= arg maxkcT kxm. The product xy of two vectors x and y is considered as coordinate-wise product. 1.3 Contribution ‚ÄĘWe Ô¨Ārst express both graphs of ReLU and its generalized derivative via basic closed semialgebraic sets, i.e., sets deÔ¨Āned with Ô¨Ānite conjunctions of polynomial (in)equalities. Indeed, if y= ReLU(x) and v ‚ąąG(x), then equivalently: y(y‚ąíx) = 0,y ‚Č•x,y ‚Č•0 and v(v‚ąí1) = 0,(v‚ąí1/2)x‚Č•0. Note that the explicit semialgebraic expression of ReLU has already been used in related works, see for example [ 31]. The semialgebraic expression for Gis our contribution. LipOpt-4), but takes more (resp. less) computational time. 2 Problem Setting In this section, we recall basic facts about optimization and build the polynomial optimization model for estimating Lipschitz constant of neural networks. 2.1 Polynomial Optimization In a polynomial optimization problem (POP), one computes the global minimum (or maximum) of a multivariate polynomial function on a basic closed semialgebraic set. If the semialgebraic set is the whole space, the problem is unconstrained, and constrained otherwise. Given a positive integer n‚ąąN, let x = (x1,...,x n)T be a vector of decision variables, and denote by [n] the set {1,...,n }. 3A POP has the canonical form: inf x‚ąąRn {f(x) : fi(x) ‚Č•0,i ‚ąą[p]; gj(x) = 0,j ‚ąą[q]}, (POP) where f,fi,gj are all polynomials in nvariables. With I ‚äā{1,...,n }, let xI := (xi)i‚ąąI and let R[x] be the space of real polynomials in the variables x while R[xI] is the space of real polynomials in the variables xI. In particular, if the objective f and constraints fi,gj in (POP) are all of degree at most 2, we say that the problem is a quadratically constrainted quadratic problem (QCQP). The Shor‚Äôs relaxation of a QCQP is a semideÔ¨Ānite program which can be solved efÔ¨Āciently numerically. If all polynomials involved in (POP) are afÔ¨Āne then the problem is a linear program (LP). 2.2 Lipschitz Constant Estimation Problem (LCEP) Suppose we train a neural network F for K-classiÔ¨Ācations and denote by Ai,bi,ck its parameters already deÔ¨Āned in section 1.2. In this situation, we propose a heuristic method which can be applied to problems with arbitrary many constraints that possibly destroy the sparsity. See Appendix E for more information to deal with higher-degree polynomial objective. 5 Experiments In this section, we provide results for the global and local Lipschitz constants of random networks of Ô¨Āxed size (80,80) and with various sparsities. We also compute bounds of a real trained 1-hidden layer network. The complete results for global/local Lipschitz constants of both 1-hidden layer and 2-hidden layer networks can be found in Appendix F and G. For all experiments we focus on the L‚ąě-norm, the most interesting case for robustness certiÔ¨Ācation. Moreover, we use the Lipschitz constants computed by various methods to certify robustness of a trained network, and compare the ratio of certiÔ¨Āed inputs with different methods. Let us Ô¨Ārst provide an overview of the methods with which we compare our results. SHOR: Shor‚Äôs relaxation applied to (LCEP). Note that this is different from Shor‚Äôs relaxation decribed in [21] since we apply it to a different QCQP. HR-2: second-order heuristic relaxation applied to (LCEP). LipOpt-3: LP-based method by [21] with degree 3. LBS: lower bound obtained by sampling 50000 random points and evaluating the dual norm of the gradient. The reason why we list LBS here is because LBS is a valid lower bound on the Lipschitz constant. Therefore all methods should provide a result not lower than LBS, a basic necessary condition of consistency. As discussed in section 2.2, if we want to estimate the global Lipschitz constant, we need the input space ‚Ą¶ to be the whole space. In consideration of numerical issues, we set ‚Ą¶ to be the ball of radius 10 around the origin. For the local Lipschitz constant, we set by default the radius of the input ball as őĶ = 0.1. In both cases, we compute the Lipschitz constant with respect to the Ô¨Ārst label. All experiments are run on a personal laptop with a 4-core i5-6300HQ 2.3GHz CPU and 8GB of RAM. We use the (Python) code provided by [21]2 to execute the experiments for LipOpt with Gurobi solver. For HR-2 and SHOR, we use the YALMIP toolbox (MATLAB) [23] with MOSEK as a backend to calculate the Lipschitz constants for random networks. For trained network, we implement our algorithm on Julia [4] with MOSEK optimizer to accelerate the computation. Remark: The crossover option 3 in Gurobi solver is activated by default, and it is used to transform the interior solution produced by barrier into a basic solution. We deactivate this option in our experiments since this computation is unnecessary and takes a lot of time. Throughout this paper, running time is referred to the time taken by the LP/SDP solver (Gurobi/Mosek) and OfM means running out of memory during building up the LP/SDP model. 5.1 Lipschitz Constant Estimation Random Networks. Note that for ŌĶ= 0.1, we improve a little bit by 67% compared to Grad-cert (65%) described in [30], as we use an exact formulation of the derivative of ReLU function. 6 Conclusion and Future Work Optimization Aspect: In this work, we propose a new heuristic moment relaxation based on the dense and sparse Lasserre‚Äôs hierarchy. In terms of performance, our method provides bounds no worse than Shor‚Äôs relaxation and better bounds in many cases. In terms of computational efÔ¨Āciency, our algorithm also applies to nearly sparse polynomial optimization problems without running into computational issue. Machine Learning Aspect: The ReLU function and its generalized derivative G(x) are semialge- braic. This semialgebraic character is easy to handle exactly in polynomial optimization (via some lifting) so that one is able to apply moment relaxation techniques to the resulting POP. Moreover, our heuristic moment relaxation provides tighter bounds than Shor‚Äôs relaxation and the state-of-the-art LP-based algorithm in [21]. Future research: The heuristic relaxation is designed for QCQP (e.g. problem (LCEP) for 1-hidden layer networks). As the number of hidden layer increases, the degree of the objective function also increases and the approach must be combined with lifting or sub-moment techniques described in Appendix E, in order to deal with higher-degree objective polynomials. EfÔ¨Ācient derivation of approximate sparse certiÔ¨Ācates for high degree polynomials should allow to enlarge the spectrum of applicability of such techniques to larger size networks and broader classes of activation functions. This is an exciting topic of future research. 9Broader Impact Developing optimization methods resulting in numerical certiÔ¨Ācates is a necessary step toward the veriÔ¨Ācation of systems involving AI trained components. Such systems are expected to be more and more common in the transport industry and constitute a major challenge in terms of certiÔ¨Ācation. We believe that polynomial optimization is one promising tool to address this challenge. Remark (Being a gradient almost everywhere is not sufÔ¨Ācient in general): As suggested by anonymous AC5, Lemma 1 may admit a simpler proof. Indeed the Clarke subdifferential is the convex hull of limits of sequences of gradients. For Lipschitz constant, we want the maximum norm element, which necessarily happens at a corner of the convex hull, therefore for our purposes it sufÔ¨Āces to consider sequences. Since the ReLU network will be almost-everywhere differentiable, we can consider a shrinking sequence of balls around any point, and we will have gradients which are arbitrarily close to any corner of the differential at our given point. Therefore, the norms will converge to that norm, and thus it sufÔ¨Āces to optimize over differentiable points, and what we choose at the nondifferentiability does not matter. The above argument is essentially valid because ReLU only contains univariate nondifferentiability which is very speciÔ¨Āc. However the argument is implicitely based on the idea that the composition of almost everywhere differentiable functions complies with calculus rules, which is not correct in gen- eral due to lack of injectivity. For more general networks, what we choose at the nondifferentiability does matter. Indeed, consider the following functions F: x‚Ü¶‚Üí ( x x ) G: ( y1 y2 ) ‚Ü¶‚Üímax {y1,y2} The composition G‚ó¶F is the identity on R and both F and Gare differentiable almost every where. Consider the mappings JF : x‚Ü¶‚Üí ( 1 1 ) JG: ( y1 y2 ) ‚Ü¶‚Üí Ô£Ī Ô£≤ Ô£≥ (0,0)T if ,y1 = y2 (1,0)T if ,y1 >y2 (0,1)T if ,y1 <y2 . JF is the Jacobian of F and JG is the gradient of Galmost everywhere. Now the product JG(F(x)) √óJF(x) = 0 for all x ‚ąąR. Hence computing the product of these gradient almost everywhere operators gives value 0, suggesting that the function is constant, while it is not. The reason for the failure here is that 5This is the occasion to thank this person for his/her work on the paper, and for this nice suggestion. 13JG, despite being gradient almost everywhere, is not conservative for G. For this reason, the product does not provide any notion of Lipschicity and the choice at nondifferentiability point does matter for G. Overall, we believe that the proof suggested by AC is correct for ReLU networks because they are built only using univariate nondifferentiabilities. However the argument that, the choice made at nondifferentiability point does not have an impact, is not correct for more general networks. This dis- cussion is kept explicit in this appendix since many other types of semialgebraic activation functions are used in deep learning, such as max pooling, for which one may use similar approaches as what we proposed to evaluate Lipschitz constants. For these more complex nondifferentiability, concervativity will be essential to ensure that one obtains proper Lipschitz constants, beyond the argument that beging differentiable almost everywhere implies that the choice made at nondifferentiability point does not matter. C Link between SDP and Sum-of-Square (SOS) The primal and dual of Lasserre‚Äôs hierarchy (MomOpt-d) nicely illustrate the duality between moments and positive polynomials. Indeed for each Ô¨Āxed d, the dual of (MomOpt-d) reads: sup t‚ąąR {t: f ‚ąít= ŌÉ0 + p‚ąĎ i=1 ŌÉigi}, (SOS-d) 14where ŌÉ0 is a sum-of-squares (SOS) polynomial of degree at most 2d, and ŌÉj are SOS polynomials of degree at most 2(d‚ąíŌČi), ŌČi = ‚Ćądeg(gj)/2‚ĆČ. The right-hand-side of the identity in (SOS-d) is nothing less than Putinar‚Äôs positivity certiÔ¨Ācate [29] for the polynomial x ‚Ü¶‚Üíf(x) ‚ąíton the compact semialgebraic set {x : gi(x) ‚Č•0, i‚ąą[p]}. Similarly, the dual problem of (MomSpOpt-d) reads: sup t‚ąąR {t: f ‚ąít= l‚ąĎ k=1 ( ŌÉ0,k + m‚ąĎ j=1 ŌÉj,kgj ) }, (SpSOS-d) where ŌÉ0,k are SOS in R[xIk] of degree at most 2d, and ŌÉj,k are SOS in R[xIk] of degree at most 2(d‚ąíŌČi), ŌČi = ‚Ćądeg(gj)/2‚ĆČ. Then (SpSOS-d) implements the sparse Putinar‚Äôs positivity certiÔ¨Ācate [18, 37]. D Illustration of Heuristic Relaxation Consider problem (NlySpOpt). We already have a sparsity pattern with subsets Ik and an additional ‚Äúbad‚ÄĚ constraintg‚Č•0 (assumed to be quadratic). Then we consider the sparse moment relaxations (MomSpOpt-d) applied to (NlySpOpt) without the bad constraint g ‚Č•0 and simply add two con- straints: (i) the moment constraint M1(y) ‚™į0 (with full dense Ô¨Ārst-order moment matrix M1(y)), and (ii) the linear moment inequality constraint Ly(g) ‚Č•0 (which is the lowest-order localizing matrix constraint M0(gy) ‚™į0). To see why the full moment constraint M1(y) ‚™į0 is needed, consider the toy problem (7). Recall that the subsets we deÔ¨Āned are I1 = {1,2}, I2 = {2,3}. Now suppose that we need to consider an additional ‚Äúbad‚ÄĚ constraint (1 ‚ąíx1 ‚ąíx2 ‚ąíx3)2 = 0 . After developing Ly(g), one needs to consider the moment variable y103 corresponding to the monomial x1x3 in the expansion of g = (1 ‚ąíx1 ‚ąíx2 ‚ąíx3)2, and y103 does not appear in the moment matrices Md(y,I1) and Md(y, I2) because x1 and x3 are not in the same subset. However y103 appears in M1(y) (which is a n√ónmatrix). Now let us see how this works for problem (LCEP). First introduce new variables zi with associated constraints zi ‚ąíAixi‚ąí1 ‚ąíbi = 0, so that all ‚Äúbad‚ÄĚ constraints are afÔ¨Āne. Equivalently, we may and will consider the single ‚Äúbad" constraint g‚Č•0 with g(z1,..., x0,x1,... ) = ‚ąí‚ąĎ i||zi ‚ąíAxi‚ąí1 ‚ąí bi||2 and solve (MomNlySpOpt-d). We brieÔ¨āy sketch the rationale behind this reformulation. Let (yd)d‚ąąN be a sequence of optimal solutions of(MomNlySpOpt-d). If d‚Üí‚ąě, then yd ‚Üíy (possibly for a subsequence (dk)k‚ąąN), and y corresponds to the moment sequence of a measure¬Ķ, supported on {(x,z) : gi(x,z) ‚Č•0, i‚ąą[p]; ‚ąę gd¬Ķ ‚Č•0}. But as ‚ąígis a square, ‚ąę gd¬Ķ‚Č•0 implies g= 0, ¬Ķ-a.e., and therefore zi = Axi‚ąí1 + bi, ¬Ķ-a.e. This is why we do not need to consider the higher-order constraints Md(gy) ‚™į0 for d> 0; only M0(gy) ‚™į0 (‚áĒLy(g) ‚Č•0) sufÔ¨Āces. In fact, we impose the stronger linear constraints Ly(g) = 0 and Ly(zi ‚ąíAxi‚ąí1 ‚ąíbi) = 0 for all i‚ąą[p]. Problem (LCEP-MLP2) can now be reformulated as: max xi,ui,t ‚ąĎ i ti‚ü®diag(A(:,i) 1 )AT 2 diag(c),s‚ü© (ReducedLCEP-MLP2) s.t. Ô£Ī Ô£īÔ£īÔ£≤ Ô£īÔ£īÔ£≥ u1(u1 ‚ąí1) = 0,(u1 ‚ąí1/2)(A1x0 + b1) ‚Č•0 , u2(u2 ‚ąí1) = 0,(u2 ‚ąí1/2)(A2x1 + b2) ‚Č•0 , x1(x1 ‚ąíA1x0 ‚ąíb1) = 0,x1 ‚Č•0,x1 ‚Č•A1x0 + b1 ; t2 ‚Č§1,(x0 ‚ąí¬Įx0 + őĶ)(x0 ‚ąí¬Įx0 ‚ąíőĶ) ‚Č§0,s = u1uT 2 . Even when p1 = p2 = 100, we add 10000 variables and constraints, which will cause a memory issue (no SDP solver is able to handle matrices of size O(104)). This is why we use the following approximation technique as a remedy. E.2 Heuristic Relaxation for Cubic Terms In this section, we introduce an alternative technique to handle the cubic terms tiuj 1uk 2 appearing in the objective function of problem (LCEP-MLP2). Recall that the main obstacle that prevents us from applying the HR-2 method is that we don‚Äôt have the moments for cubic terms tiuj 1uk 2 in the Ô¨Ārst-order moment matrix M1(y,{ti,uj 1,uk 2})). (MomLCEP2-1) F Global Lipschitz Constant Estimation for Random Networks We use the experimental settings described in Section 5. F.1 1-Hidden Layer Networks Figure 2 displays the average upper bounds of global Lipschitz constants and the time of different algorithms for 1-hidden layer random networks of different sizes and sparsities. We can see from Figure 2a that when the size of the network is small (10, 20, etc.), the LP-based method LipOpt-3 is slightly better than the SDP-based method HR-2. However, when the size and sparsity of the network increase, HR-2 provides tighter bounds. From Figure 2b, we can see that LipOpt-3 is more efÔ¨Ācient than HR-2 only when the size or the sparsity of the network is small (for (10,10) networks, or for (40,40) networks of sparsity 5, etc.). When the networks are dense or nearly dense, our method not only takes much less time, but also gives much tighter upper bounds. For global Lipschitz constant estimation, SHOR and HR-2 give nearly the same upper bounds. This is because the sizes of the toy networks are quite small. For big real network, as shown in Table 2, HR-2 provides strictly tighter bound than SHOR. Finally, SHOR is more efÔ¨Ācient than HR-2 and LipOpt-3 in terms of computational complexity. 17‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ (40,40) Global (80,80) Global (10,10) Global (20,20) Global 20 40 60 80 20 40 60 80 5 10 15 20 10 20 30 40 0.6 0.9 1.2 1.5 1 2 3 4 0.4 0.6 0.8 1.0 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 Sparsity Lipschitz Constant Algorithm ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ HR‚ąí2 SHOR LipOpt‚ąí3 LipOpt‚ąí4 LBS (a) ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ (40,40) Global (80,80) Global (10,10) Global (20,20) Global 20 40 60 80 20 40 60 80 5 10 15 20 10 20 30 40 0.1 1.0 10.0 1 10 100 1000 0.03 0.10 0.30 1e‚ąí01 1e+00 1e+01 1e+02 1e+03 Sparsity Runnging time Algorithm ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ ‚óŹ HR‚ąí2 SHOR LipOpt‚ąí3 LipOpt‚ąí4 (b) Figure 2: Global Lipschitz constant upper bounds (left) and solver running time (right) for 1-hidden layer networks with respect to L‚ąě-norm obtained by SHOR, HR-2, LipOpt-3, LipOpt-4 and LBS. F.2 2-Hidden Layer Networks For 2-hidden layer networks, we use the technique introduced in Appendix E in order to deal with the cubic terms in the objective. Figure 3 displays the average upper bounds of global Lipschitz constants and the running time of different algorithms for 2-hidden layer random networks of different sizes and sparsities. We can see from Figure 3a that the SDP-based method HR-2 performs worse than the LP-based method LipOpt-3 for networks of size (10,10,10). However, as the size and the sparsity of the network increase, the difference between HR-2 and LipOpt-3 becomes smaller (and HR-2 performs even better). For networks of size (20,20,10), (30,30,10) and (40,40,10), with sparsity greater than 10, HR-2 provides strictly tighter bounds than LipOpt-3. This fact has already been shown in Table 1 (right), HR-1 and HR-2 give consistently tighter upper bounds than LipOpt-3, with the price of higher computational time. G Local Lipschitz Constant Estimation for Random Networks We use the experimental settings described in Section 5. G.1 1-Hidden Layer Networks Figure 4 displays the average upper bounds of local Lipschitz constants and the running time of different algorithms for 1-hidden layer random networks of different sizes and sparsities. By contrast with the global case, we can see from Figure 4a that HR-2 gives strictly tighter upper bounds than SHOR when the sparsity is larger than 40. As a trade-off, HR-2 takes more computational time than SHOR. According to Figure 4b, the running time of HR-2 is around 5 times longer than SHOR. Similar to the global case, LipOpt-3 performs well when the network is sparse. However, when the sparsity increases, HR-2 and SHOR are more efÔ¨Ācient and provide better bounds than LipOpt-3. G.2 2-Hidden Layer Networks For 2-hidden layer networks, we use the approximation technique described in Appendix E in order to reduce the objective to degree 2. Figure 5a and 5b displays the average upper bounds of local Lipschitz constants and the running time of different algorithms for 2-hidden layer random networks of different sizes and sparsities. By contrast with the global case, we can see from Figure 5a that HR-2 gives strictly tighter upper bounds thanHR-1 when the sparsity is larger than 40.
