Wide residual networks. arXiv preprint arXiv:1605.07146, 2016.Obfuscated Gradients Give a False Sense of Security: Circumventing Defenses to Adversarial Examples A. Local Intrinsic Dimensionality Defense Details. The Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (Amsaleg et al., 2015) ‚Äúassesses the space-Ô¨Ālling capability of the region surrounding a reference example, based on the distance distribution of the example to its neighbors‚ÄĚ (Ma et al., 2018). The authors present evidence that the LID is signiÔ¨Ācantly larger for adversarial examples generated by existing attacks than for normal images, and they construct a classiÔ¨Āer that can distinguish these adversarial images from normal images. Again, the authors indicate that LID is not intended as a defenseand only should be used to explore properties of adversarial examples. However, it would be natural to wonder whether it would be effective as a defense, so we study its robustness; our results conÔ¨Ārm that it is not adequate as a defense. The method used to compute the LID relies on Ô¨Ānding the k nearest neighbors, a non- differentiable operation, rendering gradient descent based methods ineffective. Let Sbe a mini-batch of N clean examples. Let ri(x) denote the distance (under metric d(x,y)) between sample xand its i-th nearest neighbor in S(under metric d). Then LID can be approximated by LIDd(x) =‚ąí ( 1 k k‚ąĎ i=1 log ri(x) rk(x) )‚ąí1 where kis a defense hyperparameter the controls the num- ber of nearest neighbors to consider. The authors use the distance function dj(x,y) = ÓĶĻÓĶĻf1..j(x) ‚ąíf1..j(y) ÓĶĻÓĶĻ2 to measure the distance between the jth activation layers. The authors compute a vector of LID values for each sample: ‚ąí‚ąí‚ÜíLID(x) ={LIDdj (x)}n j=1. Finally, they compute the ‚ąí‚ąí‚ÜíLID(x) over the training data and adversarial examples generated on the training data, and train a logistic regression classiÔ¨Āer to detect adversarial examples. We are grateful to the authors for releasing their complete source code. Discussion. While LID is not a defense itself, the authors assess the ability of LID to detect different types of attacks. Through solving the formulation min. |x‚ąíx‚Ä≤|2 2 + őĪ(‚Ąď(x‚Ä≤) +LID-loss(x‚Ä≤)) the authors attempt to determine if the LID metric is a good metric for detecting adversarial examples. Here, LID-loss(¬∑) is a function that can be minimized to reduce the LID score. However, the authors report that this modiÔ¨Āed attack still achieves 0% success. Because Carlini and Wagner‚Äôs ‚Ąď2 attack is unbounded, any time the attack does not reach 100% success indicates that the attack became stuck in a local minima. When this happens, it is often possible to slightly modify the loss function and return to 100% attack success (Carlini & Wagner, 2017b). In this case, we observe the reason that performing this type of adaptive attack fails is that gradient descent does not succeed in optimizing the LID loss, even though the LID computation is differentiable. Computing the LID term involves computing the k-nearest neighbors when comput- ing ri(x). Minimizing the gradient of the distance to the current k-nearest neighbors is not representative of the true direction to travel in for the optimal set of k-nearest neigh- bors. As a consequence, we Ô¨Ānd that adversarial examples generated with gradient methods when penalizing for a high LID either (a) are not adversarial; or (b) are detected as adversarial, despite penalizing for the LID loss. Evaluation. We now evaluate what would happen if a defense would directly apply LID to detect adversarial ex- amples. Instead of performing gradient descent over a term that is difÔ¨Ācult to differentiate through, we have found that generating high conÔ¨Ādence adversarial examples (Carlini & Wagner, 2017a) (completely oblivious to to the detector) is sufÔ¨Ācient to fool this detector. We obtain from the authors their detector trained on both the Carlini and Wagner‚Äôs‚Ąď2 attack and train our own on the Fast Gradient Sign attack, both of which were found to be effective at detecting adver- sarial examples generated by other methods. By generating high-conÔ¨Ādence adversarial examples minimizing ‚Ąď‚ąědis- tortion, we are able to reduce model accuracy to 2% success within ŌĶ= 0.015. LID reports these adversarial examples are benign at a 97% rate (unmodiÔ¨Āed test data is Ô¨āagged as benign with a 98% rate). This evaluation demonstrates that the LID metric can be circumvented, and future work should carefully evaluate if building a detector relying on LID is robust to adversarial examples explicitly targeting such a detector. This work also raises questions whether a large LID is a fundamental characteristic of all adversarial examples, or whether it is a by-product of certain attacks. B. Defense-GAN Defense Details. The defender Ô¨Ārst trains a Generative Adversarial Network with a generator G(z) that maps sam- ples from a latent space (typically z‚ąľN(0,1)) to images that look like training data. Defense-GAN takes a trained classiÔ¨Āer f(¬∑), and to classify an input x, instead of return- ing f(x), returns f(arg minz|G(z) ‚ąíx|). To perform this projection to the manifold, the authors take many steps of gradient descent starting from different random initializa-Obfuscated Gradients Give a False Sense of Security: Circumventing Defenses to Adversarial Examples tions. Defense-GAN was not shown to be effective on CIFAR-10. We therefore evaluate it on MNIST (where it was argued to be secure). Discussion. In Samangouei et al. (2018), the authors con- struct a white-box attack by unrolling the gradient descent used during classiÔ¨Ācation. Despite an unbounded ‚Ąď2 per- turbation size, Carlini and Wagner‚Äôs attack only reaches 30% misclassiÔ¨Ācation rate on the most vulnerable model and under 5% on the strongest. This leads us to believe that unrolling gradient descent breaks gradients. Evaluation. We Ô¨Ānd that adversarial examples do exist on the data manifold as described by the generator G(¬∑). However, Defense-GAN does not completely project to the projection of the generator, and therefore often does not identify these adversarial examples actually on the manifold. We therefore present two evaluations. In the Ô¨Ārst, we as- sume that Defense-GAN were to able to perfectly project to the data manifold, and give a construction for generating adversarial examples. In the second, we take the actual im- plementation of Defense-GAN as it is, and perform BPDA to generate adversarial examples with 50% success under reasonable ‚Ąď2 bounds. Evaluation A. Performing the manifold projection is non- trivial as an inner optimization step when generating adver- sarial examples. To sidestep this difÔ¨Āculty, we show that adversarial examples exist directly onthe projection of the generator. That is, we construct an adversarial example x‚Ä≤= G(z‚ąó) so that |x‚ąíx‚Ä≤|is small and c(x) Őł= c(x‚Ä≤). To do this, we solve the re-parameterized formulation min. ‚ą•G(z) ‚ąíx‚ą•2 2 + c¬∑‚Ąď(G(z)). We initialize z= arg minz|G(z) ‚ąíx|(also found via gradi- ent descent). We train a WGAN using the code the authors provide (Gulrajani et al., 2017), and a MNIST CNN to 99.3% accuracy. We run for 50k iterations of gradient descent for generating each adversarial example; this takes under one minute per instance. The unsecured classiÔ¨Āer requires a mean ‚Ąď2 distor- tion of 0.0019 (per-pixel normalized, 1.45 un-normalized) to fool. When we mount our attack, we require a mean distortion of 0.0027, an increase in distortion of 1.46√ó; see Figure 2 for examples of adversarial examples. The reason our attacks succeed with 100% success without suffering from vanishing or exploding gradients is that our gradient computation only needs to differentiate through the genera- tor G(¬∑) once. Figure 2.Images on the MNIST test set. Row 1: Clean images. Row 2: Adversarial examples on an unsecured classiÔ¨Āer. Row 3: Adversarial examples on Defense-GAN. Concurrent to our work, Ilyas et al. (2017) also develop a nearly identical approach to Defense-GAN; they also Ô¨Ānd it is vulnerable to the attack we outline above, but increase the robustness further with adversarial training. We do not evaluate this extended approach. Evaluation B. The above attack does not succeed on Defense-GAN. While the adversarial examples are directly on the projection of the Generator, the projection process will actually move it off the projection. To mount an attack on the approximate projection process, we use the BPDA attack regularized for ‚Ąď2 distortion. Our attack approach is identical to that of PixelDefend, except we replace the manifold projection with a PixelCNN with the manifold projection by gradient descent on the GAN. Under these settings, we succeed at reducing model accu- racy to 55% with a maximum normalized distortion of.0051 for successful attacks.

reference_research_study_list.title: Evasion attacks against machine learning at test time
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
In security-sensitive applications, the success of machine learning depends
on a thorough vetting of their resistance to adversarial data. In one
pertinent, well-motivated attack scenario, an adversary may attempt to evade a
deployed system at test time by carefully manipulating attack samples. In this
work, we present a simple but effective gradient-based approach that can be
exploited to systematically assess the security of several, widely-used
classification algorithms against evasion attacks. Following a recently
proposed framework for security evaluation, we simulate attack scenarios that
exhibit different risk levels for the classifier by increasing the attacker's
knowledge of the system and her ability to manipulate attack samples. This
gives the classifier designer a better picture of the classifier performance
under evasion attacks, and allows him to perform a more informed model
selection (or parameter setting). We evaluate our approach on the relevant
security task of malware detection in PDF files, and show that such systems can
be easily evaded. We also sketch some countermeasures suggested by our
analysis.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Evasion attacks against machine learning at test time Battista Biggio1, Igino Corona1, Davide Maiorca1, Blaine Nelson2, Nedim ňáSrndi¬ī c3, Pavel Laskov3, Giorgio Giacinto1, and Fabio Roli1 1 Dept. of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, University of Cagliari, Piazza d‚ÄôArmi, 09123 Cagliari, Italy {battista.biggio, igino.corona, davide.maiorca, giacinto, roli}@diee.unica.it, WWW home page: http://prag.diee.unica.it/ 2 Institut f¬® ur Informatik, Universit¬® at Potsdam, August-Bebel-Stra√üe 89, 14482 Potsdam, Germany bnelson@cs.uni-potsdam.de 3 Wilhelm Schickard Institute for Computer Science, University of T¬® ubingen, Sand 1, 72076 T¬® ubingen, Germany {nedim.srndic, pavel.laskov}@uni-tuebingen.de Abstract. In security-sensitive applications, the success of machine learn- ing depends on a thorough vetting of their resistance to adversarial data. In one pertinent, well-motivated attack scenario, an adversary may at- tempt to evade a deployed system at test time by carefully manipulating attack samples. In this work, we present a simple but eÔ¨Äective gradient- based approach that can be exploited to systematically assess the security of several, widely-used classiÔ¨Ācation algorithms against evasion attacks. Following a recently proposed framework for security evaluation, we sim- ulate attack scenarios that exhibit diÔ¨Äerent risk levels for the classiÔ¨Āer by increasing the attacker‚Äôs knowledge of the system and her ability to manipulate attack samples. This gives the classiÔ¨Āer designer a better pic- ture of the classiÔ¨Āer performance under evasion attacks, and allows him to perform a more informed model selection (or parameter setting). We evaluate our approach on the relevant security task of malware detection in PDF Ô¨Āles, and show that such systems can be easily evaded. We also sketch some countermeasures suggested by our analysis. Keywords: adversarial machine learning, evasion attacks, support vec- tor machines, neural networks 1 Introduction Machine learning is being increasingly used in security-sensitive applications such as spam Ô¨Āltering, malware detection, and network intrusion detection [3,5,9,11,14,15,16,19,21]. Due to their intrinsic adversarial nature, these applications diÔ¨Äer from the classi- cal machine learning setting in which the underlying data distribution is assumed to be stationary. To the contrary, in security-sensitive applications, samples (and, arXiv:1708.06131v1  [cs.CR]  21 Aug 2017thus, their distribution) can be actively manipulated by an intelligent, adaptive adversary to confound learning; e.g., to avoid detection, spam emails are often modiÔ¨Āed by obfuscating common spam words or inserting words associated with legitimate emails [3,9,16,19]. This has led to an arms race between the designers of learning systems and their adversaries, which is evidenced by the increasing complexity of modern attacks and countermeasures. For these reasons, classical performance evaluation techniques are not suitable to reliably assess the secu- rity of learning algorithms, i.e., the performance degradation caused by carefully crafted attacks [5]. To better understand the security properties of machine learning systems in adversarial settings, paradigms from security engineering and cryptography have been adapted to the machine learning Ô¨Āeld [2,5,14]. Following common se- curity protocols, the learning system designer should use proactive protection mechanisms that anticipate and prevent the adversarial impact. This requires (i) Ô¨Ānding potential vulnerabilities of learning before they are exploited by the adversary; (ii) investigating the impact of the corresponding attacks ( i.e., eval- uating classiÔ¨Āer security); and ( iii) devising appropriate countermeasures if an attack is found to signiÔ¨Ācantly degrade the classiÔ¨Āer‚Äôs performance. Two approaches have previously addressed security issues in learning. The min-max approach assumes the learner and attacker‚Äôs loss functions are antago- nistic, which yields relatively simple optimization problems [10,12]. A more gen- eral game-theoretic approach applies for non-antagonistic losses; e.g., a spam Ô¨Āl- ter wants to accurately identify legitimate email while a spammer seeks to boost his spam‚Äôs appeal. Under certain conditions, such problems can be solved using a Nash equilibrium approach [7,8]. Both approaches provide a secure counterpart to their respective learning problems; i.e., an optimal anticipatory classiÔ¨Āer. Realistic constraints, however, are too complex and multi-faceted to be incor- porated into existing game-theoretic approaches. Instead, we investigate the vul- nerabilities of classiÔ¨Ācation algorithms by deriving evasion attacks in which the adversary aims to avoid detection by manipulating malicious test samples. 4 We systematically assess classiÔ¨Āer security in attack scenarios that exhibit increas- ing risk levels, simulated by increasing the attacker‚Äôs knowledge of the system and her ability to manipulate attack samples. Our analysis allows a classiÔ¨Āer designer to understand how the classiÔ¨Ācation performance of each considered model degrades under attack, and thus, to make more informed design choices. The problem of evasion at test time was addressed in prior work, but lim- ited to linear and convex-inducing classiÔ¨Āers [9,19,22]. In contrast, the methods presented in Sections 2 and 3 can generally evade linear or non-linear classiÔ¨Āers using a gradient-descent approach inspired by Golland‚Äôs discriminative direc- tions technique [13]. Although we focus our analysis on widely-used classiÔ¨Āers such as Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and neural networks, our approach is applicable to any classiÔ¨Āer with a diÔ¨Äerentiable discriminant function. 4 Note that other kinds of attacks are possible, e.g., if the adversary can manipulate the training data. A comprehensive taxonomy of attacks can be found in [2,14].This paper is organized as follows. We present the evasion problem in Sec- tion 2 and our gradient-descent approach in Section 3. In Section 4 we Ô¨Ārst visually demonstrate our attack on the task of handwritten digit recognition, and then show its eÔ¨Äectiveness on a realistic application related to the detection of PDF malware. Finally in Section 5, we summarize our contributions, discuss possibilities for improving security, and suggest future extensions of this work. 2 Optimal evasion at test time We consider a classiÔ¨Ācation algorithm f : X ‚Ü¶‚ÜíYthat assigns samples repre- sented in some feature space x ‚ąąX to a label in the set of predeÔ¨Āned classes y ‚ąąY = {‚ąí1,+1}, where ‚ąí1 (+1) represents the legitimate (malicious) class. The classiÔ¨Āer f is trained on a dataset D= {xi,yi}n i=1 sampled from an under- lying distribution p(X,Y ). The label yc = f(x) given by a classiÔ¨Āer is typically obtained by thresholding a continuous discriminant function g : X‚Ü¶‚Üí R. In the sequel, we use yc to refer to the label assigned by the classiÔ¨Āer as opposed to the true label y. We further assume that f(x) = ‚ąí1 if g(x) <0, and +1 otherwise. 2.1 Adversary model To motivate the optimal attack strategy for evasion, it is necessary to disclose one‚Äôs assumptions of the adversary‚Äôs knowledge and ability to manipulate the data. To this end, we exploit a general model of the adversary that elucidates spe- ciÔ¨Āc assumptions about adversary‚Äôs goal, knowledge of the system, and capability to modify the underlying data distribution. The considered model is part of a more general framework investigated in our recent work [5], which subsumes eva- sion and other attack scenarios. This model can incorporate application-speciÔ¨Āc constraints in the deÔ¨Ānition of the adversary‚Äôs capability, and can thus be ex- ploited to derive practical guidelines for developing the optimal attack strategy. Adversary‚Äôs goal. As suggested by Laskov and Kloft [17], the adversary‚Äôs goal should be deÔ¨Āned in terms of a utility (loss) function that the adversary seeks to maximize (minimize). In the evasion setting, the attacker‚Äôs goal is to manipulate a single (without loss of generality, positive) sample that should be misclassiÔ¨Āed. Strictly speaking, it would suÔ¨Éce to Ô¨Ānd a sample x such that g(x) < ‚ąíŌĶ for any ŌĶ > 0; i.e., the attack sample only just crosses the decision boundary. 5 Such attacks, however, are easily thwarted by slightly adjusting the decision threshold. A better strategy for an attacker would thus be to create a sample that is misclassiÔ¨Āed with high conÔ¨Ādence; i.e., a sample minimizing the value of the classiÔ¨Āer‚Äôs discriminant function,g(x), subject to some feasibility constraints. Adversary‚Äôs knowledge. The adversary‚Äôs knowledge about her targeted learn- ing system may vary signiÔ¨Ācantly. Such knowledge may include: ‚Äď the training set or part of it; 5 This is also the setting adopted in previous work [9,19,22].‚Äď the feature representation of each sample; i.e., how real objects such as emails, network packets are mapped into the classiÔ¨Āer‚Äôs feature space; ‚Äď the type of a learning algorithm and the form of its decision function; ‚Äď the (trained) classiÔ¨Āer model; e.g., weights of a linear classiÔ¨Āer; ‚Äď or feedback from the classiÔ¨Āer; e.g., classiÔ¨Āer labels for samples chosen by the adversary. Adversary‚Äôs capability. In the evasion scenario, the adversary‚Äôs capability is limited to modiÔ¨Ācations of test data; i.e.altering the training data is not allowed. However, under this restriction, variations in attacker‚Äôs power may include: ‚Äď modiÔ¨Ācations to the input data (limited or unlimited); ‚Äď modiÔ¨Ācations to the feature vectors (limited or unlimited); ‚Äď or independent modiÔ¨Ācations to speciÔ¨Āc features (the semantics of the input data may dictate that certain features are interdependent). Most of the previous work on evasion attacks assumes that the attacker can arbitrarily change every feature [8,10,12], but they constrain the degree of manipulation, e.g., limiting the number of modiÔ¨Ācations, or their total cost. However, many real domains impose stricter restrictions. For example, in the task of PDF malware detection [20,24,25], removal of content is not feasible, and content addition may cause correlated changes in the feature vectors. 2.2 Attack scenarios In the sequel, we consider two attack scenarios characterized by diÔ¨Äerent levels of adversary‚Äôs knowledge of the attacked system discussed below. Perfect knowledge (PK). In this setting, we assume that the adversary‚Äôs goal is to minimize g(x), and that she has perfect knowledge of the targeted classiÔ¨Āer; i.e., the adversary knows the feature space, the type of the classiÔ¨Āer, and the trained model. The adversary can transform attack points in the test data but must remain within a maximum distance ofdmax from the original attack sample. We use dmax as parameter in our evaluation to simulate increasingly pessimistic attack scenarios by giving the adversary greater freedom to alter the data. The choice of a suitable distance measure d : X√óX ‚Ü¶‚Üí R+ is application speciÔ¨Āc [9,19,22]. Such a distance measure should reÔ¨āect the adversary‚Äôs eÔ¨Äort required to manipulate samples or the cost of these manipulations. For example, in spam Ô¨Āltering, the attacker may be bounded by a certain number of words she can manipulate, so as not to lose the semantics of the spam message. Limited knowledge (LK). Here, we again assume that the adversary aims to minimize the discriminant function g(x) under the same constraint that each transformed attack point must remain within a maximum distance of dmax from the corresponding original attack sample. We further assume that the attacker knows the feature representation and the type of the classiÔ¨Āer, but does not know either the learned classiÔ¨Āer f or its training data D, and hence can not directlycompute g(x). However, we assume that she can collect a surrogate dataset D‚Ä≤ = {ňÜxi,ňÜyi}nq i=1 of nq samples drawn from the same underlying distribution p(X,Y ) from which Dwas drawn. This data may be collected by an adversary in several ways; e.g., by sniÔ¨Éng some network traÔ¨Éc during the classiÔ¨Āer operation, or by collecting legitimate and spam emails from an alternate source. Under this scenario, the adversary proceeds by approximating the discrimi- nant function g(x) as ňÜg(x), where ňÜg(x) is the discriminant function of a surrogate classiÔ¨Āer ňÜf learnt on D‚Ä≤. The amount of the surrogate data, nq, is an attack pa- rameter in our experiments. Since the adversary wants her surrogate ňÜf to closely approximate the targeted classiÔ¨Āer f, it stands to reason that she should learn ňÜf using the labels assigned by the targeted classiÔ¨Āer f, when such feedback is available. In this case, instead of using the true class labels ňÜ yi to train ňÜf, the adversary can query f with the samples of D‚Ä≤and subsequently learn using the labels ňÜyc i = f(ňÜxi) for each xi. 2.3 Attack strategy Under the above assumptions, for any target malicious sample x0 (the adver- sary‚Äôs desired instance), an optimal attack strategy Ô¨Ānds a samplex‚ąóto minimize g(¬∑) or its estimate ňÜg(¬∑), subject to a bound on its distance 6 from x0: x‚ąó= arg min x ňÜg(x) (1) s.t. d(x,x0) ‚Č§dmax. Generally, this is a non-linear optimization problem. One may approach it with many well-known techniques, like gradient descent, or quadratic techniques such as Newton‚Äôs method, BFGS, or L-BFGS. We choose a gradient-descent procedure. However, ňÜg(x) may be non-convex and descent approaches may not achieve a global optima. Instead, the descent path may lead to a Ô¨āat region (local minimum) outside of the samples‚Äô support ( i.e., where p(x) ‚Čą0) where the attack sample may or may not evade depending on the behavior of g in this unsupported region (see left and middle plots in Figure 1). Locally optimizing ňÜg(x) with gradient descent is particularly susceptible to failure due to the nature of a discriminant function. Besides its shape, for many classiÔ¨Āers, g(x) is equivalent to a posterior estimatep(yc = ‚ąí1|x); e.g., for neural networks, and SVMs [23]. The discriminant function does not incorporate the evidence we have about the data distribution, p(x), and thus, using gradient descent to optimize Eq. 1 may lead into unsupported regions (p(x) ‚Čą0). Because of the insigniÔ¨Ācance of these regions, the value of g is relatively unconstrained by criteria such as risk minimization. This problem is compounded by our Ô¨Ānite (and possibly small) training set, since it provides little evidence in these regions 6 One can also incorporate additional application-speciÔ¨Āc constraints on the attack samples. Thus, when our gradient descent procedure produces an evasion example in these regions, the attacker cannot be conÔ¨Ādent that this sample will actually evade the corresponding classiÔ¨Āer. Therefore, to increase the probability of successful evasion, the attacker should favor attack points from densely populated regions of legitimate points, where the estimate ňÜg(x) is more reliable (closer to the real g(x)), and tends to become negative in value. To overcome this shortcoming, we introduce an additional component into our attack objective, which estimates p(x|yc = ‚ąí1) using a density estimator. The extra component favors attack points that imitate features of known legitimate samples. In doing so, it reshapes the objective function and thereby biases the resulting gradient descent towards regions where the negative class is concentrated (see the bottom plot in Fig. 1). This produces a similar eÔ¨Äect to that shown by mimicry attacks in network intrusion detection [11].7 For this reason, although our setting is rather diÔ¨Äerent, in the sequel we refer to this extra term as the mimicry component. Finally, we point out that, when mimicry is used ( őĽ > 0), our gradient descent clearly follows a suboptimal path compared to the case when only g(x) is minimized (őĽ= 0). Therefore, more modiÔ¨Ācations may be required to reach the same value of g(x) attained when őĽ= 0. However, as previously discussed, when őĽ= 0, our descent approach may terminate at a local minimum where g(x) >0, without successfully evading detection. This behavior can thus be qualitatively regarded as a trade-oÔ¨Ä between the probability of evading the targeted classiÔ¨Āer and the number of times that the adversary must modify her samples. 3 Gradient descent attacks Algorithm 1 solves the optimization problem in Eq. 2 via gradient descent. In such cases, we need to Ô¨Ānd a feasible neighborx that maximally decrease F(x). A simple approach to this problem is to probe F at every point in a small neighborhood of x, which would however require a large number of queries. For classiÔ¨Āers with a diÔ¨Äerentiable decision function, we can instead select the neighbor whose change best aligns with ‚ąáF(x) and decreases the objective function; i.e., to prevent overshooting a minimum. 4 Experiments In this section, we Ô¨Ārst report a toy example from the MNIST handwritten digit classiÔ¨Ācation task [18] to visually demonstrate how the proposed algorithm modiÔ¨Āes digits to mislead classiÔ¨Ācation. We then show the eÔ¨Äectiveness of the proposed attack on a more realistic and practical scenario: the detection of mal- ware in PDF Ô¨Āles. 4.1 A toy example on handwritten digits Similar to Globerson and Roweis [12], we consider discriminating between two distinct digits from the MNIST dataset [18]. Each digit example is represented as a gray-scale image of 28 √ó28 pixels arranged in raster-scan-order to give feature vectors of d= 28 √ó28 = 784 values. We normalized each feature (pixel) x ‚ąą[0,1]d by dividing its value by 255, and we constrained the attack samples to this range. Accordingly, we optimized Eq. (2) subject to 0 ‚Č§xf ‚Č§1 for all f. We only consider the perfect knowledge (PK) attack scenario. We used the Manhattan distance ( ‚Ąď1-norm), d, both for the kernel density estimator ( i.e., a Laplacian kernel) and for the constraint d(x,x0) ‚Č§dmax in Eq. (3), which bounds the total diÔ¨Äerence between the gray level values of the original image x0 and the attack image x. We used dmax = 5000 255 to limit the total gray-level change to 5000. At each iteration, we increased the ‚Ąď1-norm value of x ‚ąíx0 by 10 255 , or equivalently, we changed the total gray level by 10. This is eÔ¨Äectively the gradient step size. The targeted classiÔ¨Āer was an SVM with the linear kernel and C = 1. We randomly chose 100 training samples and applied the attacks to a correctly-classiÔ¨Āed positive sample. In Fig. 3 we illustrate gradient attacks in which a ‚Äú3‚ÄĚ is to be misclassiÔ¨Āed as a ‚Äú7‚ÄĚ. This is the same eÔ¨Äect demonstrated in the top-left plot of Fig. 1: the classiÔ¨Āer is evaded by making the attack sample suÔ¨Éciently dissimilar from the malicious class. Conversely, when őĽ = 10, the attack images strongly resemble the target class because the mimicry term favors samples that are more similar to the target class. This is the same eÔ¨Äect seen in the bottom plot of Fig. 1. Finally note that, as expected, g(x) tends to decrease more gracefully when mimicry is used, as we follow a suboptimal descent path. Since the targeted classiÔ¨Āer can be easily evaded when őĽ = 0, exploiting the mimicry component would not be the optimal choice in this case. However, in the case of limited knowledge, as discussed at the end of Section 2.3, mimicry may allow us to trade for a higher probability of evading the targeted classiÔ¨Āer, at the expense of a higher number of modiÔ¨Ācations. 4.2 Malware detection in PDF Ô¨Āles We now focus on the task of discriminating between legitimate and malicious PDF Ô¨Āles, a popular medium for disseminating malware [26]. PDF Ô¨Āles are excel- lent vectors for malicious-code, due to their Ô¨āexible logical structure, which can described by a hierarchy of interconnected objects. As a result, an attack can be easily hidden in a PDF to circumvent Ô¨Āle-type Ô¨Āltering. The PDF format fur- ther allows a wide variety of resources to be embedded in the document including JavaScript, Flash, and even binary programs. The type of the embedded ob- ject is speciÔ¨Āed by keywords, and its content is in a data stream. Several recentworks proposed machine-learning techniques for detecting malicious PDFs using the Ô¨Āle‚Äôs logical structure to accurately identify the malware [20,24,25]. In this case study, we use the feature representation of Maiorca et al.[20] in which each feature corresponds to the tally of occurrences of a given keyword. The PDF structure imposes natural constraints on attacks. Although it is diÔ¨Écult to remove an embedded object (and its keywords) from a PDF without corrupting the PDF‚Äôs Ô¨Āle structure, it is rather easy to insert new objects (and, thus, keywords) through the addition of a new version to the PDF Ô¨Āle [1]. In our feature representation, this is equivalent to allowing only feature increments,i.e., requiring x0 ‚Č§x as an additional constraint in the optimization problem given by Eq. (2). Further, the total diÔ¨Äerence in keyword counts between two samples is their Manhattan distance, which we again use for the kernel density estimator and the constraint in Eq. (3). Accordingly, dmax is the maximum number of additional keywords that an attacker can add to the original x0. Experimental setup. For experiments, we used a PDF corpus with 500 mali- cious samples from the Contagio dataset8 and 500 benign samples collected from the web. We randomly split the data into Ô¨Āve pairs of training and testing sets with 500 samples each to average the Ô¨Ānal results. The features (keywords) were extracted from each training set as described in [20]. On average, 100 keywords were found in each run. Further, we also bounded the maximum value of each feature to 100, as this value was found to be close to the 95 th percentile for each feature. This limited the inÔ¨āuence of outlying samples. We simulated the perfect knowledge (PK) and the limited knowledge (LK) scenarios described in Section 2.1. In the LK case, we set the number of samples used to learn the surrogate classiÔ¨Āer to ng = 100. The reason is to demonstrate that even with a dataset as small as the 20% of the original training set size, the adversary may be able to evade the targeted classiÔ¨Āer with high reliability. Further, we assumed that the adversary uses feedback from thetargeted classiÔ¨Āer f; i.e., the labels ňÜyc i = f(ňÜxi) for each surrogate sample ňÜxi ‚ąąD‚Ä≤.9 As discussed in Section 3.2, the value of őĽis chosen according to the scale of the discriminant function g(x), the bandwidth parameter hof the kernel density estimator, and the number of legitimate samples n in the surrogate training set. For computational reasons, to estimate the value of the KDE at x, we only consider the 50 nearest (legitimate) training samples to x; therefore, n‚Č§50 in our case. The bandwidth parameter was set to h = 10, as this value provided a proper rescaling of the Manhattan distances observed in our dataset for the KDE. We thus set őĽ= 500 to be comparable with O(nh). For each targeted classiÔ¨Āer and training/testing pair, we learned Ô¨Āve sur- rogate classiÔ¨Āers by randomly selecting ng samples from the test set, and we averaged their results. In each plot we considered diÔ¨Äerent values of the classiÔ¨Āer parameters, i.e., the regu- larization parameter C for the linear SVM, the kernel parameter ő≥ for the SVM with RBF kernel, and the number of neurons min the hidden layer for the neural network, as reported in the plot title and legend.Experimental results. We report our results in Figure 4, in terms of the false negative (FN) rate attained by the targeted classiÔ¨Āers as a function of the maximum allowable number of modiÔ¨Ācations, dmax ‚ąą[0,50]. We compute the FN rate corresponding to a Ô¨Āxed false positive (FP) rate of FP= 0 .5%. For dmax = 0, the FN rate corresponds to a standard performance evaluation using unmodiÔ¨Āed PDFs. As expected, the FN rate increases with dmax as the PDF is increasingly modiÔ¨Āed. Accordingly, a more secure classiÔ¨Āer will exhibit a more graceful increase of the FN rate. Results for őĽ = 0. We Ô¨Ārst investigate the eÔ¨Äect of the proposed attack in the PK case, without considering the mimicry component (Figure 4, Ô¨Ārst column), for varying parameters of the considered classiÔ¨Āers. The linear SVM (Figure 4, top-left plot) is almost always evaded with as few as 5 to 10 modiÔ¨Ācations, in- dependent of the regularization parameter C. It is worth noting that attacking a linear classiÔ¨Āer amounts to always incrementing the value of the same highest- weighted feature (corresponding to the /Linearized keyword in the majority of the cases) until it reaches its upper bound. This continues with the next highest weighted non-bounded feature until termination. This occurs simply because the gradient of g(x) does not depend on x for a linear classiÔ¨Āer (see Section 3.1). With the RBF kernel (Figure 4, middle-left plot), SVMs exhibit a similar be- havior with C = 1 and various values of its ő≥ parameter,10 and the RBF SVM provides a higher degree of security compared to linear SVMs ( cf. top-left plot and middle-left plot in Figure 4). Interestingly, compared to SVMs, neural net- works (Figure 4, bottom-left plot) seem to be much more robust against the proposed evasion attack. This behavior can be explained by observing that the decision function of neural networks may be characterized by Ô¨āat regions ( i.e., regions where the gradient of g(x) is close to zero). Hence, the gradient descent algorithm based solely on g(x) essentially stops after few attack iterations for most of the malicious samples, without being able to Ô¨Ānd a suitable attack. In the LK case, without mimicry, classiÔ¨Āers are evaded with a probability only slightly lower than that found in the PK case, even when only ng = 100 surrogate samples are used to learn the surrogate classiÔ¨Āer. This aspect highlights the threat posed by a skilled adversary with incomplete knowledge: only a small set of samples may be required to successfully attack the target classiÔ¨Āer using the proposed algorithm. Results for őĽ = 500 . When mimicry is used (Figure 4, second column), the success of the evasion of linear SVMs (with C = 1) decreases both in the PK (e.g., compare the blue curve in the top-left plot with the solid blue curve in the top-right plot) and LK case ( e.g., compare the dashed red curve in the top-left plot with the dashed blue curve in the top-right plot). The reason is that the computed direction tends to lead to a slower descent; i.e., a less direct path that often requires more modiÔ¨Ācations to evade the classiÔ¨Āer. In the non-linear case (Figure 4, middle-right and bottom-right plot), instead, mimicking exhibits some beneÔ¨Ācial aspects for the attacker, although the constraint on feature addition 10 We also conducted experiments using C = 0 .1 and C = 100, but did not Ô¨Ānd signiÔ¨Ācant diÔ¨Äerences compared to the presented results using C = 1.may make it diÔ¨Écult to properly mimic legitimate samples. In particular, note how the targeted SVMs with RBF kernel (with C = 1 and ő≥ = 1) in the PK case (e.g., compare the solid blue curve in the middle-left plot with the solid blue curve in the middle-right plot) is evaded with a signiÔ¨Ācantly higher probability than in the case of őĽ= 0. The reason is that, as explained at the end of Section 2.3, a pure descent strategy on g(x) may Ô¨Ānd local minima ( i.e., attack samples) that do not evade detection, while the mimicry component biases the descent towards regions of the feature space more densely populated by legitimate samples, where g(x) eventually attains lower values. For neural networks, this aspect is even more evident, in both the PK and LK settings (compare the dashed/solid curves in the bottom-left plot with those in the bottom-right plot), sinceg(x) is essentially Ô¨āat far from the decision boundary, and thus pure gradient descent on g can not even commence for many malicious samples, as previously mentioned. In this case, the mimicry term is thus critical for Ô¨Ānding a reasonable descent path to evasion. Discussion. Our attacks raise questions about the feasibility of detecting ma- licious PDFs solely based on logical structure. We found that /Linearized, /OpenAction, /Comment, /Root and /PageLayout were among the most com- monly manipulated keywords. They indeed are found mainly in legitimate PDFs, but can be easily added to malicious PDFs by the versioning mechanism. The attacker can simply insert comments inside the malicious PDF Ô¨Āle to augment its /Comment count. Similarly, she can embedlegitimate OpenAction code to add /OpenAction keywords or add new pages to insert /PageLayout keywords. 5 Conclusions, limitations and future work In this work we proposed a simple algorithm for evasion of classiÔ¨Āers with dif- ferentiable discriminant functions. We investigated the attack eÔ¨Äectiveness in the case of perfect and limited knowledge of the attacked system, and empir- ically showed that very popular classiÔ¨Ācation algorithms (in particular, SVMs and neural networks) can still be evaded with high probability even if the adver- sary can only learn a copy of the classiÔ¨Āer from a small surrogate dataset. Thus, our investigation raises important questions on whether such algorithms can be reliably employed in security-sensitive applications. We believe that the proposed attack formulation can be extended to classiÔ¨Āers with non-diÔ¨Äerentiable discriminant functions as well, such as decision trees and k-nearest neighbors; e.g., by deÔ¨Āning suitable search heuristics similar to our mimicry term to minimize g(x). Interestingly our analysis also suggests improvements for classiÔ¨Āer security. From Fig. 1, it is clear that a tighter enclosure of the legitimate samples increas- ingly forces the adversary to mimic the legitimate class, which may not always be possible; e.g., malicious network packets or PDF Ô¨Āles must contain a valid exploit for the attack to be successful. Accordingly, more secure classiÔ¨Āers can be designed by employing regularization terms that promote enclosure of the legit- imate class; e.g., by penalizing ‚Äúblind spots‚ÄĚ - regions with low p(x) - classiÔ¨Āedas legitimate. Alternatively, one may explicitly model the attack distribution, as in [4]; or add the generated attack samples to the training set. Nevertheless, improving security probably must be balanced with a higher FP rate. In our example applications, the feature representations could be inverted to obtain a corresponding real-world objects (e.g., spam emails, or PDF Ô¨Āles);i.e., it is straightforward to manipulate the given real-world object to obtain the desired feature vector x‚ąó of the optimal attack. However, in practice some complex feature mappings can not be easily inverted; e.g., n-gram features [11]. Another idea would be to modify the real-world object at each step of the gradient descent to obtain a sample in the feature space which is as close as possible to the sample that would be obtained at the next attack iteration. A similar technique has been already exploited by [6] to overcome the pre-image problem. Other interesting extensions of our work may be to (i) consider more eÔ¨Äective strategies such as those proposed by [19,22] to build a small but representative set of surrogate data; and ( ii) improve the classiÔ¨Āer estimate ňÜg(x). To this end, one may exploit ensemble techniques such as bagging or the random subspace method to train several classiÔ¨Āers and then average their output. Acknowledgments. This work has been partly supported by the project CRP- 18293, L.R. 7/2007, Bando 2009, and by the project ‚ÄúAdvanced and secure sharing of multimedia data over social networks in the future Internet‚ÄĚ (CUP F71J11000690002), both funded by Regione Autonoma della Sardegna. Davide Maiorca gratefully acknowledges Regione Autonoma della Sardegna for the Ô¨Ā- nancial support of his PhD scholarship. Blaine Nelson thanks the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation for providing additional Ô¨Ānancial support. The opinions expressed in this paper are solely those of the authors and do not necessarily reÔ¨āect the opinions of any sponsor. References 1. Adobe: PDF Reference, sixth edition, version 1.7 2. reference_research_study_list.title: Unrestricted adversarial examples
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
We introduce a two-player contest for evaluating the safety and robustness of
machine learning systems, with a large prize pool. Unlike most prior work in ML
robustness, which studies norm-constrained adversaries, we shift our focus to
unconstrained adversaries. Defenders submit machine learning models, and try to
achieve high accuracy and coverage on non-adversarial data while making no
confident mistakes on adversarial inputs. Attackers try to subvert defenses by
finding arbitrary unambiguous inputs where the model assigns an incorrect label
with high confidence. We propose a simple unambiguous dataset ("bird-or-
bicycle") to use as part of this contest. We hope this contest will help to
more comprehensively evaluate the worst-case adversarial risk of machine
learning models.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Unrestricted Adversarial Examples Tom B. Brown‚ąó Nicholas Carlini‚ąó Chiyuan Zhang‚ąó Catherine Olsson‚ąó Paul Christiano‚Ä† Ian Goodfellow‚ąó Abstract We introduce a two-player contest for evaluating the safety and robustness of machine learning systems, with a large prize pool. Unlike most prior work in ML robustness, which studies norm-constrained adversaries, we shift our focus to unconstrained adversaries. Defenders submit machine learning models, and try to achieve high accuracy and coverage on non-adversarial data while making no conÔ¨Ādent mistakes on adversarial inputs. Attackers try to subvert defenses by Ô¨Ānding arbitrary unambiguous inputs where the model assigns an incorrect label with high conÔ¨Ādence. We propose a simple unambiguous dataset ("bird-or- bicycle‚ÄĚ) to use as part of this contest. We hope this contest will help to more comprehensively evaluate the worst-case adversarial risk of machine learning models. 1 Introduction Deep learning systems are superhuman at image classiÔ¨Ācation [He et al., 2016], translation [Wu et al., 2016] and game playing [Mnih et al., 2013]. However, goodexpected performance can be misleading: deep learning systems still make surprising and egregious errors in the worst case [Szegedy et al., 2013]. For example, image classiÔ¨Āers have been found to be vulnerable to a range of attacks: ‚ÄĘ using optimization to make small pixel-wise modiÔ¨Ācations to clean inputs [Szegedy et al., 2013]; ‚ÄĘ applying ordinary spatial transformations to the input (such as translations and rotations on images) [Engstrom et al., 2017]; ‚ÄĘ and performing simple guess-and-check (trying a variety of inputs that seem likely to be misclassiÔ¨Āed) [Gilmer et al., 2018]. Even more troubling, these systems often report high conÔ¨Ādence on blatant errors [Nguyen et al., 2015]. As a result, it is not currently possible to protect against these problems simply by trusting the system only when it is highly certain in its prediction. In safety-critical situations, conÔ¨Ādent errors could be catastrophic (e.g., the vision system behind a self-driving car conÔ¨Ādently believing there is no person in front of it when there is). Even in situations where peoples‚Äô lives are not under immediate risk, it is still be important for systems to know when they are uncertain.3 For instance, a home assistant might think that it has heard its owner ask to text a sensitive message to some unintended recipient. If the system is not conÔ¨Ādent that it heard the recipient correctly, it could ask for conÔ¨Ārmation, but if it is incorrectly conÔ¨Ādent then it could send a potentially-sensitive message to someone who it was not intended to receive it ([Chokshi, 2018]). ‚ąóGoogle Brain. Correspondence to: Tom B. Brown <tombrown@google.com>. ‚Ä†OpenAI. 3Indeed, there is a whole sub-Ô¨Āeld of machine learning for estimating conÔ¨Ādence in predictions. For an introduction, see [Guo et al., 2017]. arXiv:1809.08352v1  [stat.ML]  22 Sep 2018Figure 1: Unrestricted adversarial examples Adversarial examples can be generated in various ways, including small modiÔ¨Ācations to the input pixels, spatial transformations, or simple guess-and- check to Ô¨Ānd misclassiÔ¨Āed inputs. We believe it is important to design machine learning systems that never make conÔ¨Ādent mistakes. 4 Such a guarantee would help protect not only against deliberate attacks by adversaries, but also against unintentional accidents. Is the goal of ‚Äúno conÔ¨Ādent mistakes‚ÄĚ even possible? Two aspects seem particularly challenging. First, current models consistently make conÔ¨Ādent mistakes even when the model input has been only subtly changed. Second, even if a model is able to resist a certain attack, there is no guarantee that it will be robust to a different attack. For example, defending against an attacker who can make small modiÔ¨Ācations to all pixels in an image does not protect against an adversary who can make small rotations to an image [Engstrom et al., 2017]. One might therefore conclude that no progress can be made towards the goal of robustness to arbitrary inputs, especially given the limited progress in the Ô¨Āeld of adversarial examples over the last several years. We disagree. In this paper, we present two tools aimed at making this research direction tractable: 1.1 Contest Overview We construct a two-class unambiguous dataset where all images are either an unambiguous image of a bird or an unambiguous image of a bicycle. We describe this dataset in Section 2. Defenders build models that take an image as input, and must return either bird, bicycle or abstain.5 For inputs upon which the model does not abstain, the model must never make a mistake. Every few weeks, we ‚Äústake‚ÄĚ a set of defenses that we believe have a chance of succeeding, and award a small attacker prize to the Ô¨Ārst team who can break the defense. To break a defense, an attacker must submit an unambiguous image of either a bird or bicycle that the model labels incorrectly (as decided 4Note that it may only be possible to achieve this on some limited set of tasks which are unambiguous. 5For an introduction to abstaining classiÔ¨Āers and the selective classiÔ¨Ācation paradigm, see Geifman and El-Yaniv [2017] 2Figure 2: Examples of candidate images for the bird-or-bicycle task. The left two images are valid images for the bird-or-bicycle dataset. The right two images are invalid and not included in the dataset. by an ensemble of human judges). Models are allowed to abstain (by returning low-conÔ¨Ādence predictions) on any adversarial input6, but even one conÔ¨Ādent error will result in a broken defense. The Ô¨Ārst defense to remain un-broken for a 90-day period will win a large defender prize. We will run a one-sided warm-up to the contest to test our infrastructure and to ensure that there are defenses that can soundly defeat our Ô¨Āxed attacks. This warm-up is discussed in Section 3. 2 The Unambiguous Bird or Bicycle Task We propose a two-way classiÔ¨Ācation task for the purposes of this challenge: ‚ÄúIs this an unambiguous picture of a bird, a bicycle, or is it (ambiguous / not obvious)?‚ÄĚ. We call this the "bird-or-bicycle" task. We selected this task because (a) given an arbitrary image, humans can robustly determine the correct label, (b) modern machine learning techniques can also achieve perfect accuracy on an IID test set, but (c) the task is not so simplistic that it is possible to solve without machine learning (see Appendix F for more detail). We collect our images from the OpenImages [Krasin et al., 2017] dataset; however, we intend the bird-or-bicycle task to be well-deÔ¨Āned on arbitrary images. To obtain the ground truth label for an image, we ask multiple human taskers to label the image as either a bird, a bicycle, or as being ambiguous / not obvious. Any images where taskers did not unanimously agree the image is obviously either a bird or bicycle are excluded from the dataset. We provide some examples in Fig. 2 and full instructions provided to the taskers are shown in Appendix G.1. 3 Phase One of the Challenge: The Warm-up Before running the full unrestricted adversarial examples contest, we would like to ensure that it is possible to solve this simple, unambiguous task for a set of state-of-the-art Ô¨Āxed attacks. We have selected three attacks for this warm-up, including two black-box pixel-wise optimization attacks [Uesato et al., 2018, Brendel et al., 2017] and a spatial attack [Engstrom et al., 2017]. Because these are Ô¨Āxed and restricted attacks, we expect that there will be many ‚Äúuninteresting defenses‚ÄĚ that are tailored to perform well against these speciÔ¨Āc Ô¨Āxed attacks, but will not generalize to other attacks.7 Further, we expect that many defenses will unintentionally over-Ô¨Āt against these speciÔ¨Āc attacks and not generalize to other attacks. This further serves to motivate the full unrestricted two-player challenge. After the research community can conclusively beat these Ô¨Āxed attacks, we will begin the two-sided contest phase. Interested parties can learn more about the warm-up on github. 6 Models are prevented from always abstaining through the abstaining mechanism described in Section 4.2 7We give an example of one such uninteresting defense in Appendix E. 34 Phase Two of the Challenge: The Contest 4.1 Differences from prior adversarial examples contests We propose a two-sided adversarial examples contest. Unlike prior contests (e.g., the 2017 NIPS Adversarial Examples Contest [Kurakin et al., 2018]), our contest (a) is ongoing with no Ô¨Āxed end-date, (b) is fully white-box, and (c) allows completely unrestricted attacks. Our contest is ongoing, rather than Ô¨Āxed length. We release new defenses that have been pro- posed every few weeks, and release attacks at the end of the week that they are submitted. The rapid release cycle allows many rounds of back-and-forth between attackers and defenders, which more closely resembles the dynamics of systems deployed in the real world, and also allows contestants to individually verify that the submitted adversarial images are in fact unambiguous. When assigning the Ô¨Ānal defense prize, the organizers will re-evaluate all the attack attempts to verify that they have all failed. Only when a defense has remained undefeated for several months will we award the Ô¨Ānal defense prize. Our contest is fully white-box, and does not rely on oblivious adversaries. Because the goal of this contest is to Ô¨Ānd models that make no conÔ¨Ādent mistakes (and not just for which it is hard for an attacker to Ô¨Ānd mistakes) we require that all defenses are open-source and available to the attackers. Thus, attackers have the option to craft adversarial examples that target speciÔ¨Āc models. This provides a realistic threat model for evaluating defenses, as recommended by Athalye et al. [2018]. Our contest is unrestricted, and not constrained by any norm-ball. As a consequence of not restricting attacks to a neighborhood around a pre-labeled test point, each image must be evaluated by human judges to obtain a true class. An attack is successful if all the judges agree that it is unambiguously class A and the model mistakenly classiÔ¨Āes it as class B with high conÔ¨Ādence (instead of class Aor abstaining). 4.2 Abstaining mechanism for the contest In the contest, defenders attempt to create a model that never makes a conÔ¨Ādent mistake. Although the task is binary (A vs. B), models are allowed to output three labels: ‚ÄúClass A‚ÄĚ, ‚ÄúClass B‚ÄĚ, or ‚ÄúAbstain‚ÄĚ. We deÔ¨Āne a conÔ¨Ādent mistake as the model assigning one class label, when a unanimous ensemble of human taskers give the other class label. Abstaining on all adversarial inputs is acceptable, and we allow defenses to choose when to abstain using any mechanism they desire. To prevent models from abstaining on all inputs, models must reach 80% accuracy on a private eligibility dataset of clean bird-or-bicycle images. We reserve the right to perform additional tests to ensure defenders do not over-Ô¨Āt against our private held-out data [Markoff, 2015]. 4.3 The contest mechanics are open-sourced We have open-sourced our proposed contest mechanics and are soliciting feedback on them before launching the contest. Readers can Ô¨Ānd the most up-to-date contest proposal on GitHub, and we also include a snapshot of the contest proposal in Appendices A, B & C. 5 Conclusion We have presented the unrestricted adversarial examples challenge, consisting of a high-dimensional image classiÔ¨Ācation task, and an ongoing contest to evaluate defenses. We hope that this challenge will encourage more researchers to investigate the difÔ¨Ācult and conspicuously open problem of building robust classiÔ¨Āers. We invite interested parties to test their defenses in the warm-up, and to give feedback on the detailed contest mechanics in our open-source repository. 4Acknowledgments We thank the Hyderabad team for helping to create the bird-or-bicycle dataset. We thank Alexey Kurakin for help in assembling early versions of the dataset. We thank Justin Gilmer, Vincent Vanhouke, Jonathan Uesato, Robert Obryk, Balaji Lakshminarayanan and Ananya Kumar for useful conversations and feedback on early drafts of this paper. 5References Anish Athalye, Nicholas Carlini, and David A. Wagner. Obfuscated gradients give a false sense of security: Circumventing defenses to adversarial examples. CoRR, abs/1802.00420, 2018. URL http://arxiv.org/abs/1802.00420. W. Brendel, J. Ivan Krasin, Tom Duerig, Neil Alldrin, Vittorio Ferrari, Sami Abu-El-Haija, Alina Kuznetsova, Hassan Rom, Jasper Uijlings, Stefan Popov, Shahab Kamali, Matteo Malloci, Jordi Pont- Tuset, Andreas Veit, Serge Belongie, Victor Gomes, Abhinav Gupta, Chen Sun, Gal Chechik, David Cai, Zheyun Feng, Dhyanesh Narayanan, and Kevin Murphy.
