reference_research_study_list.title: On the effectiveness of interval bound propagation for training verifiably robust models
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
Recent work has shown that it is possible to train deep neural networks that
are provably robust to norm-bounded adversarial perturbations. Most of these
methods are based on minimizing an upper bound on the worst-case loss over all
possible adversarial perturbations. While these techniques show promise, they
often result in difficult optimization procedures that remain hard to scale to
larger networks. Through a comprehensive analysis, we show how a simple
bounding technique, interval bound propagation (IBP), can be exploited to train
large provably robust neural networks that beat the state-of-the-art in
verified accuracy. While the upper bound computed by IBP can be quite weak for
general networks, we demonstrate that an appropriate loss and clever
hyper-parameter schedule allow the network to adapt such that the IBP bound is
tight. This results in a fast and stable learning algorithm that outperforms
more sophisticated methods and achieves state-of-the-art results on MNIST,
CIFAR-10 and SVHN. It also allows us to train the largest model to be verified
beyond vacuous bounds on a downscaled version of ImageNet.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: On the Effectiveness of Interval Bound Propagation for Training VeriÔ¨Āably Robust Models Sven Gowal* DeepMind sgowal@google.com Krishnamurthy (Dj) Dvijotham* dvij@google.com Robert Stanforth* stanforth@google.com Rudy Bunel Chongli Qin Jonathan Uesato Relja Arandjelovi ¬īc Timothy Mann Pushmeet Kohli Abstract Recent work has shown that it is possible to train deep neural networks that are provably robust to norm-bounded adversarial perturbations. Most of these methods are based on minimizing an upper bound on the worst-case loss over all possible adversarial perturbations. While these tech- niques show promise, they often result in difÔ¨Ācult optimiza- tion procedures that remain hard to scale to larger networks. Through a comprehensive analysis, we show how a simple bounding technique, interval bound propagation (IBP), can be exploited to train large provably robust neural networks that beat the state-of-the-art in veriÔ¨Āed accuracy. While the upper bound computed by IBP can be quite weak for general networks, we demonstrate that an appropriate loss and clever hyper-parameter schedule allow the network to adapt such that the IBP bound is tight. This results in a fast and stable learning algorithm that outperforms more sophisticated methods and achieves state-of-the-art results on MNIST , CIFAR -10 and SVHN . It also allows us to train the largest model to be veriÔ¨Āed beyond vacuous bounds on a downscaled version of IMAGE NET. 1. Introduction Despite the successes of deep learning [ 1], it is well- known that neural networks are not robust. In particular, it has been shown that the addition of small but carefully cho- sen deviations to the input, called adversarial perturbations, can cause the neural network to make incorrect predictions with high conÔ¨Ādence [2‚Äď6]. Starting with Szegedy et al. [6], there has been a lot of work on understanding and generating adversarial perturbations [3, 7], and on building models that are robust to such perturbations [ 4, 8‚Äď10]. Unfortunately, many of the defense strategies proposed in the literature are targeted towards a speciÔ¨Āc adversary (e.g., obfuscating gra- dients against projected gradient attacks), and as such they are easily broken by stronger adversaries [11, 12]. Robust Perturbation found through exhaustive search (via MIP solver) Perturbation found using PGD Empirically robust but not provably robust model Predictions 2 (MIP) 8 (PGD) Figure 1: Example motivating why robustness to the projected gradient descent (PGD) attack is not a true measure of robustness (even for small convolutional neural networks). Given a seemingly robust neural network, the worst-case perturbation of size ŌĶ= 0.1 found using 200 PGD iterations and 10 random restarts (shown at the top) is correctly classiÔ¨Āed as an ‚Äúeight‚ÄĚ. However, a worst case perturbation classiÔ¨Āed as a ‚Äútwo‚ÄĚ can be found through exhaustive search (shown at the bottom). optimization techniques, like the one developed by Madry et al. [9], overcome this problem by trying to Ô¨Ānd the worst- case adversarial examples at each training step and adding them to the training data. While the resulting models show strong empirical evidence that they are robust against many attacks, we cannot yet guarantee that a different adversary (for example, one that does brute-force enumeration to com- pute adversarial perturbations) cannot Ô¨Ānd inputs that cause the model to predict incorrectly. In fact, Figure 1 provides an example that motivates why projected gradient descent (PGD) ‚Äď the technique at the core of Madry et al.‚Äôs method ‚Äď does not always Ô¨Ānd the worst-case attack (a phenomenon also observed by Tjeng et al. [13]). This has driven the need for formal veriÔ¨Ācation: a prov- 1 arXiv:1810.12715v4  [cs.LG]  29 Aug 2019able guarantee that neural networks are consistent with a speciÔ¨Ācation for all possible inputs to the network. Sub- stantial progress has been made: from complete methods that use SatisÔ¨Āability Modulo Theory (SMT) [ 14‚Äď16] or Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) [13, 17, 18] to incom- plete methods that rely on solving a convex relaxation of the veriÔ¨Ācation problem [19‚Äď26]. Complete methods, which provide exact robustness bounds, are expensive and difÔ¨Ācult to scale (since they perform exhaustive enumeration in the worst case). Incomplete methods provide robustness bounds that can be loose. However, they scale to larger models than complete methods and, as such, can be used inside the train- ing loop to build models that are not only robust, but also intrinsically easier to verify [20, 23, 24, 27]. In this paper, we study interval bound propagation (IBP), which is derived from interval arithmetic [ 14, 15, 28]: an incomplete method for training veriÔ¨Āably robust classiÔ¨Āers. IBP allows to deÔ¨Āne a loss to minimize an upper bound on the maximum difference between any pair of logits when the input can be perturbed within an ‚Ąď‚ąěnorm-bounded ball. Compared to more sophisticated approaches [20, 23, 24, 27], IBP is very fast ‚Äď its computational cost is comparable to two forward passes through the network. This enables us to have a much faster training step, allowing us to scale to larger models with larger batch sizes and perform more ex- tensive hyper-parameter search. While the core approach behind IBP has been studied to some extent in previous papers [20, 23], blindly using it results in a difÔ¨Ācult opti- mization problem with unstable performance. Most notably, we develop a training curriculum and show that this approach can achieve strong results, outperforming the state-of-the-art. The contributions of this paper are as follows: ‚ÄĘWe propose several enhancements that improve the per- formance of IBP for veriÔ¨Āed training. In particular, we differentiate ourselves from Mirman et al. [20] by using a different loss function, and by eliding the last linear layer of the neural network, thereby improving our estimate of the worst-case logits. We also develop a curriculum that stabilizes training and improves gen- eralization. ‚ÄĘWe compare our trained models to those from other ap- proaches in terms of robustness to PGD attacks [3] and show that they are competitive against Madry et al. [9] and Wong et al. [25] across a wide range of ‚Ąď‚ąěpertur- bation radii (hereafter denoted by ŌĶ). We also compare IBP to Wong et al.‚Äôs method in terms of veriÔ¨Āed error rates. ‚ÄĘWe demonstrate that IBP is not only computationally cheaper, but that it also achieves the state-of-the-art veriÔ¨Āed accuracy for single-model architecture. 1 We 1The use of ensembles or cascades (as done by Wong et al. [25]) is orthogonal to the work presented here. reduce the veriÔ¨Āed error rate from 3.67% to 2.23% on MNIST (with ‚Ąď‚ąěperturbations of ŌĶ = 0.12), from 19.32% to 8.05% on MNIST (at ŌĶ = 0.3), and from 78.22% to 67.96% on CIFAR -10 (at ŌĶ= 8/255). Thus, demonstrating the extent to which the model is able to adapt itself during training so that the simple relaxation induced by IBP is not too weak. ‚ÄĘWe train the Ô¨Ārst provably robust model on IMAGE NET (downscaled to 64 √ó64 images) at ŌĶ= 1/255. Using a WideResNet-10-10, we reach 93.87% top-1 veriÔ¨Āed error rate. This constitutes the largest model to be veriÔ¨Āed beyond vacuous bounds (a random or constant classiÔ¨Āer would achieve a 99.9% veriÔ¨Āed error rate). ‚ÄĘFinally, the code for training provably robust neural networks using IBP is available at https://github. com/deepmind/interval-bound-propagation. 2. Related Work Work on training veriÔ¨Āably robust neural networks typi- cally falls in one of two primary categories. First, there are empirical approaches exempliÔ¨Āed perfectly by Xiao et al. [29]. This work takes advantage of the nature of MIP-based veriÔ¨Ācation ‚Äď the critical bottleneck being the number of integer variables the solver needs to branch over. The au- thors design a regularizer that aims to reduce the number of ambiguous ReLU activation units (units for which bound propagation is not able to determine whether they are on or off) so that veriÔ¨Ācation after training using a MIP solver is efÔ¨Ācient. This method, while not providing any meaningful measure of the underlying veriÔ¨Āed accuracy during training, is able to reach state-of-the-art performance once veriÔ¨Āed after training with a MIP solver. Second, there are methods that compute a differentiable upper bound on the violation of the speciÔ¨Ācation to verify. This upper bound, if fast to compute, can be used within a loss (e.g., hinge loss) to optimize models through regular Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). In this category, we highlight the works by Raghunathan et al. [27], Wong et al. [25], Dvijotham et al. [23] and Mirman et al. [20]. Raghu- nathan et al. [27] use a semi-deÔ¨Ānite relaxation that provides an adaptive regularizer that encourages robustness. Wong et al. [25] extend their previous work [24], which considers the dual formulation of the underlying LP. Critically, any feasible dual solution provides a guaranteed upper bound on the solution of the primal problem. This allows Wong and Kolter to Ô¨Āx the dual solution and focus on computing tight activation bounds that, in turn, yield a tight upper bound on the speciÔ¨Ācation violation. Alternatively, Dvijotham et al. [23] Ô¨Āx the activation bounds and optimize the dual solution using an additional veriÔ¨Āer network. Finally, Mirman et al. [20] introduce geometric abstractions that bound activations 2ŌĶis measured with respect to images normalized between 0 and 1.as they propagate through the network. To the contrary of the conclusions from these previous works, we demonstrate that tighter relaxations (such as the dual formulation from Dvijotham et al. [23], or the zonotope domain from Mirman et al. [20]) are not necessary to reach tight veriÔ¨Āed bounds. IBP, which often leads to loose upper bounds for arbi- trary networks, has a signiÔ¨Ācant computational advantage, since computing IBP bounds only requires two forward passes through the network. This enables us to apply IBP to signiÔ¨Ācantly larger models and train with extensive hyper- parameter tuning. We show that thanks to this capability, a carefully tuned veriÔ¨Āed training process using IBP is able to achieve state-of-the-art veriÔ¨Āed accuracy. Perhaps sur- prisingly, our results show that neural networks can easily adapt to make the rather loose bound provided by IBP much tighter ‚Äď this is in contrast to previous results that seemed to indicate that more expensive veriÔ¨Ācation procedures are needed to improve the veriÔ¨Āed accuracy of neural networks in image classiÔ¨Ācation tasks. 3. Methodology Neural network. We focus on feed-forward neural net- works trained for classiÔ¨Ācation tasks. The input to the net- work is denoted x0 and its output is a vector of raw un- normalized predictions (hereafter logits) corresponding to its beliefs about which class x0 belongs to. During training, the network is fed pairs of input x0 and correct output label ytrue, and trained to minimize a misclassiÔ¨Ācation loss, such as cross-entropy. For clarity of presentation, we assume that the neural network is deÔ¨Āned by a sequence of transformations hk for each of its K layers. That is, for an input z0 (which we deÔ¨Āne formally in the next paragraph), we have zk = hk(zk‚ąí1) k= 1,...,K (1) The output zK ‚ąą RN has N logits corresponding to N classes. VeriÔ¨Ācation problem. We are interested in verifying that neural networks satisfy a speciÔ¨Ācation by generating a proof that this speciÔ¨Ācation holds. We consider speciÔ¨Ācations that require that for all inputs in some set X(x0) around x0, the network output satisÔ¨Āes a linear relationship cTzK + d‚Č§0 ‚ąÄz0 ‚ąąX(x0) (2) where cand dare a vector and a scalar that may depend on the nominal input x0 and label ytrue. As shown by Dvijotham et al. [22], many useful veriÔ¨Ācation problems Ô¨Āt this deÔ¨Āni- tion. In this paper, we focus on the robustness to adversarial perturbations within some ‚Ąď‚ąěnorm-bounded ball around the nominal input x0. Adversarial polytope Interval bounds SpeciÔ¨Ācation Upper bound Figure 2: Illustration of interval bound propagation. From the left, the adversarial polytope (illustrated in 2D for clarity) of the nominal image of a ‚Äúnine‚ÄĚ (in red) is propagated through a convolutional network. At each layer, the polytope deforms itself until the last layer where it takes a complicated and non-convex shape in logit space. Interval bounds (in gray) can be propagated similarly: after each layer the bounds are reshaped to be axis-aligned bounding boxes that always encompass the adversarial polytope. In logit space, it becomes easy to compute an upper bound on the worst- case violation of the speciÔ¨Ācation to verify. A network is adversarially robust at a point x0 if there is no choice of adversarial perturbation that changes the classiÔ¨Ācation outcome away from the true label ytrue, i.e., argmaxizK,i = ytrue for all elements z0 ‚ąą X(x0). For- mally, we want to verify that for each class y: (ey‚ąíeytrue )TzK ‚Č§0 ‚ąÄz0 ‚ąąX(x0) ={x|‚ą•x‚ąíx0‚ą•‚ąě<ŌĶ} (3) where ei is the standard ith basis vector and ŌĶis the perturba- tion radius. Verifying a speciÔ¨Ācation like (2) can be done by search- ing for a counter-example that violates the speciÔ¨Ācation con- straint: max z0‚ąąX(x0) cTzK + d subject to zk = hk(zk‚ąí1) k= 1,...,K (4) If the optimal value of the above optimization problem is smaller than 0, the speciÔ¨Ācation (2) is satisÔ¨Āed. Interval bound propagation. IBP‚Äôs goal is to Ô¨Ānd an up- per bound on the optimal value of the problem (4). The sim- plest approach is to bound the activation zk of each layer by an axis-aligned bounding box (i.e., zk(ŌĶ) ‚Č§zk ‚Č§zk(ŌĶ) 3) using interval arithmetic. For ‚Ąď‚ąěadversarial perturbations of size ŌĶ, we have for each coordinate zk,i of zk: zk,i(ŌĶ) = min zk‚ąí1(ŌĶ)‚Č§zk‚ąí1‚Č§zk‚ąí1(ŌĶ) eT ihk(zk‚ąí1) zk,i(ŌĶ) = max zk‚ąí1(ŌĶ)‚Č§zk‚ąí1‚Č§zk‚ąí1(ŌĶ) eT ihk(zk‚ąí1) (5) 3For simplicity, we abuse the notation ‚Č§ to mean that all coordinates from the left-hand side need to be smaller than the corresponding coordi- nates from the right-hand side.where z0(ŌĶ) = x0 ‚ąíŌĶ1 and z0(ŌĶ) = x0 + ŌĶ1. The above optimization problems can be solved quickly and in closed form for afÔ¨Āne layers and monotonic activation functions. An illustration of IBP is shown in Figure 2. For the afÔ¨Āne layers (e.g., fully connected layers, con- volutions) that can be represented in the form hk(zk‚ąí1) = Wzk‚ąí1 + b, solving the optimization problems (5) can be done efÔ¨Āciently with only two matrix multiplies: ¬Ķk‚ąí1 = zk‚ąí1 + zk‚ąí1 2 rk‚ąí1 = zk‚ąí1 ‚ąízk‚ąí1 2 ¬Ķk = W¬Ķk‚ąí1 + b rk = |W|rk‚ąí1 zk = ¬Ķk ‚ąírk zk = ¬Ķk + rk (6) where |¬∑| is the element-wise absolute value operator. Prop- agating bounds through any element-wise monotonic acti- vation function (e.g., ReLU, tanh, sigmoid) is trivial. Con- cretely, if hk is an element-wise increasing function, we have: zk = hk(zk‚ąí1) zk = hk(zk‚ąí1) (7) Notice how for element-wise non-linearities the (zk,zk) formulation is better, while for afÔ¨Āne transformations (¬Ķk,rk) is more efÔ¨Ācient (requiring two matrix multiplies instead of four). Switching between parametrizations de- pending on hk incurs a slight computational overhead, but since afÔ¨Āne layers are typically more computationally inten- sive, the formulation (6) is worth it. Finally, the upper and lower bounds of the output logits zK can be used to construct an upper bound on the solution of (4): max zK(ŌĶ)‚Č§zK‚Č§zK(ŌĶ) cTzK + d (8) Overall, the adversarial speciÔ¨Ācation (3) is upper-bounded by zK,y(ŌĶ) ‚ąízK,ytrue (ŌĶ). It corresponds to an upper bound on the worst-case logit difference between the true classytrue and any other class y. Elision of the last layer. Bound propagation is not nec- essary for the last linear layer of the network. Indeed, we can Ô¨Ānd an upper bound to the solution of (4) that is tighter than proposed by (8) by eliding the Ô¨Ānal linear layer with the speciÔ¨Ācation. Loss. In the context of classiÔ¨Ācation under adversarial per- turbation, solving the optimization problem (8) for each target class y Őł= ytrue results in a set of worst-case logits where the logit of the true class is equal to its lower bound and the other logits are equal to their upper bound: ňÜzK,y(ŌĶ) = { zK,y(ŌĶ) if yŐł= ytrue zK,ytrue (ŌĶ) otherwise (10) That is for all yŐł= ytrue, we have (ey ‚ąíeytrue )T ňÜzK(ŌĶ) = max zK(ŌĶ)‚Č§zK‚Č§zK(ŌĶ) (ey ‚ąíeytrue )TzK (11) We can then formulate our training loss as L= őļ‚Ąď(zK,ytrue)Óīô ÓīėÓīó Óīö LÔ¨Āt +(1 ‚ąíőļ) ‚Ąď(ňÜzK(ŌĶ),ytrue)Óīô ÓīėÓīó Óīö Lspec (12) where ‚Ąďis the cross-entropy loss and őļis a hyperparameter that governs the relative weight of satisfying the speciÔ¨Ā- cation (Lspec) versus Ô¨Ātting the data ( LÔ¨Āt ). If ŌĶ = 0 then zK = ňÜzK(ŌĶ), and thus (12) becomes equivalent to a standard classiÔ¨Ācation loss. Training procedure. To stabilize the training process and get a good trade-off between nominal and veriÔ¨Āed accuracy under adversarial perturbation, we create a learning curricu- lum by scheduling the values of őļand ŌĶ: ‚ÄĘőļcontrols the relative weight of satisfying the speci- Ô¨Ācation versus Ô¨Ātting the data. Hence, we found that starting with őļ= 1and slowly reducing it throughout training helps get more balanced models with higher nominal accuracy. In practice, we found that using a Ô¨Ā- nal value of őļ= 1/2 works well on MNIST , CIFAR -10, SVHN and IMAGE NET. ‚ÄĘMore importantly, we found that starting with ŌĶ = 0 and slowly raising it up to a target perturbation radius ŌĶtrain is necessary. We note thatŌĶtrain does not need to be equal to the perturbation radius used during testing, us- ing higher values creates robust models that generalize better.Additional details that relate to speciÔ¨Āc datasets are available in the supplementary material in Appendix A. 4. Results We demonstrate that IBP can train veriÔ¨Āably robust net- works and compare its performance to state-of-the-art meth- ods on MNIST , CIFAR -10 and SVHN . Highlights include an improvement of the veriÔ¨Āed error rate from 3.67% to 2.23% on MNIST at ŌĶ = 0.1, from 19.32% to 8.05% on MNIST at ŌĶ= 0.3, and from 78.22% to 67.96% on CIFAR - 10 at ŌĶ= 8/255. We also show that IBP can scale to larger networks by training a model on downscaled IMAGE NET that reaches a non-vacuous veriÔ¨Āed error rate of 93.87% at ŌĶ= 1/255. Finally, Section 4.3 illustrates how training with the loss function and curriculum from Section 3 allows the training process to adapt the model to ensure that the bound computed by IBP is tight. Unless stated otherwise, we compute the empirical ad- versarial accuracy (or error rate) on the test set using 200 untargeted PGD steps and 10 random restarts. As the ver- iÔ¨Āed error rate computed for a network varies greatly with the veriÔ¨Ācation method, we calculate it using an exact solver. Several previous works have shown that training a network with a loss function derived from a speciÔ¨Āc veriÔ¨Ācation pro- cedure renders the network amenable to veriÔ¨Ācation using that speciÔ¨Āc procedure only [ 23, 24, 27]. In order to cir- cumvent this issue and present a fair comparison, we use a complete veriÔ¨Ācation algorithm based on solving a MIP ‚Äď such an algorithm is expensive as it performs a brute force enumeration in the worst case. However, in practice, we Ô¨Ānd that commercial MIP solvers like Gurobi can handle veriÔ¨Āca- tion problems from moderately sized networks. In particular, we use the MIP formulation from Tjeng et al. [13]. For each example of the test set, a MIP is solved using Gurobi with a timeout of 10 minutes. Upon timeout, we fallback to solv- ing a relaxation of the veriÔ¨Ācation problem with a LP [15] using Gurobi again. When both approaches fail to provide a solution within the imparted time, we count the example as attackable. Thus, the veriÔ¨Āed error rate reported may be over-estimating the exact veriÔ¨Āed error rate. 4 We always report results with respect to the complete test set of 10K images for both MNIST and CIFAR -10, and 26K images for SVHN . For downscaled IMAGE NET, we report results on the validation set of 10K images. 4.1. Tightness Figure 4 shows the evolution of an adversarial polytope and its outer approximation during training. In this setup (similar to Wong and Kolter [24]), we train a 2-100-100- 100-2 network composed of fully-connected layers with ReLU activations on a toy two-dimensional dataset. This dataset consists of 13 randomly drawn 2-dimensional points in [0,1]2, Ô¨Āve of which are from the positive class. The ‚Ąď‚ąě distance between each pair of points is at least 0.08, which corresponds to the ŌĶ and ŌĶtrain values used during testing and training, respectively. The adversarial polytope at the last layer (shown in gray) is computed by densely sampling inputs within an ‚Ąď‚ąě-norm bounded ball around a nominal input (corresponding to one of the positive training exam- ples). The outer bounds (in green) correspond to the interval bounds at the last layer computed using (5). We observe that, while initially the bounds are very loose, they do become tighter as training progresses. We observe that IBP itself is a good estimate of the veriÔ¨Āed error rate and pro- vides estimates that are competitive with more sophisticated solvers (when models are trained using IBP). While intuitive, it is surprising to see that the IBP bounds are so close to the MIP bounds. This highlights that veriÔ¨Ācation becomes easier when models are trained to be veriÔ¨Āable as a simple method like IBP can verify a large proportion of the MIP veriÔ¨Āed samples. This phenomenon was already observed by Dvijotham et al. [23] and Xiao et al. [29] and explains why some methods cannot be veriÔ¨Āed beyond trivial bounds within a reasonable computational budget. 5. Conclusion We have presented an approach for training veriÔ¨Āable models and provided strong baseline results for MNIST , CIFAR -10, SVHN and downscaled IMAGE NET. Our experi- ments have shown that the proposed approach outperforms competing techniques in terms of veriÔ¨Āed bounds on adver- sarial error rates in image classiÔ¨Ācation problems, while also training faster. In the future, we hope that these results can serve as a useful baseline. Training parameters For IBP, across all datasets, the networks were trained using the Adam [31] algorithm with an initial learning rate of 10‚ąí3. We linearly ramp-down the value ofőļbetween 1 and őļÔ¨Ānal after a Ô¨Āxed warm-up period (őļÔ¨Ānal is set to both 0 or 0.5 and the best result is used). Simultaneously, we lineary ramp-up the value of ŌĶbetween 0 and ŌĶtrain (for CIFAR -10 and SVHN , we use a value of ŌĶtrain that is 10% higher than the desired robustness radius). MNIST is trained on a single Nvidia V100 GPU. CIFAR -10 , SVHN and IMAGE NET are trained on 32 tensor processing units (TPU) [32] with 2 workers with 16 TPU cores each. ‚ÄĘFor MNIST , we train on a single Nvidia V100 GPU for 100 epochs with batch sizes of 100. The total number of training steps is 60K. We decay the learning rate by 10√óat steps 15K and 25K. We use warm-up and ramp-up durations of 2K and 10K steps, respectively. We do not use any data augmentation techniques and use full28 √ó28 images without any normalization. ‚ÄĘ CIFAR -10 , we train for 3200 epochs with batch sizes of 1600 (training for 350 epochs with batch sizes of 50 reaches 71.70% veriÔ¨Āed error rate when ŌĶ= 8/255). The total number of training steps is 100K. We decay the learning rate by 10√óat steps 60K and 90K. We use warm-up and ramp-up durations of 5K and 50K steps, respectively. During training, we add random translations and Ô¨āips, and normalize each image channel (using the channel statistics from the train set). ‚ÄĘFor SVHN , we train for 2200 epochs with batch sizes of 50 (training for 240 epochs with batch sizes of 50 reaches within 1% of the veriÔ¨Āed error rate). The total number of training steps is 100K. The rest of the schedule is identical to CIFAR -10. During training, we add random translations, and normalize each image channel (using the channel statistics from the train set). ‚ÄĘFor IMAGE NET, we train for 160 epochs with batch sizes of 1024. The total number of training steps is 200K. We decay the learning rate by 10 √óat steps 120K and 180K. We use warm-up and ramp-up durations of 10K and 100K steps, respectively. We use images downscaled to 64 √ó64 (resampled using pixel area relation, which gives moir¬īe-free images). During training, we use random crops of 56 √ó56 and random Ô¨āips. During testing, we use a central 56 √ó56 crop. We also normalize each image channel (using the channel statistics from the train set). The networks trained using Wong et al. [25] were trained using the schedule and learning rate proposed by the authors. For Madry et al. [9], we used a learning rate schedule identical to IBP and, for the inner optimization, adversarial examples are generated by 7 steps of PGD with Adam [31] and a learning rate of 10‚ąí1. Note that our reported results for these two methods closely match or beat published results, giving us conÔ¨Ādence that we performed a fair comparison. Figure 5 shows how the empirical PGD accuracy (on the test set) increases as training progresses for IBP and Madry et al.. This plot shows the median performance (along with the 25th and 75th percentiles across 10 independent training processes) and conÔ¨Ārms that IBP is stable and produces consistent results. Additionally, for IBP, we clearly see the effect of ramping the value of ŌĶup during training (which happens between steps 2K and 12K). 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 Training step 0% 20% 40% 60% 80% 100% Test accuracy IBP (PGD őĶ = 0.3) IBP (nominal őĶ = 0) Madry et al. (PGD őĶ = 0. 3) Madry et al. (nominal őĶ = 0) Figure 5: Median evolution of the nominal (no attacks) and empirical PGD accuracy (under perturbations of ŌĶ= 0.3) as training progresses for 10 independently trained large models on MNIST . The shaded areas indicate the 25th and 75th percentiles.B. Convolutional Ô¨Ālters Figure 6 shows the Ô¨Ārst layer convolutional Ô¨Ālters resulting from training a small robust model on MNIST against a perturbation radius of ŌĶ= 0.1. Overall, the Ô¨Ālters tend to be extremely sparse ‚Äď at least when compared to the Ô¨Ālters obtained by training a nominal non-robust model (this observation is consistent with [25]). We can qualitatively observe that Wong et al. produces the sparsest set of Ô¨Ālters. Similarly, as shown in Figure 7, robust models trained on CIFAR -10 exhibit high levels of sparsity in their convolutional Ô¨Ālters. Madry et al. seems to produce more meaningful Ô¨Ālters, but they remain sparse compared to the non-robust model. This analysis suggests that IBP strongly limits the capacity of the underlying network. As a consequence, larger models are often preferable. Larger models, however, can lead to the explosion of intermediate interval bounds ‚Äď and this is the main reason why values of ŌĶmust be carefully scheduled. Techniques that combine tighter (but slower) relaxations with IBP could be used in the initial training stages when training deeper and wider models. (a) Nominal  (b) IBP (c) Wong et al.  (d) Madry et al. Figure 6: First layer convolutional Ô¨Ālters resulting from training a small robust model on MNIST against a perturbation radius of ŌĶ= 0.1 for all methods. (a) Nominal  (b) IBP (c) Wong et al.  (d) Madry et al. Figure 7: First layer convolutional Ô¨Ālters resulting from training a small robust model on CIFAR -10 against a perturbation radius of ŌĶ= 2/255 for all methods.C. Ablation study Table 5 reports the performance of each model for different training methods onMNIST with ŌĶ= 0.4 (averaged over 10 independent training processes). We chose MNIST for ease of experimentation and a large perturbation radius to stress the limit of IBP. The table shows the effect of the elision of the last layer and the cross-entropy loss independently. We do not report results without the schedule on ŌĶas all models without the schedule are unable to train (i.e., reaching only 11.35% accuracy at best). We observe that all components contribute to obtaining a good Ô¨Ānal veriÔ¨Āed error rate. This is particularly visible for the small model, where adding elision improves the bound by 3.88% (12.9% relative improvement) when using a softplus loss. Using cross-entropy (instead of softplus) also results in a signiÔ¨Ācant improvement of 4.42% (14.7% relative improvement). Ultimately, the combination of cross-entropy and elision shows an improvement of 5.15%. Although present for larger models, these improvements are less visible. This is another example of how models with larger capacity are able to adapt to get tight bounds. Finally, it is worth noting that elision tends to provide results that are more consistent (as shown by the 25th and 75th percentiles). D. Additional results Table 6 provides complementary results to the ones in Table 4. It includes results for each individual model architecture, as well as results from the literature (for matching model architectures). The test error corresponds to the test set error rate when there is no adversarial perturbation. For models that we trained ourselves, the PGD error rate is calculated using 200 iterations of PGD and 10 random restarts. The veriÔ¨Āed bound on the error rate is obtained using the MIP/LP cascade described in Section 4. We observe that with the exception of CIFAR -10 with ŌĶ= 2/255, IBP outperforms all other models by a signiÔ¨Ācant margin (even for equivalent model sizes). For CIFAR -10 with ŌĶ = 2/255, it was important to increase the model size to obtain competitive results. However, since IBP is cheap to run (i.e., only two additional passes), we can afford to run it on much larger models. E. Runtime When training the small network on MNIST with a Titan Xp GPU (where standard training takes 1.5 seconds per epoch), IBP only takes 3.5 seconds per epoch compared to 8.5 seconds for Madry et al. and 2 minutes for Wong et al. (using random projection of 50 dimensions). Indeed, as detailed in Section 3 (under the paragraph ‚Äúinterval bound propagation‚ÄĚ), IBP creates only two additional passes through the network compared to Madry et al. for which we used seven PGD steps during training. Xiao et al.‚Äôs method takes the same amount of time as IBP as it needs to perform bound propagation too. When Projected Gradient Descent is not enough For a given example in MNIST , this section compares the worst-case attack found by PGD with the one found using a complete solver. The underlying model is a medium sized network trained using IBP with ŌĶ= 0.1. The nominal image, visible in Figure 8a, has the label ‚Äúeight‚ÄĚ, and corresponds to the 1365th image of the test set. The worst-case perturbation of size ŌĶ= 0.1 found using 200 PGD iterations and 10 random restarts is shown in Figure 8b. In this particular case, the robust network is still able to successfully classify the attack as an ‚Äúeight‚ÄĚ. Without any veriÔ¨Āable proof, we could wrongly assume that our network is robust to ‚Ąď‚ąěperturbation on that image. However, when running a complete solver (using a MIP formulation), we are able to Ô¨Ānd a counter-example that successfully induces the model to misclassify the ‚Äúeight‚ÄĚ as a ‚Äútwo‚ÄĚ (as shown in Figure 8c). Label: 8 - Prediction: 8 - Adversarial: 8 (a) Nominal image correctly classiÔ¨Āed as an ‚Äúeight‚ÄĚ Label: 8 - Prediction: 8 - Adversarial: 8 (b) Worst attack found using PGD still classiÔ¨Āed as an ‚Äúeight‚ÄĚ Label: 8 - Prediction: 8 - Secondary: 2 (c) Actual worst attack found using a MIP solver incorrectly classiÔ¨Āed as a ‚Äútwo‚ÄĚ Figure 8: Attacks of size ŌĶ= 0.1 found on the 1365th image of the MNIST test set. For (b) and (c), the left pane shows the adversarial image, while the right pane shows the perturbation rescaled for clarity. Figure 9 shows the untargeted adversarial loss (optimized by PGD) around the nominal image. In these loss landscapes, we vary the input along a linear space deÔ¨Āned by the worse perturbations found by PGD and the MIP solver. The uand vaxes represent the magnitude of the perturbation added in each of these directions respectively and the zaxis represents the loss. Typical cases where PGD is not optimal are often a combination of two factors that are qualitatively visible in this Ô¨Āgure: ‚ÄĘWe can observe that the MIP attack only exists in a corner of the projected ‚Ąď‚ąě-bounded ball around the nominal image. Indeed, since PGD is a gradient-based method, it relies on taking gradient steps of a given magnitude (that depends on the learning rate) at each iteration. That is, unless we allow the learning rate to decay to a sufÔ¨Āciently small value, the reprojection on the norm-bounded ball at each iteration will force the PGD solution to bounce between the edges of that ball without hitting its corner. ‚ÄĘThe second, more subtle, effect concerns the gradient direction. Figure 9b, which shows a top-view of the loss landscape, indicates that a large portion of ‚Ąď‚ąěball around the nominal image pushes the PGD solution towards the right (rather than the bottom). In other words, gradients cannot always be trusted to point towards the true worst-case attack.u 0.15 0.10 0.05 0.000.050.100.150.20 v 0.15 0.10 0.05 0.00 0.05 0.10 0.15 0.20 loss 2 0 2 4 6 8 10 2 8 (a) 0.10  0.05  0.00 0.05 0.10 u 0.10 0.05 0.00 0.05 0.10 v 1.8 0.0 1.8 3.6 5.4 7.2 9.0 loss (b) Figure 9: Loss landscapes around the nominal image of an ‚Äúeight‚ÄĚ. reference_research_study_list.title: Semantic adversarial examples
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
Deep neural networks are known to be vulnerable to adversarial examples,
i.e., images that are maliciously perturbed to fool the model. Generating
adversarial examples has been mostly limited to finding small perturbations
that maximize the model prediction error. Such images, however, contain
artificial perturbations that make them somewhat distinguishable from natural
images. This property is used by several defense methods to counter adversarial
examples by applying denoising filters or training the model to be robust to
small perturbations.
  In this paper, we introduce a new class of adversarial examples, namely
"Semantic Adversarial Examples," as images that are arbitrarily perturbed to
fool the model, but in such a way that the modified image semantically
represents the same object as the original image. We formulate the problem of
generating such images as a constrained optimization problem and develop an
adversarial transformation based on the shape bias property of human cognitive
system. In our method, we generate adversarial images by first converting the
RGB image into the HSV (Hue, Saturation and Value) color space and then
randomly shifting the Hue and Saturation components, while keeping the Value
component the same. Our experimental results on CIFAR10 dataset show that the
accuracy of VGG16 network on adversarial color-shifted images is 5.7%.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Semantic Adversarial Examples Hossein Hosseini Radha Poovendran Network Security Lab (NSL) Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, W A dog frog ship bird cat horse ship automobile airplane automobile airplane automobile bird cat deer dog frog horse ship truckOriginal Labels: Predicted Labels: Original  Images Adversarial  Images Figure 1: Samples of CIFAR10 original images (top) and semantic adversarial examples (bottom) on VGG16 network. Adversarial images are generated by converting original images into the HSV color space and randomly shifting the Hue and Saturation components, while keeping Value the same. All images in Ô¨Ārst row are correctly classiÔ¨Āed by the model. Abstract Deep neural networks are known to be vulnerable to ad- versarial examples, i.e., images that are maliciously per- turbed to fool the model. Generating adversarial examples has been mostly limited to Ô¨Ānding small perturbations that maximize the model prediction error. Such images, how- ever, contain artiÔ¨Ācial perturbations that make them some- what distinguishable from natural images. This property is used by several defense methods to counter adversarial ex- amples by applying denoising Ô¨Ālters or training the model to be robust to small perturbations. In this paper, we introduce a new class of adversarial ex- amples, namely ‚ÄúSemantic Adversarial Examples, ‚ÄĚ as im- ages that are arbitrarily perturbed to fool the model, but in such a way that the modiÔ¨Āed image semantically represents the same object as the original image. We formulate the problem of generating such images as a constrained opti- mization problem and develop an adversarial transforma- tion based on the shape bias property of human cognitive system. In our method, we generate adversarial images by Ô¨Ārst converting the RGB image into the HSV (Hue, Satura- tion and Value) color space and then randomly shifting the Hue and Saturation components, while keeping the Value component the same. Our experimental results on CIFAR10 dataset show that the accuracy of VGG16 network on ad- versarial color-shifted images is 5.7%. 1. Introduction Image classiÔ¨Āers are vulnerable to adversarial inputs, i.e., it is possible to carefully modify an image such that the model will classify it into a wrong label, while a human observer perceives the original object [1, 2]. The existence of adversarial examples is intriguing from learning perspec- tive, since models that outperform human in classifying nat- ural images can be so easily fooled by adding a hardly vis- ible perturbation. Also from the security perspective, such attacks pose a major threat, as machine learning systems are being increasingly integrated into critical and security- sensitive applications, such as autonomous cars, medical di- agnosis, and banking. Generating adversarial examples has been mostly limited to Ô¨Ānding small perturbations that maximize the model pre- diction error [3‚Äď6]. Such modiÔ¨Āed images, however, con- tain artiÔ¨Ācial perturbations that make them somewhat dis- tinguishable from natural images. This property is used by several defense methods to make deep learning models ro- bust against small perturbations [7, 8] or to map the per- turbed image back into the space of natural images by ap- plying preprocessing Ô¨Ālters [9, 10]. In practice, however, the adversary may not be con- strained with slightly modifying the image. That is, the ad- versary may perturb the image a lot, but in such a way that the modiÔ¨Āed image semantically represents the same object as the original image (because otherwise, we cannot expect 1 arXiv:1804.00499v1  [cs.CV]  16 Mar 2018the model to classify it correctly). To construct such images, we need to identify the types of transformations that human vision is invariant to and investigate how do start-of-the-art deep learning models compare to humans. One property of human cognitive system is the ‚Äúshape bias‚ÄĚ, i.e., when assigning a name to new items, humans weight shape more heavily than they do other dimensions of perceptual similarity, such as size or texture [11]. Convo- lutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are similarly designed to take into account the spatial structure of image data. In fact, models trained on ImageNet are shown to display shape bias as well [12]. In this paper, we make the following contributions. ‚ÄĘWe introduce a new class of adversarial examples, namely semantic adversarial examples, as images that are arbitrarily perturbed to fool the model, but seman- tically represent the original objects. We formulate the problem as a constrained optimization problem, re- quiring the modiÔ¨Āed image to be smooth and natural- looking, so as to be undetectable by current defense methods. ‚ÄĘWe propose a method for generating semantic adver- sarial images, based on the shape bias property of hu- man cognitive system. In our method, we Ô¨Ārst convert the image from RGB into the HSV color space, com- posed of Hue, Saturation and Value color channels. We then randomly shift the hue and saturation compo- nents, while keeping the value the same. This approach generates images that contain the original object with different colors and colorfulness. ‚ÄĘWe perform the experiments on CIFAR10 dataset and VGG16 network. The results show that the model accuracy on adversarial color-shifted images is 5.7%. Figure 1 shows samples of CIFAR10 original images and their corresponding modiÔ¨Āed images that fool the VGG16 model. As can be seen, modiÔ¨Āed images represent the same object as original images. We also apply the attack on the state-of-the-art robust model against adversarial examples [8] and show that the model accuracy drops to 8.6% when tested on adversarial color-shifted images. The code for gener- ating adversarial color-shifted images is available at https://github.com/HosseinHosseini/ Semantic-Adversarial-Examples. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the problem of generating semantic adversarial ex- amples. In section3, we Ô¨Ārst provide a background on shape bias property and HSV color space and then propose a method for generating adversarial color-shifted images. Section 4 presents the experimental results. Section 5 re- views the related works and Section 6 concludes the paper. 2. Problem Statement 2.1. Adversarial Examples In this paper, we consider the misclassiÔ¨Ācation attack. Current techniques for generating adversarial examples try to Ô¨Ānd a perturbation that maximizes the network prediction error. Let F be the machine learning classiÔ¨Āer and xbe the given image. The adversarial perturbation is typically found by solving the following optimization problem [2]: min ‚ą•őī‚ą• (1) s.t. F(x+ őī) Őł= F(x). The added perturbation in adversarial examples is usu- ally small and, hence, the modiÔ¨Āed image is likely to belong to the same class as the original image. The image, however, does contain an artiÔ¨Ācial perturbation that makes it some- what distinguishable from natural images. This property is used by several defense methods to counter adversarial ex- amples by explicitly applying denoising operations [9, 10] or training the model to do so implicitly [7, 8]. 2.2. Semantic Adversarial Examples In practice, the adversary may not be constrained with slightly modifying the image. That is, the image can be modiÔ¨Āed by any transformation, conditioned that the trans- formation preservers the semantics of the image. Let ‚Ą¶ be the human vision system. The problem of generating se- mantic adversarial examples is stated as follows: Ô¨Ānd x‚ąó (2) s.t. ‚Ą¶(x‚ąó) = ‚Ą¶(x) and F(x‚ąó) Őł= F(x). The problem (2) can be seen as mapping any given image into the space of natural images that are misclassiÔ¨Āed by the model, but contain the original object. In this sense, wrongly-classiÔ¨Āed clean images are adversarial examples with zero perturbation. Identifying and studying adversarial transformations is important from the learning perspective, since it helps to investigate how the model compares to human visual system and also to analyze the model generalization performance. Moreover, such adversarial transformations will be able to evade state-of-the-art defense methods that try to reverse the added perturbation. Therefore, it is important also from the security perspective to identify the attack space and develop more robust defense mechanisms. 3. Proposed Method In this section, we Ô¨Ārst review the shape bias property of human cognitive system. We then provide a background on HSV color space and Ô¨Ānally propose a method for gener- ating semantic adversarial examples by shifting the image color components.3.1. Shape Bias Property of Human Cognitive Sys- tem To construct semantic adversarial examples, we need to identify the properties of human vision system. One such property is the ‚Äúshape bias,‚ÄĚ stating that humans prefer to categorize objects according to their shape rather than color [11]. Therefore, we expect machine learning models to also correctly classify images that contain the original object with different colors. In the following, we propose a method to generate such images. 3.2. HSV Color Space HSV (Hue, Saturation and Value) is an alternative to RGB (red, green and blue) color space and is known to more closely represent the way human vision perceives color [13]. The hue channel corresponds to the color‚Äôs po- sition on the color wheel. As hue increases from 0 to 1, the color transitions from red to orange, yellow, green, cyan, blue, magenta, and Ô¨Ānally back to red. Saturation measures the colorfulness, i.e., setting saturation to 0 yields a gray- scale image and increasing it to 1 generates the most col- orful image with same colors. Value shows the brightness, which is maximum value of red, green and blue compo- nents. 3.3. Color-Shifted Images as Semantic Adversarial Examples HSV can be seen as a color space, in which color com- ponents (hue and saturation) are decoupled from the object structure (brightness). Therefore, by changing the hue and saturation components and keeping the value the same, we can generate images that contain the original object with different color and colorfulness. Let xH,xS and xV re- spectively denote the hue, saturation and value components of image x. The problem of generating semantic adversar- ial examples by changing the image color can be stated as follows: Ô¨Ānd x‚ąó (3) s.t. x‚ąó V = xV and F(x‚ąó) Őł= F(x). For solving (3), we can generate random images, set their value component to xV and then choose the ones that are misclassiÔ¨Āed by the model. Equally, we can start from the given image and randomly perturb the hue and saturation components in such a way that the modiÔ¨Āed image can fool the model. These methods, however, will generate images with visible noise. In order to generate smooth and natural-looking images, we shift the hue and saturation components of all pixels by the same amount. This approach generates an image where all pixels are equally colored if the shift in saturation is posi- tive or decolored if the shift is negative. The color of all pix- Hue Saturation Figure 2: Illustration of shifting color components (hue and saturation) in HSV color space on a sample image of CI- FAR10 dataset. The center image is the original one. Shift- ing hue and saturation components changes the color and colorfulness, respectively. As can be seen, the original ob- ject is recognizable in all images. els (the hue component) is also shifted by a Ô¨Āxed amount. We call such images as color-shifted images. We also note that signiÔ¨Ācantly decreasing or increasing the saturation component generates gray-scale or too color- ful images, respectively, and hence causes the image to be less like a natural color image. To generate better looking images, we add a requirement that the saturation compo- nent be minimally shifted. Figure 2 shows a sample image of CIFAR10 dataset and several color-shifted images. The center image is the original one. As can be seen, images differ in color and colorfulness across variations in hue and saturation components, respectively. Also, while the origi- nal object is recognizable in all images, images closer to the middle column look more like natural color images. Let őīH and őīS denote the shifts in hue and saturation components, respectively. Adversarial color-shifted images can be generated by solving the following problem: min |őīS| (4) s.t. Ô£Ī Ô£īÔ£≤ Ô£īÔ£≥ x‚ąó H = (xH + őīH) mod 1 x‚ąó S = clip(xS + őīS,0,1) x‚ąó V = xV and F(x‚ąó) Őł= F(x). Note that őīH and őīS are scalars. The color in hue compo- nent changes in a circle, i.e., hue of 1 is equal to hue of 0. Hence, we compute the modulo of hue component with1 tomap it to [0,1]. The saturation component, however, should be clipped to the interval of [0,1]. 3.4. Algorithm To solve (4), we do a random search over parametersőīH and őīS, and obtain the modiÔ¨Āed image as described in Ô¨Ārst constraint of (4). We continue generating color-shifted im- ages until the modiÔ¨Āed image is misclassiÔ¨Āed by the model or the maximum number of trials, denoted byN, is reached. The shift in hue component is chosen as őīH ‚ąľ U(0,1), where U(a,b) denotes uniform distribution in [a,b]. How- ever, to Ô¨Ānd adversarial images with smaller saturation shift, őīS is chosen as őīS ‚ąľU(‚ąíi N , i N ), where iis the iteration number. That is, we start with őīS = 0 and linearly in- crease the interval after each trial. Algorithm 1 describes the method. Algorithm 1Generating Adversarial Color-shifted Images 1: Input: ClassiÔ¨Āer F, Image x, Maximum number of tri- als N 2: Output: Adversarial color-shifted image x‚ąó or ‚ąÖ 3: xH,xS,xV ‚ÜźHue, Saturation and Value components of image x, respectively. 4: x‚ąó ‚Üźx 5: for i= 0,...,N ‚ąí1 do 6: őīH ‚Üźa number uniformly chosen in [0,1] 7: őīS ‚Üźa number uniformly chosen in [‚ąíi N , i N ] 8: x‚ąó H = (xH + őīH) mod 1 9: x‚ąó S = clip(xS + őīS,0,1) 10: if F(x‚ąó) Őł= F(x) then 11: return x‚ąó 12: end if 13: end for 14: return ‚ąÖ 4. Experimental Results Experiments are performed on image dataset CIFAR10, which consists of natural color images in 10 classes of air- plane, automobile, bird, cat, deer, dog, frog, horse, ship and truck [14]. We apply the attack on the pretrained VGG16 network [15], a robust network proposed by Madry et al. [8], and the VGG16 network trained with both original and color-shifted images. In experiments, we set maximum number of trials N = 1000. Table 1 provides the results for different models. The ac- curacy of pretrained VGG16 network drops to 5.7% when tested on adversarial color-shifted images. Figure 1 shows samples of original images and their corresponding color- shifted images that are misclassiÔ¨Āed by the model. As can be seen, the original object is recognizable in all of the ad- versarial images. Figure 3 shows attack success rate versus Table 1: Accuracy of different CNNs on test data and adversarial color-shifted images. The VGG-augmented is a VGG16 network trained with both original and color- shifted images. Network Accuracy on test images Accuracy on adversarial color-shifted images Pretrained VGG16 [15] 93.6% 5.7% Madry et al. Model [8] 87.3% 8.4% VGG-augmented 89.9% 69.1% 0 200 400 600 800 1000 Number of Trials 0 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 Attack Success Rate Figure 3: Attack success rate versus number of trials. For more than 14% of images, the model is fooled after trying only one color-shifted image, obtained by randomly shifting only the hue component. number of trials. For more than 14% of images, the model can be fooled by trying only one modiÔ¨Āed image, obtained by randomly shifting only the hue component. The results show that although the model achieves accuracy of 93.6% on test data, it is fragile to images with maliciously-shifted color components. We also applied targeted attack on VGG16 network. In our method, the attack space is limited, since we search over only two parameters. Nevertheless, we could achieve 35.4% success rate on images that are correctly classiÔ¨Āed by model, i.e., adversarial color-shifting can change the model prediction to an average of more than three classes. Figure 4 shows sample images of CIFAR10 dataset, each with three color-shifted versions classiÔ¨Āed into different labels. The model proposed by Madry el al. yields the state-of- the-art results against adversarial examples [16], by provid- ing robustness against worse-case perturbations [8]. We ap- ply our attack also on this model to ensure that our method does not add noise-like perturbation to the image. We ob- served that the accuracy of the robust CNN is 8.4% on adversarial color-shifted images, implying that even if theairplane automobile frog ship bird airplane dog frog horse bird cat frog ship automobile bird horse truck airplane frogautomobile Figure 4: Sample images of CIFAR10 dataset, along with three color-shifted versions classiÔ¨Āed into different labels by VGG16 network. The leftmost-column shows original images. model is robust to perturbations around data points, it does not provide robustness to semantic adversarial examples. We also applied the attack on a VGG16 network which, at each epoch, is trained with original and color-shifted im- ages with őīH ‚ąľU(0,1) and őīS ‚ąľU(‚ąí1,1). The accuracy on adversarial color-shifted images is69.1%, indicating that the model shows more robustness when trained with same types of images. However, as pointed out in [17], robustness achieved by data augmentation may not be an indication that the model has learned higher level semantic features in the dataset. That is, the network is likely to be vulnerable to other types of semantic adversarial examples. We will explore other attack and defense methods in future works. 5. Related Work Over the past several years, many techniques have been proposed to generate adversarial examples by Ô¨Ānding a small perturbation that can fool the model. Such techniques, including Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) [3], Deep- Fool [4], Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) [5], and Car- lini and Wagner attacks [6], usually involve an optimization problem that solves for a perturbation that maximizes the model prediction error. In this paper, we proposed an im- age transformation that may introduce large pixel-wise per- turbation, but preserves the semantics of the image. Our proposed transformation has tunable parameters that can be searched over to generate adversarial images. It has been shown that CNNs trained with clean im- ages generalize poorly to images with reversed brightness, called negative images [18]. Compared to color-shifted im- ages, negative images are less recognizable to humans [19]. Moreover, image complementing is a Ô¨Āxed transformation and cannot be adjusted to force the model to misclassify im- ages. In [17], Fourier Ô¨Āltering methods are used to slightly modify images such that they contain the same high level abstractions but exhibit different surface statistical regular- ities. They showed that, in the worst case, the CNN accu- racy reduces more than 20% when tested on such images. In comparison, our method introduces large perturbation to the image and reduces model accuracy by about 90%. In [20], the authors showed that adversarial translation and rotation can fool CNNs. The rotated images, however, may look unnatural to humans, due to the black borders and the existence of tilted objects. In [21], the authors consid- ered the problem of generating natural adversarial exam- ples using generative adversarial networks. Their proposed method generates images that look natural, but may belong to a different class than the original class. Hence, such im- ages do not Ô¨Āt into our deÔ¨Ānition of semantic adversarial ex- amples. In [22], the authors suggested that instead of small or imperceptible perturbations, the adversary may opt for more effective but noticeable perturbations, and proposed a method to create adversarial patches. Our method also generates images with large modiÔ¨Ācation, however in such a way that the perturbation is not visible, i.e., the modiÔ¨Āed image is smooth and natural-looking. 6. Conclusion In this paper, we introduced Semantic Adversarial Ex- amples as images that semantically represent the original object, but are misclassiÔ¨Āed by the model. We proposed a method for generating such images by shifting the color components of the image in HSV color space, and showed that the generated images are smooth and natural-looking. Our experimental results on CIFAR10 dataset show that the accuracy of state-of-the-art CNNs on adversarial color- shifted images is less than 10%. new_method.method:
Title: TAHES++ ‚ÄĒ Tangent-Aware Higher-Order Elliptical Smoothing with provable, spoof-resistant subspace control

Motivation
- Problem: Randomized smoothing (RS) scales to modern models but its certified L2 radius decays with ambient dimension and is vulnerable to off-manifold spoofing (Shadow Attack). First-order certification (HO-Cert) breaks the information-limited barrier but assumes isotropic geometry and thus remains blind to the data manifold (semantics vary mostly along tangents; nuisance lies largely along normals).
- Key gap: Existing certifiers either (i) ignore data geometry (RS4A, HO-Cert), (ii) rely on heavy second-order machinery that struggles to scale (CRC/curvature), or (iii) improve radii by transitivity but still reason in pixel L2 (GICR). None proactively exposes and constrains the normal (off-manifold) sensitivity that Shadow exploits.
- Idea: Fix the smoothing distribution but make it manifold-aware and anisotropic; certify in the corresponding Mahalanobis geometry with directional (tangent/normal) first-order constraints; and provably clamp normal-direction sensitivity using scalable Lipschitz upper bounds. Then, extend transitivity to the same ellipsoidal metric and train with a light regularizer that flattens the smoothed classifier off-manifold. This combination promises larger, better-calibrated, spoof-resistant certificates at CNN/ViT scale.

Methodology
High-level: Learn a single, global ellipsoidal covariance ő£ aligned to the data manifold; whiten to isotropic space; run first-order HO-Cert with projected (tangent/normal) constraints; cap normal sensitivity via conservative Lipschitz bounds; expand regions via geometry-aware transitivity; and add a tiny training regularizer to reduce normal gradients. Report full ellipsoidal certificates plus scalar L2 summaries and a spoofability index.

A) Geometry and fixed anisotropic smoothing (keeps RS validity)
1) Tangent estimation and aggregation (data/model-agnostic)
- Use a small frozen generative backbone (contractive AE, diffusion decoder, or VQ-VAE). For each training image x, map to latent z and compute decoder Jacobian J=‚ąāG/‚ąāz.
- For each x, take top-k left singular vectors of J as local tangents; form projectors Px=UxU‚ä§x. Aggregate across data (or per class in an input-independent way) by averaging projectors and taking the top-k eigenvectors; call the resulting basis BT (normal basis BN completes it). This yields a single, fixed subspace split common to all inputs, preserving RS validity.

2) Ellipsoidal smoothing and whitening
- Define ő£=ŌÉT^2 BTBT‚ä§ + ŌÉN^2 BNBN‚ä§ with anisotropy ő≤=ŌÉN/ŌÉT‚ąą[0.3,0.7]. Keep ő£ global and fixed.
- Compute L with LL‚ä§=ő£ and whiten inputs y=L‚ąí1x. In y-space, noise is N(0,I) and HO-Cert applies unchanged; the certified set maps back to an ellipsoid {őī: ||L‚ąí1őī||2 ‚Č§ ry} in x-space.

B) First-order certification with directional control
3) Projected-gradient HO-Cert in whitened space
- Extend HO-Cert‚Äôs low-variance estimators to obtain high-confidence intervals for gy(x) and projected gradient norms ||PT‚ąágy||2 and ||PN‚ąágy||2 using shared antithetic noise and control variates. Subspace versions are obtained by pre-multiplying noise by the corresponding projectors.
- Certificate: compute ry (‚Ąď2 in y); report the full ellipsoidal certificate (ő£, ry) and a conservative L2 summary rL2=ry¬∑‚ąöőĽmin(ő£) for comparability.
- Directional tightening: plug the two projected-norm CIs into HO-Cert‚Äôs subspace formulas to reduce worst-case sensitivity in each subspace; this enlarges the certified set along tangent directions while not over-claiming along normals.

4) Lipschitz-backed normal clamping (spoof resistance)
- Global bound: compute a scalable network Lipschitz upper bound Lglob via ECLipsE-Fast (fallback: product of per-layer spectral norms). This is input-independent and valid network-wide.
- Local projected bound (optional): approximate the local Jacobian spectral norm restricted to PN via a conservative overbound: multiply per-layer spectral norms but only for paths that pass through PN at the input (subspace-aware spectral product); this yields Lloc,N ‚Č• sup||PN‚ąágy||2. Avoids heavy SDPs, remains an upper bound.
- Clamp: use LŐāN(x)=min{CI upper bound of ||PN‚ąágy||2, Lglob (or Lloc,N)} in the subspace HO-Cert program. This strictly tightens the normal-direction constraint and prevents Shadow-style off-manifold inflation without changing the smoothing distribution.

C) Geometry-aware transitivity and training
5) Transitivity in the ellipsoidal metric
- Operate in y-space (Euclidean). Adapt GICR‚Äôs single/double-bubble to search for auxiliary points along learned tangent axes (principal components in BT), then union the corresponding ellipsoidal certificates (measured in ||¬∑||2 in y or ||L‚ąí1¬∑||2 in x). This increases coverage along semantic directions with fewer, more meaningful auxiliary points.

6) Light-touch training regularizer (TaReS)
- During standard RS training (Cohen/MACER/CRT), add őĽR¬∑EőĶ[||PN‚ąáygőł(x)||2^2] with stop-gradient PN and a handful of noise samples. This biases the smoothed classifier to be flat off-manifold while preserving tangent sensitivity. No change to the fixed ő£.

D) Provability, implementation, and reporting
7) Validity and theory
- Fixed ő£ keeps classical RS validity. Whitening reduces certification to HO-Cert‚Äôs isotropic case. Subspace constraints follow HO-Cert‚Äôs subspace results.
- Normal clamping uses valid Lipschitz upper bounds, so it is conservative.
- Under an m-dimensional manifold with bounded curvature, anisotropic smoothing plus subspace first-order certification yields direction-wise robustness driven by m (via ||PT‚ąág||) rather than ambient d; this evades RS4A‚Äôs information-limited curse by using first-order info and a non-isotropic metric.

8) Engineering plan (drop-in to HO-Cert/RS4A/GICR codebases)
- geometry/tangent.py: Jacobian SVD per sample; projector aggregation; save BT, BN.
- geometry/whiten.py: build L; y=L‚ąí1x transforms and N(0,ő£) samplers.
- hocert/subspace_grad.py: projected gradient CI estimators with shared noise.
- lipschitz/lip_proj.py: ECLipsE-Fast wrapper; subspace-aware spectral products; compute LŐāN.
- transitivity/latent_search.py: tangent-axis auxiliary search and ellipsoidal unions in y-space.
- train/tares.py: PN-gradient penalty for Cohen/MACER/CRT.

9) Outputs per input
- Full ellipsoidal certificate (ő£, ry), Mahalanobis radius ry, scalar L2 summary rL2, and a spoofability index S=||PN‚ąágy||2 / ||PT‚ąágy||2 with CIs for auditing.

What‚Äôs new vs prior work
- RS4A: We keep its general-noise/whitening machinery but fix a learned, input-independent ő£ aligned to the manifold and move beyond information-limited certification via first-order, directional constraints in the Mahalanobis metric.
- HO-Cert: We port first-order certification to anisotropic metrics and add provable normal clamping through Lipschitz upper bounds, explicitly separating tangent vs normal sensitivity.
- GICR: We conduct auxiliary-point search along learned tangent axes and union ellipsoids in the learned metric; this is stronger and more sample-efficient than pixel-L2 transitivity.
- Curvature/Lipschitz certs (CRC, LiPopt): We avoid heavy SDPs and only use fast, conservative Lipschitz bounds to cap normal sensitivity; our main certificate remains first-order and scalable.
- Shadow Attack: We expose off-manifold sensitivity (S-index), reduce it during training (TaReS), and cap it during certification (clamping), making spoofing substantially harder.

Expected impact
- Larger, more faithful certificates at scale: Ellipsoidal certificates aligned to semantics enlarge safe regions along tangents while remaining conservative off-manifold; scalar L2 summaries allow apples-to-apples comparisons.
- Spoof resistance and transparency: Directional clamping plus S-index and TaReS directly target Shadow-style spoofing and improve calibration of certificates.
- Practicality and generality: All components scale to CNNs/ViTs; ő£ is fixed, whitening and first-order estimators are standard, and Lipschitz bounds use ECLipsE-Fast or spectral products. Works with existing RS training (Cohen/MACER/CRT) and integrates into HO-Cert/GICR pipelines.
- Broader applicability: The framework naturally extends to multi-subspace or perceptual metrics (swap BT,BN from a perceptual Jacobian), to per-dataset mixtures (via RS4A‚Äôs mixture-of-noise generalization), and to reporting direction-wise robustness for auditing and model selection.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
EXPERIMENT 1 ‚ÄĒ Core certification gains and calibration with anisotropic, tangent-aware first-order smoothing
Objective
- Quantify how TAHES++ enlarges certified regions and improves calibration versus RS/HO-Cert baselines.
- Validate reporting in the ellipsoidal (Mahalanobis) metric and L2 summary, plus show directional sensitivity control via S-index.

Datasets and models
- CIFAR-10 (50k train, 10k test), ImageNet-100 (100 classes subset; 120k train, 5k val/test).
- Models: ResNet-18, ViT-S/16. Train with standard RS (Cohen) and MACER variants.

Geometry backbone for tangents
- For CIFAR-10: train a small contractive autoencoder (CAE) with 64-dim latent (2M iters, MSE loss + 1e-3 Jacobian penalty), or use a public VQ-VAE.
- For ImageNet-100: use a pre-trained VQ-VAE or a light diffusion decoder (frozen).

Global tangent/normal split (BT, BN)
- For each of N=30k training samples (CIFAR) or N=50k (ImageNet-100):
  - Map x -> z, compute decoder Jacobian J = ‚ąāG/‚ąāz via batched JVPs (PyTorch autograd). If latent dim m is small (<=128), you can explicitly form J by backprop against m standard basis vectors; else estimate top-k left singular vectors via power iteration with JVPs.
  - Extract top-k left singular vectors Ux (k=64 for CIFAR-10, k=128 for ImageNet-100); form projector Px = Ux Ux^T.
- Aggregate: PŐĄ = (1/N) ő£ Px (or class-wise then average across classes). Compute eigendecomposition of PŐĄ and take BT as its top-k eigenvectors. BN is the orthonormal complement in R^d.
- Sanity checks: proportion of energy along BT for random images should exceed 0.6 (using ||BT BT^T (x - recon(x))|| vs total).

Anisotropic smoothing and whitening
- Choose ŌÉT = 0.25 (CIFAR), ŌÉT = 0.5 (ImageNet-100). Anisotropy ő≤ = ŌÉN/ŌÉT ‚ąą {0.4, 0.6}. Set ŌÉN = ő≤ ŌÉT.
- ő£ = ŌÉT^2 BT BT^T + ŌÉN^2 BN BN^T. Compute L such that L L^T = ő£ via eigendecomposition; precompute L^{-1}.
- Whitening: y = L^{-1} x. Noise in y-space is N(0, I).

First-order certification with directional control (HO-Cert in y-space)
- For each test point x:
  - Draw M=8192 Gaussian samples őĶ ~ N(0, I) in y-space with antithetic pairs; evaluate g(y+őĶ) via the smoothed classifier estimator; use control variates for variance reduction.
  - Estimate mean score gap gy between top-2 classes and its CI.
  - Estimate projected gradient norms: ||PT ‚ąág||_2 and ||PN ‚ąág||_2 CIs using the same shared noise but with projected perturbations (PT őĶ, PN őĶ).
  - Compute ry (isotropic ‚Ąď2 radius in y) using HO-Cert‚Äôs first-order formulas with subspace-sensitive tightening (plug in the two CIs for projected norms).
  - Report ellipsoidal certificate (ő£, ry) and scalar r_L2 = ry * sqrt(őĽ_min(ő£)).
- Lipschitz-backed normal clamping:
  - Compute network spectral norm upper bounds per layer via power iteration (20 iters) and multiply to get Lglob.
  - Optional: subspace-aware spectral product for PN paths at input (fast conservative overbound) to get Lloc,N.
  - Clamp the PN gradient CI upper bound by min(CI, Lglob) or min(CI, Lloc,N) before plugging into HO-Cert.

Baselines and ablations
- RS (Cohen) with isotropic ŌÉ matching ŌÉT.
- RS4A (general-noise RS) using the same ő£ but zero first-order constraints.
- HO-Cert (isotropic) with ŌÉ=ŌÉT.
- HO-Cert + GICR (standard transitivity in pixel L2; 4 auxiliary points).
- TAHES++ variants: without clamping; with clamping; with/without TaReS regularizer (see Exp 3 for training).
- Anisotropy sweep: ő≤ ‚ąą {0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.7}.
- Tangent dimensionality sweep: k ‚ąą {32, 64, 96} for CIFAR; {64, 128, 192} for ImageNet-100.

Metrics
- Certified accuracy at fixed L2 radii r ‚ąą {0.25, 0.5, 0.75} (CIFAR), {0.5, 1.0, 1.5} (ImageNet-100) using r_L2 summary.
- Certified Mahalanobis accuracy at ry ‚ąą {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}.
- Average and quantiles (p50/p90) of r_L2 and ry.
- S-index = ||PN ‚ąág|| / ||PT ‚ąág|| with CIs; correlation of S-index with r_L2 and with empirical attack success (from Exp 2).
- Calibration: fraction of certified points that remain robust under adversarial search constrained in Mahalanobis metric.

Expected outcomes
- TAHES++ (full) increases r_L2 by 15‚Äď40% over HO-Cert and RS4A at matched compute, with the largest gains on ViT-S/16 and along semantic classes with strong manifolds.
- Clamping reduces S-index tails (>95th percentile) and tightens calibration gap without hurting ry along BT.
- Moderate ő≤ (0.4‚Äď0.6) and k near intrinsic manifold dimension maximize area under the certified-accuracy curve.

Implementation sketch (PyTorch-like pseudo-code)
- Build BT:
  P_bar = 0
  for x in dataloader:
      z = enc(x).detach();
      # get top-k left singular vectors of J = dG/dz
      Ux = topk_left_singulars_of_decoder_jacobian(x, k)  # power-iter via JVPs
      P_bar += Ux @ Ux.T
  P_bar /= N
  eigvals, eigvecs = topk_eig(P_bar, k)
  BT = eigvecs; BN = orthonormal_complement(BT)

- Whitening and certificates per batch:
  L = eigvecs_full @ diag(sqrt(eigvals_full)) @ eigvecs_full.T  # from ő£
  Linv = inv(L)
  y = (Linv @ x_flat.T).T.reshape_as(x)
  # Monte Carlo estimators produce gy, CI_T, CI_N
  r_y = hocert_with_subspace_CIs(y, CI_T_clamped, CI_N_clamped)
  r_L2 = r_y * sqrt(lambda_min(ő£))

Compute budget
- CIFAR-10/ResNet-18: ~1x V100 GPU for 8‚Äď12 hours for geometry + certification on 10k test.
- ImageNet-100/ViT-S: 4x A100 for 24‚Äď36 hours (geometry with JVPs is the bottleneck). Certification is parallelizable.

Risk and mitigations
- If geometry is noisy, shrink k and use class-averaged PŐĄ. Verify stability of BT across random subsets.
- If Lipschitz bounds are too loose, rely on Lglob (spectral product) and show robust gains from TaReS in Exp 3.


EXPERIMENT 2 ‚ÄĒ Spoof resistance under Shadow-style off-manifold attacks
Objective
- Demonstrate that TAHES++ resists off-manifold spoofing by clamping normal sensitivity and lowering S-index.
- Show improved alignment between certificates and empirical robustness under strong, PN-focused attacks.

Attack design (Shadow-inspired)
- We approximate the gradient of the smoothed class score g(y) wrt input using MC-EST with antithetic noise in y-space.
- Constrain updates primarily in PN by projecting each step: őī <- PN őī.
- Two attacks per sample:
  1) PN-constrained PGD (untargeted): maximize g_other(y+őī) - g_true(y+őī) with őī in ball ||őī||_2 ‚Č§ őĶ_y and őī in span(PN) (enforced by projection). Steps: T=50, step=őĶ_y/5, restarts=5, M=512 noise samples per gradient estimate.
  2) Mixed-direction attack: 80% PN, 20% PT (to test robustness off- but near-manifold).
- Map őĶ_y to x-space L2 using őĶ_x ‚Čą őĶ_y * sqrt(őĽ_min(ő£)). Also test fixed őĶ_x budgets and convert to őĶ_y via ő£.

Compared methods
- RS (Cohen), HO-Cert (isotropic), RS4A.
- TAHES++ (no clamp), TAHES++ (clamp), TAHES++ (clamp + TaReS).

Protocol
- Use 1k randomly selected correctly classified test images per dataset/model.
- For each őĶ_y ‚ąą {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}:
  - Run both attacks; record success if misclassification occurs.
  - Check whether the point was certified at radius ‚Č• őĶ_y (or its L2 summary). Count certified-yet-broken cases (calibration error under attack).
- Record S-index CIs before attack and relate to attack success per image.

Metrics
- Attack success rate (ASR) vs őĶ_y and őĶ_x.
- Certified-robust-yet-broken rate (lower is better).
- AUC of ASR vs S-index (monotonicity): higher S-index should predict vulnerability; TAHES++ should shift distribution to lower S.
- Average PN energy of adversarial perturbations: ||PN őī||_2 / ||őī||_2 (should be high for Shadow; TAHES++ should still hold).

Expected outcomes
- HO-Cert/RS/RS4A show high ASR under PN-PGD even at modest őĶ_y; TAHES++ with clamping reduces ASR by 20‚Äď40% absolute.
- TAHES++ with TaReS shows the lowest certified-robust-yet-broken rate, indicating improved certificate calibration.
- S-index strongly ranks vulnerability; post-TaReS, the tail of S-index shrinks and ASR drops.

Implementation sketch (attack)
- def smoothed_grad(y, label, M=512):
    eps = torch.randn(M, *y.shape, device=y.device)
    logits_plus = f(y + eps)  # base model logits
    logits_minus = f(y - eps)
    # antithetic trick and control variates can be added
    g_est = (logits_plus + logits_minus) / 2
    grad_est = autograd.grad(g_est[:, label].mean(), y, create_graph=False)[0]
    return grad_est

- for restart in range(R):
    őī = torch.zeros_like(y).normal_(0, 1e-5)
    for t in range(T):
        grad = smoothed_grad(y + őī, y_label)
        grad = PN @ grad  # project to normal space
        őī = őī + őĪ * sign(grad)  # or normalized grad
        őī = project_to_l2_ball(őī, őĶ_y)  # in y-space

- Convert őī_y to x via őī_x = L őī_y and attack x directly if needed.

Risk and mitigations
- Gradient estimation variance: increase M to 1024 for ViT or apply variance reduction (same noise across steps; momentum).
- Time: Attacks can be parallelized over GPUs; reduce dataset size if needed for exploratory runs.


EXPERIMENT 3 ‚ÄĒ Geometry-aware transitivity and effect of TaReS training
Objective
- Show that auxiliary-point search along learned tangent axes in y-space improves certificates more per budget than pixel-L2 transitivity (GICR), and that TaReS further boosts gains by flattening PN gradients.

Training with TaReS (light-touch)
- Start from a standard RS or MACER training loop with fixed ő£.
- Add TaReS penalty: őĽR * E_őĶ [ ||PN ‚ąá_y g_őł(x)||_2^2 ], with őĽR ‚ąą {1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3}, estimated with K=8 noise samples per batch. Stop-gradient through PN. Keep ő£ and BT fixed.
- Train for 100‚Äď150 epochs (CIFAR), 90 epochs (ImageNet-100) with cosine LR decay.

Ellipsoidal transitivity along tangents
- Work in y-space. For each test point y0 of the true class c:
  - Let A = columns of BT (top-dominant axes). For i in {1..m}, m ‚ąą {2, 4, 8}:
    - Axis a_i = A[:, i]. Perform line search t ‚ąą R to find the furthest point y_i = y0 + t a_i that preserves class c with high MC confidence (e.g., bootstrap CI margin > ŌĄ). Binary search with 12 evals and MC M=2048 per eval.
    - Run HO-Cert at y_i to get ry_i.
  - Union rule: certified if a point x lies in union over i of ellipsoids E_i = {őī: ||őī_y||_2 ‚Č§ ry_i} translated to y_i, plus the base ellipsoid at y0. Implementation: same as GICR‚Äôs union-of-balls check but in y-space; report r_L2 summary via max over ellipsoids of ry_i * sqrt(őĽ_min(ő£)).

Comparators and ablations
- GICR in pixel-L2 with m auxiliary points found by their heuristic; same MC budget.
- TAHES++ without TaReS.
- TAHES++ with TaReS (őĽR sweep).
- Axis selection ablations: use random BT axes vs top-eigen BT axes; also test class-conditional BT (if built) vs global.

Metrics
- Certified accuracy vs number of auxiliary points m at fixed compute budget (MC calls).
- Average incremental gain in ry and r_L2 per auxiliary point (marginal utility curves).
- Runtime per certified sample vs achieved r_L2 (efficiency frontier).
- PN gradient norm distribution before/after TaReS; S-index shift.

Expected outcomes
- Tangent-axis transitivity achieves equal or better certified accuracy than pixel-L2 GICR with 2‚Äď4x fewer auxiliary points.
- TaReS lowers PN gradients (median drop >30%), which translates into larger ry along BT and better union coverage, particularly for ViT-S.
- Calibration stays strong: certified-robust-yet-broken rate does not increase with transitivity.

Implementation sketch (transitivity)
- def find_axis_point(y0, a, M=2048, ŌĄ=0.05):
    t_lo, t_hi = 0.0, t_max
    for _ in range(12):
        t_mid = 0.5*(t_lo + t_hi)
        y_mid = y0 + t_mid * a
        conf_CI = mc_confidence_CI(y_mid, M)
        if conf_CI.lower > ŌĄ: t_lo = t_mid
        else: t_hi = t_mid
    return y0 + t_lo * a

- Auxiliary set = [y0] + [find_axis_point(y0, A[:,i]) for i in range(m)]
- Certificates = [hocert(y_i) for y_i in Auxiliary set]
- Union check: point is certified if distance in y-space to nearest center ‚Č§ that center‚Äôs ry.

Compute budget
- Training with TaReS adds ~10‚Äď15% walltime vs RS/MACER (K=8 small). Transitivity evaluation adds ~1.5‚Äď2x over base HO-Cert depending on m.

Risk and mitigations
- If axis search is unstable, cap t_max using a validation-derived limit or add a prior based on class radius statistics.
- If TaReS hurts clean accuracy, reduce őĽR or apply early stopping on validation clean accuracy.

General notes for all experiments
- Reproducibility: fix RNG seeds, log ő£, BT eigenvalues, ő≤, k, and MC seeds per experiment.
- Reporting: always include (ő£, ry), r_L2, S-index CIs. Publish code to recompute BT, ő£, and to verify ellipsoidal unions.
- Validity: ő£ and BT must remain fixed after being computed on the training set; no test-time adaptation to maintain RS validity.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
EXPERIMENT 1 ‚ÄĒ Core certification gains and calibration with anisotropic, tangent-aware first-order smoothing

Goal
- Quantify certification gains and calibration of TAHES++ vs RS, RS4A, HO-Cert and HO-Cert+GICR. Report in Mahalanobis (ellipsoidal) and scalar L2 summaries. Validate directional sensitivity control (S-index) and the effect of Lipschitz-backed clamping.

Common setup (applies across Exp 1‚Äď3)
- Framework: PyTorch 2.2+ with CUDA; use torch.compile where safe; functorch (torch.func) for JVP/VJP.
- Libraries: torchvision, timm (for ViT-S/16), scikit-learn (IncrementalPCA, randomized_svd fallback), SciPy (sparse.linalg eigsh), numpy, pandas, tqdm; optional auto_LiRPA or torchlip for Lipschitz upper bounds; otherwise torch.nn.utils.parametrizations.spectral_norm for fast per-layer spectral norm upper bounds.
- Reproducibility: seed torch, numpy, cuda; deterministic cudnn if feasible; log seeds, ő£ hyperparams (ŌÉT, ŌÉN, ő≤), k, BT eigenvalues, training/val/test splits, MC seeds for cert/attacks.
- Data preprocessing: standard dataset stats; CIFAR-10 32x32; ImageNet-100 224x224 center-crop and resize. Normalize inputs before applying TAHES++ transforms. Flatten x to vectors of dim d when operating in subspace math.
